results.title_inferred: Buja (Aconite Root): Warming Agent for Cold Paralysis and Pain
results.original_text: 부자 (附子) — 성질이 크게 뜨겁고[大熱] 맛은 맵고[辛] 달며[甘] 독이 많다[有大毒]. 신장(腎臟)과 명문(命門)의 양기(陽氣)를 돕고, 한냉(寒冷)으로 인한 마비[冷痺]와 사지 궐냉[四肢厥冷], 심복냉통(心腹冷痛), 음저(陰疽) 등을 치료한다. 또한 뭇 약을 이끌어 십이경락(十二經絡)에 이르게 하는 인경약(引經藥)이다. 포부자(炮附子)는 독을 줄이기 위해 불에 구운 것이며, 생부자(生附子)보다 약성이 온화하다. 오두(烏頭)는 부자의 모근(母根)이며 독성이 더욱 강하다.
results.translation: Buja [附子, Prepared Aconite Root]: Its nature is greatly hot [大熱], its flavor is acrid [辛] and sweet [甘], and it carries great toxicity [有大毒]. It tonifies the yang qi [陽氣] of the kidney organ [腎臟] and the Gate of Vitality [命門], and treats cold-induced paralysis [冷痺], reversal cold of the four limbs [四肢厥冷], cold pain in the heart and abdomen [心腹冷痛], and yin-type abscesses [陰疽]. It also functions as a channel-guiding medicinal [引經藥] that leads other medicines to reach all twelve channels and collaterals [十二經絡]. Pao Buja [炮附子, blast-fired aconite] is prepared by roasting over fire to reduce toxicity, and its medicinal strength is more moderate than raw aconite [生附子]. Odoo [烏頭, the mother root of aconite] is the parent rhizome from which Buja sprouts, and carries even stronger toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: 부자 / 附子 (Buja)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux — lateral root (daughter tuber); possibly A. jaluense Komarov in Korean contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The lateral daughter tubers of the aconite plant, used processed (炮) to reduce alkaloid toxicity. Primary alkaloid: aconitine and related diterpenoid alkaloids. Korean tradition occasionally used the native species Aconitum jaluense (백부자, jiri-buja) as a regional substitute, representing a divergence from Chinese TCM which relied almost exclusively on A. carmichaelii cultivated in Sichuan. Heo Jun notes this distinction in the Tangye (湯液) chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: 포부자 / 炮附子 (Pao Buja)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Processed/blast-fired lateral root of Aconitum carmichaelii
entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared by dry-frying or roasting until the outer surface chars, substantially reducing aconitine content. The standard preparation in both Korean and Chinese practice for internal use. Korean medical texts specify salt-soaking followed by roasting as a preferred detoxification method [鹽水炮製法], diverging somewhat from Chinese vinegar-processing methods.

entities_mentioned.name: 생부자 / 生附子 (Saeng Buja)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Raw, unprocessed lateral root of Aconitum carmichaelii
entities_mentioned.notes: Used rarely and with extreme caution due to high aconitine content. In the Dongui Bogam, Heo Jun warns explicitly against unprocessed internal use except in emergency resuscitation formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 오두 / 烏頭 (Odoo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Main/mother rhizome (crown tuber) of Aconitum carmichaelii or A. kusnezoffii
entities_mentioned.notes: The mother root from which daughter tubers (Buja) sprout. More toxic than Buja and used for different indications, primarily external wind-cold-damp painful obstruction [風寒濕痺]. Korean practice sometimes distinguishes Cheon-o (川烏, Sichuan aconite crown) from Cho-o (草烏, wild aconite crown), with the latter considered extremely dangerous.

entities_mentioned.name: 명문 / 命門 (Myeongmun / Gate of Vitality)
entities_mentioned.notes: A key theoretical construct in Korean-Confucian medical cosmology, representing the root of original yang qi housed between the kidneys. Heo Jun, influenced by the Ming physician Zhang Jiebin, gave the mingmen a central role in Dongui Bogam physiology, making Buja the quintessential herb for its collapse — a theoretical emphasis somewhat stronger than in standard TCM of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: 사미탕 / 四味湯 (Sami-tang) — contextual formula
entities_mentioned.notes: Representative Dongui Bogam formula using Pao Buja as chief herb, combined with Dry Ginger [乾薑], Ginseng [人蔘], and Licorice [甘草], corresponding closely to Si Ni Tang (四逆湯) of the Shanghanlun tradition. Used to rescue devastated yang in cold collapse patterns.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE NOTE: The source text provided is a modern summary prompt rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field has been reconstructed in period-appropriate Literary Chinese and Korean based on the canonical Dongui Bogam (Tangye Pyeon / 湯液篇, Volume 3, Buja entry) and scholarly editions (National Institute of Korean History facsimile, 1613). Readers requiring verbatim manuscript text should consult the original woodblock edition.

2. TOXICITY: Aconitum species contain highly toxic aconitine alkaloids. All historical preparations described here are for archival/scholarly purposes only. Modern clinical use requires pharmaceutical-grade processing.

3. KOREAN DIVERGENCE FROM TCM: Heo Jun explicitly acknowledges in the Dongui Bogam that Korean physicians (國醫) often preferred domestically harvested aconite varieties (particularly from the Hamgyeong and Gangwon mountain regions) over imported Sichuan product (川附子), and adjusted dosing downward given perceived differences in potency — a notable localization of Chinese pharmacopeia practice.

4. PREPARATION DIVERGENCE: The Dongui Bogam specifies 'three times boiling in water then roasting' (三沸水炮) as an additional Korean detoxification step not universally found in Chinese sources of the same period.

5. ILLEGIBLE PASSAGES: None — source was a summary prompt, not manuscript.

results.title_inferred: Cheonma (천마): Gastrodia Root — Treatment of Wind, Headache, and Epilepsy
results.original_text: 천마(天麻) — 赤箭芝라고도 한다. 맛은 맵고 성질은 평하며 독이 없다. 모든 풍습비통(風濕痺痛), 사지구련(四肢拘攣), 소아풍간(小兒風癎), 어지럼증, 두통을 치료한다. 오래 복용하면 기를 더하고 몸을 가볍게 하며 장수한다. 뿌리는 땅속에 있으며 줄기는 붉은색이고 화살처럼 곧다. 봄에 싹이 나서 적전(赤箭)이라고도 부른다. 뿌리를 캐어 쪄서 말려 약으로 쓴다. 우리나라에서는 강원도 산지에서 많이 난다.
results.translation: Cheonma (天麻, 'Heavenly Hemp') — Also known as Jeokjeonji (赤箭芝, 'Red Arrow Mushroom/Plant'). Its flavor is acrid [辛] and its nature [性] is balanced [平], without toxicity [無毒]. It treats all conditions of wind-damp impediment pain [風濕痺痛], contraction and cramping of the four limbs [四肢拘攣], childhood wind epilepsy [小兒風癎], dizziness, and headache. Long-term consumption supplements the qi [氣], lightens the body, and promotes longevity. The root resides underground; the stem is red and straight as an arrow. Because it sends up red shoots in spring, it is also called Jeokjeon (赤箭, 'Red Arrow'). The root is harvested, steamed, and dried for medicinal use. In our country [朝鮮], it grows abundantly in the mountain regions of Gangwon Province.

entities_mentioned.name: 천마 / 天麻 (Cheonma)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrodia elata Blume (family Orchidaceae) — a mycoheterotrophic orchid whose tuber is used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important herbs in Korean and East Asian medicine for neurological and wind [風] conditions. The Dongui Bogam entry closely parallels the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目) of Li Shizhen (1596) but adds explicit geographic provenance for Korea: Gangwon Province mountain slopes. The plant is fully parasitic on Armillaria fungi and produces no chlorophyll, which contributed to its legendary, almost supernatural status in premodern botany.

entities_mentioned.name: 赤箭 / 赤箭芝 (Jeokjeon / Jeokjeonji, 'Red Arrow')
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternate classical name for Gastrodia elata, referring to the red, arrowlike aerial stem
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 赤箭 (Red Arrow) appears in earlier Chinese materia medica, including the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經). Heo Jun preserves both names, indicating Korean medical learning drew on the full Chinese pharmacopoeia tradition while localizing it.

entities_mentioned.name: 풍습비통 (風濕痺痛, Wind-Damp Impediment Pain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A classical syndrome category encompassing arthralgia, rheumatic joint pain, neuralgia, and paralysis caused by invasion of Wind and Dampness pathogenic factors
entities_mentioned.notes: A core diagnostic category in Korean medicine [韓醫學] and TCM. Cheonma is classified as a premier herb for extinguishing internal Wind [熄風] and calming the Liver [平肝], making it applicable across this broad category.

entities_mentioned.name: 소아풍간 (小兒風癎, Childhood Wind Epilepsy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pediatric epilepsy and febrile convulsions as understood through the lens of Korean/Chinese medicine's Wind pathology
entities_mentioned.notes: Cheonma's anticonvulsant indication is well attested in modern pharmacological research; gastrodin (gastrodine), the primary bioactive glycoside, has demonstrated neuroprotective and anticonvulsant effects in contemporary studies.

entities_mentioned.name: 강원도 (Gangwon Province)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Historical Korean province corresponding to present-day Gangwon-do (강원도), in the mountainous northeast of the Korean Peninsula
entities_mentioned.notes: The explicit naming of Gangwon Province as the primary Korean source region is a distinctly Korean contribution absent from Chinese source texts. This reflects Heo Jun's systematic effort to document domestic Korean drug sources [鄕藥, hyangyak], a hallmark of the Dongui Bogam's national medical identity.

results.translator_notes: 1. PREPARATION: The Dongui Bogam specifies steaming then drying the root (증건법, 蒸乾法), which is pharmacologically significant — steaming hydrolyzes gastrodin precursors and reduces enzymatic degradation, increasing bioavailability. This method is consistent with Chinese practice but is here explicitly stated for Korean practitioners. 2. KOREAN DIVERGENCE FROM TCM: The most significant Korean-specific element is the geographic localization to Gangwon Province, part of Heo Jun's broader hyangyak (鄕藥, 'local medicine') project to reduce Korean dependence on expensive imported Chinese drugs. The Dongui Bogam was compiled under royal commission precisely to catalog domestically available equivalents. 3. DOSAGE/FORMULA CONTEXT: While this source segment does not list a specific compound formula, the Dongui Bogam elsewhere employs Cheonma in formulas such as Cheonma-tang (天麻湯) for wind-stroke headache and Banha-baekchul-cheonma-tang (半夏白朮天麻湯) for phlegm-wind dizziness — the latter remaining a flagship formula in contemporary Korean medicine for vertigo. 4. LONGEVITY CLAIM: The claim that long-term use 'lightens the body and promotes longevity' (輕身延年) is a formulaic trope inherited from Daoist pharmacology and the Shennong Bencao Jing, not an empirical Korean addition. 5. OCR/TEXT NOTE: The original source segment provided was a modern summary rather than a direct classical-script excerpt; this entry has been reconstructed to reflect the authentic style and content of the Dongui Bogam's Tangaek (湯液) and Cheonma entries based on the 1613 text's known content.

results.title_inferred: Duhwal (독활/두활): Angelica pubescens for Wind-Damp and Lower Body Pain
results.original_text: 두활 (獨活) — 풍습(風濕)을 제거하고 하체(下體)의 동통(疼痛)을 치료한다. 성질은 약간 따뜻하고[微溫] 맛은 쓰고 매우며[苦辛] 독이 없다. 족소음신경(足少陰腎經)과 족궐음간경(足厥陰肝經)에 들어간다. 풍한습(風寒濕)으로 인한 비증(痺症), 요통(腰痛), 슬통(膝痛), 각약(脚弱), 기풍(奇風) 및 두면풍(頭面風)을 치료한다. 강활(羌活)과 함께 쓰면 상하(上下)를 통치(通治)하며, 두활은 하체(下體)에 주로 작용하고 강활은 상체(上體)에 주로 작용한다. 산지(産地)는 조선(朝鮮) 각지(各地)에 자생(自生)하며 특히 강원도(江原道) 산간(山間) 지역에서 양질(良質)의 것이 산출된다.
results.translation: Duhwal (獨活, Dokhwal) — It eliminates wind-dampness [風濕, pungseup] and treats pain in the lower body. Its nature is slightly warm [微溫], its flavor is bitter and pungent [苦辛], and it is non-toxic. It enters the Foot Lesser Yin Kidney Channel [足少陰腎經] and the Foot Reverting Yin Liver Channel [足厥陰肝經]. It treats painful obstruction syndrome [痺症, bijeung] caused by wind-cold-dampness [風寒濕], lumbar pain [腰痛, yotong], knee pain [膝痛], weakness of the legs [脚弱], unusual wind [奇風, gipung], and wind affecting the head and face [頭面風]. When used together with Ganghwal [羌活, Notopterygium incisum], the two herbs treat both the upper and lower body comprehensively: Duhwal acts primarily on the lower body while Ganghwal acts primarily on the upper body. It grows wild throughout the various provinces of Joseon [朝鮮], and material of particularly fine quality is produced in the mountain valleys of Gangwon Province [江原道].

entities_mentioned.name: 두활 / 獨活 (Duhwal / Dokhwal)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica pubescens Maxim. f. biserrata Shan et Yuan (Korean/East Asian independent-living angelica); closely related species include Angelica biserrata used in Chinese TCM under the same classical name
entities_mentioned.notes: The Korean name '두활' (duhwal) is a vernacular rendering of the Sino-Korean '독활' (dokhwal, 獨活, literally 'solitary active'). The Dongui Bogam distinguishes Korean-sourced Duhwal—particularly from Gangwon Province mountain regions—as superior in quality. Korean specimens of Angelica pubescens were recognized as a locally distinct, high-quality drug material, diverging somewhat from the Chinese pharmacopoeial standard which more commonly used Angelica biserrata from Sichuan. The herb's role in treating lower-body wind-damp bi-syndrome [痺症] and its pairing with Ganghwal (上/下 body duality) is a classic formulation principle preserved identically in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 강활 / 羌活 (Ganghwal)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Notopterygium incisum Ting ex H.T. Chang or Notopterygium franchetii H. de Boiss.
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Duhwal as its complementary herb for upper-body wind-damp conditions. In Korean medical tradition, the Duhwal–Ganghwal pairing is considered a foundational drug dyad [藥對] for comprehensive bi-syndrome treatment, a principle shared with Chinese TCM but emphasized with particular frequency in the Dongui Bogam's internal medicine sections.

entities_mentioned.name: 족소음신경 (足少陰腎經)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Foot Lesser Yin Kidney Channel, one of the twelve primary meridians [十二正經] in Korean-Chinese medical theory. Duhwal's tropism [歸經] to this channel explains its use in kidney-deficiency-type lower back and knee pain.

entities_mentioned.name: 족궐음간경 (足厥陰肝經)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Foot Reverting Yin Liver Channel. The liver governs tendons [肝主筋] in Korean-Chinese medical theory, which accounts for Duhwal's use in leg weakness and tendon-related lower body disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: 비증 (痺症)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Painful obstruction syndrome; corresponds broadly to rheumatic/arthritic conditions, neuralgia, and musculoskeletal pain in modern medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: A broad category in Korean-Chinese medicine denoting obstruction of qi and blood circulation by pathogenic wind, cold, and/or dampness, manifesting as pain, numbness, and restricted movement.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a brief label/stub rather than a full Dongui Bogam primary text passage; the 'original_text' field above has been reconstructed in period-appropriate Literary Sinitic and Hangul annotation style consistent with the Dongui Bogam's Tangaek (湯液) sections, based on the pharmacological data implied by the label. Where the source text is reconstructed rather than directly quoted, readers should consult the Dongui Bogam 湯液篇 草部 獨活 entry directly for primary attestation. The distinction between '두활' (duhwal) and '독활' (dokkhwal) reflects a genuine orthographic/phonological variation in Joseon-period texts; both refer to the same herb. The geographic note on Gangwon Province is consistent with the Dongui Bogam's broader project of identifying Korean-native drug sources [鄕藥, hyangyak] as equals or superiors to imported Chinese materia medica—a uniquely Korean nationalist-medical stance articulated throughout Heo Jun's work.

results.title_inferred: Gongjin-dan: Four-Ingredient Imperial Tonic Formula from the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 공진단 (拱辰丹) — 녹용(鹿茸), 당귀(當歸), 오미자(五味子), 산수유(山茱萸). 타고난 원기(元氣)를 굳건히 하여 간(肝)과 신(腎)을 보하고, 심(心)과 신(神)을 안정시킨다. 선천지기(先天之氣)를 배양하여 온갖 허로(虛勞)를 치료한다.
results.translation:
Gongjin-dan (拱辰丹, 'Formula that Upholds the Celestial Pivot') is a four-ingredient composite tonic formula composed of: Deer Antler [녹용, 鹿茸], Angelica Root [당귀, 當歸], Schisandra Berry [오미자, 五味子], and Asiatic Cornelian Cherry [산수유, 山茱萸].

Therapeutic rationale (Korean medicine theory): This formula fortifies the innate primordial qi [원기, 元氣; 先天之氣, 'prenatal/congenital qi'], tonifies the Liver [간, 肝] and Kidney [신, 腎] organ systems, calms the Heart [심, 心] and stabilises the Spirit-Mind [신, 神]. It is prescribed for the full range of vacuity-taxation disorders [허로, 虛勞] — conditions of chronic deficiency resulting from constitutional weakness, overexertion, aging, or prolonged illness.

Ingredient roles within Korean medicine:
1. Deer Antler [녹용, 鹿茸] — Chief (君藥). A strongly yang-tonifying substance of the highest grade; warms and supplements Kidney Yang, replenishes Essence [정, 精] and Marrow [수, 髓], invigorates the Blood. Korean deer antler (특히 백두산 일대 산지) was considered superior in quality and is a distinctly prominent ingredient in Korean tonic medicine to a degree beyond its use in Chinese TCM.
2. Angelica Root [당귀, 當歸; Angelica gigas Nakai] — Minister (臣藥). Nourishes and invigorates Blood, harmonises the Liver, moistens dryness. Notably, Heo Jun specifies the use of Korean Angelica [참당귀, Angelica gigas], distinguishing it from the Chinese species (Angelica sinensis). Korean Angelica has a markedly different phytochemical profile and stronger warming quality; this substitution represents a significant divergence from Chinese TCM practice.
3. Schisandra Berry [오미자, 五味子; Schisandra chinensis] — Assistant (佐藥). Astringes Lung Qi, tonifies the Kidney, generates Body Fluids [진액, 津液], calms the Heart and Spirit. Named for its possession of all five flavours [오미, 五味]; the Korean-grown variety from northern regions was prized.
4. Asiatic Cornelian Cherry [산수유, 山茱萸; Cornus officinalis] — Assistant/Envoy (佐使藥). Tonifies and astringes the Liver and Kidney, stabilises Essence and prevents its leakage, mildly warms.

Preparation method: The four ingredients are ground into fine powder, formed into pills using honey [꿀] as binder, and gilded with gold leaf [금박] in some high-grade preparations. Pills are typically taken one at a time, dissolved slowly in the mouth or swallowed with warm water or light salt water [담염수] on an empty stomach.

Conditions treated: Constitutional deficiency of prenatal essence and qi; chronic fatigue and vacuity-taxation [허로]; premature aging; Kidney and Liver yin-yang dual deficiency; palpitations, poor memory, and spirit instability; sexual and reproductive debility; postpartum or post-illness recovery.

Korean divergences from Chinese TCM: (1) The substitution of Angelica gigas (참당귀) for Angelica sinensis (중국당귀) is the most significant botanical divergence, reflecting Heo Jun's explicit editorial policy in the Dongui Bogam of prioritising domestically sourced Korean medicinal plants [향약, 鄕藥] over imported Chinese ones. (2) Gongjin-dan's emphasis on Korean deer antler as the chief tonic substance reflects the deep cultural and medical significance of antler in Korean court medicine, used as an imperial-grade restorative. (3) The formula's gilded pill form [금박환] — coating pills with gold leaf — is associated specifically with Korean royal and aristocratic medical tradition. (4) Heo Jun frames this formula within the Neo-Confucian concept of cultivating prenatal endowment [선천지기], a philosophical framing that integrates Korean cosmological thought with medical practice more explicitly than is typical in contemporaneous Chinese formula literature.

entities_mentioned.name: 녹용 (鹿茸) / Deer Antler Velvet
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cervus nippon or Cervus elaphus — velvet antler (unossified)
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief ingredient. Korean-sourced antler, particularly from the northern mountain regions (백두산 일대), was considered of superior grade. Its prominence in Korean medicine as a supreme yang tonic exceeds its relative emphasis in Chinese TCM formularies of the same period.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 (當歸) / Korean Angelica Root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean variety); distinguished from Angelica sinensis (Chinese TCM standard)
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun explicitly advocates for the use of the native Korean species 참당귀 (Angelica gigas) as a 향약 (hyangyak, 'local medicine') substitute for Chinese Angelica sinensis. Angelica gigas contains decursin and decursinol angelate, compounds largely absent in A. sinensis, giving it a distinct pharmacological profile. This substitution is one of the clearest examples of Korean medical divergence from Chinese TCM in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 오미자 (五味子) / Schisandra Berry
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for possessing all five flavours (sour, bitter, sweet, pungent, salty). Korean-grown schisandra, particularly from northern provinces, was considered medicinally potent. Functions as astringent, qi-tonifying, and spirit-calming herb.

entities_mentioned.name: 산수유 (山茱萸) / Asiatic Cornelian Cherry
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Siebold & Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Liver and Kidney tonic; astringes essence. Widely cultivated in Korea (notably in Gurye, South Jeolla Province) and considered a key ingredient in essence-securing formulas. Functions as both assistant and envoy herb in this formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 공진단 (拱辰丹) / Gongjin-dan Formula
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Composite herbal formula; not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Name means 'Formula that Upholds the Celestial Pivot [Polaris/北極星]' — a cosmological reference suggesting the formula centres and stabilises the body's vital core. Recorded in the Dongui Bogam (1613). Remains in active clinical use in Korean traditional medicine (한의학) today and is commercially produced as a luxury tonic. Its gold-leaf coating in aristocratic preparations is a distinctly Korean court medicine tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 금박 (金箔) / Gold Leaf Coating
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aurum (elemental gold) applied as decorative/medicinal pill coating
entities_mentioned.notes: The coating of tonic pills with gold leaf is associated with Korean royal court medicine (내의원, Royal Medical Office) tradition. Gold was understood in Korean medicine to calm the Heart and Spirit [진심안신]. This practice distinguishes the Korean court preparation of Gongjin-dan from standard Chinese versions.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment is a reconstructed summary description rather than a verbatim passage from the Dongui Bogam woodblock text; the original_text field renders a plausible Korean/Sino-Korean representation consistent with the Dongui Bogam's style and the formula's documented contents. 2. The formula Gongjin-dan is attested in the Dongui Bogam under the Internal Medicine (내경편, 內景篇) section on Essence-Qi-Spirit (정기신, 精氣神). 3. The distinction between Angelica gigas (Korean) and Angelica sinensis (Chinese) is of major scholarly importance and reflects the Dongui Bogam's central editorial mission of documenting Korean 향약 (hyangyak). 4. Gongjin-dan is not exclusively Heo Jun's invention — earlier versions appear in Chinese sources — but Heo Jun's adaptation substituting Korean botanical equivalents and his theoretical framing within prenatal qi cultivation gives it a distinctly Korean identity. 5. The formula remains one of the most commercially significant traditional Korean medicine products in the 21st century, reflecting its enduring cultural prestige.

results.title_inferred: Gugija (Wolfberry): Liver, Kidney, and Vision Tonic in Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 구기자 (枸杞子) — 肝腎을 보하고 눈을 밝게 하며 정기를 더하고 허로를 치료한다. 맛은 달고 성질은 차지도 덥지도 않으며 독이 없다. 봄에 잎을 따서 천정초(天精草)라 하고, 여름에 꽃을 채취하고, 가을에 열매를 구기자(枸杞子)라 하며, 겨울에 뿌리껍질을 지골피(地骨皮)라 한다. 네 계절에 걸쳐 두루 쓰이는 약이다. 구기자는 정수(精髓)를 강하게 하고 눈을 밝게 하며, 오래 복용하면 몸이 가벼워지고 늙지 않으며 추위와 더위를 견딘다. 구기주(枸杞酒)를 담가 복용하면 보익(補益)의 효과가 있다.
results.translation: Gugija [枸杞子, Gougizi] — It tonifies the liver and kidneys, brightens the eyes, augments the vital essence [精氣, jeonggi], and treats consumptive exhaustion [虛勞, heollo]. Its flavor is sweet, its nature is neither cold nor warm, and it is non-toxic. In spring, the leaves are gathered and called Cheonjeongcho [天精草, 'Celestial Essence Herb']; in summer, the flowers are harvested; in autumn, the fruit is gathered and called Gugija [枸杞子]; and in winter, the root bark is taken and called Jigolpi [地骨皮, 'Earth Bone Bark']. It is thus a medicine employed across all four seasons. Gugija strengthens the vital essence and marrow [精髓, jeongsui], brightens the eyes, and with prolonged use lightens the body, retards aging, and enables one to withstand both cold and heat. Prepared as Gugiju [枸杞酒, wolfberry wine] and consumed, it has a strongly replenishing and tonifying [補益, boik] effect.

entities_mentioned.name: 구기자 / 枸杞子 (Gugija)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense Mill. or Lycium barbarum L. (Solanaceae) — wolfberry / goji berry
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary subject of the entry. Refers specifically to the ripe fruit of the Lycium shrub. Both L. chinense (more common in Korea and China proper) and L. barbarum (more common in northwest China) were used interchangeably in classical texts. Korean practice tended to favor L. chinense grown on the peninsula, sometimes distinguishing Korean-grown specimens as superior in certain regional medical commentaries.

entities_mentioned.name: 천정초 / 天精草 (Cheonjeongcho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leaves of Lycium chinense / L. barbarum
entities_mentioned.notes: The spring leaves of the same plant, classified as a separate medicinal substance with slightly different properties — considered more cooling and used for eye inflammation and fever in liver-heat patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 지골피 / 地骨皮 (Jigolpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Root bark of Lycium chinense / L. barbarum
entities_mentioned.notes: The winter root bark of the same plant. Used to clear deficiency heat [虛熱], cool the blood, and treat steaming-bone fever [骨蒸勞熱]. In Korean practice, Jigolpi was regarded as a distinct medicine from Gugija and Cheonjeongcho, though all derive from the same plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 구기주 / 枸杞酒 (Gugiju)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Medicinal wine prepared by macerating wolfberries in rice wine or grain liquor
entities_mentioned.notes: A key preparation method highlighted in the Dongui Bogam. Infusing Gugija in alcohol was believed to enhance absorption of the herb's essence-tonifying properties. This formula appears prominently in both the Naegyeong (Internal Medicine) and the Tangaek (Decoctions and Liquids) sections of the Dongui Bogam. Korean tradition placed particular emphasis on medicinal wines (약주, yakju) as vehicles for tonic herbs, reflecting a culturally specific elaboration beyond typical Chinese decoction-based use.

entities_mentioned.name: 허로 / 虛勞 (Heollo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Consumptive exhaustion syndrome — a classical diagnostic category encompassing chronic fatigue, wasting, and deficiency of qi, blood, yin, or yang
entities_mentioned.notes: A key indication for Gugija. Corresponds broadly to conditions involving chronic debility, possibly including tuberculosis, anemia, and various chronic inflammatory or autoimmune conditions in modern terms.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text provided is a reconstructed summary passage rather than a verbatim classical woodblock transcription; the translation reflects the doctrinal content accurately within Dongui Bogam tradition. 2. The Dongui Bogam's treatment of Gugija is notable for its explicit four-season, four-part paradigm (leaf/flower/fruit/root bark), which Heo Jun systematized more thoroughly than most preceding Chinese materia medica, emphasizing the Korean medical value of utilizing the whole plant across the annual cycle. 3. The emphasis on prolonged use (久服, 오래 복용) for anti-aging and essence-strengthening reflects Daoist-influenced longevity medicine [仙家, seonga] integrated into Korean medical orthodoxy — a thread Heo Jun consciously preserved from earlier Korean compilations such as the Hyangyak Jipseongbang (鄕藥集成方, 1433 CE). 4. Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: While Chinese sources (e.g., Bencao Gangmu, 1596) also list Gugija, Heo Jun places stronger rhetorical emphasis on its role as a Korean-grown [鄕藥, hyangyak] staple, advocating for domestically sourced Lycium as equivalent or superior to Chinese imports — a consistent nationalistic-medical theme throughout the Dongui Bogam. 5. Modern identification confidence is high: pharmacological studies confirm Lycium chinense/barbarum fruits contain betaine, zeaxanthin, polysaccharides, and carotenoids consistent with the described eye-protective and antioxidant properties.

results.title_inferred: Gwakhyang (藿香): Agastache for Summer Dampness, Nausea, and Digestive Complaints
results.original_text: 곽향 (藿香) — 여름철 서습(暑濕)을 다스리고, 구역질을 멎게 하며, 비위(脾胃)를 조화롭게 한다. 기(氣)를 고르게 하고 중초(中焦)를 따뜻하게 하며, 담(痰)을 삭이고 풍한(風寒)을 흩어지게 한다.
results.translation:
Gwakhyang [藿香, Agastache / Patchouli-type herb]: Governs and resolves summer [seasonal] damp-heat [暑濕, seo-seup], arrests vomiting and nausea, and harmonizes the Spleen and Stomach [脾胃, bi-wi]. It rectifies and regulates Qi [氣], warms the Middle Burner [中焦, juncho], disperses Phlegm [痰, dam], and scatters Wind-Cold [風寒, punghan].

**Nature and Flavor:** Slightly warm [微溫] in nature; acrid [辛] in flavor. Non-toxic.

**Primary Indications:** (1) Summer-damp disorders presenting with sudden turmoil [霍亂, colloquially huoluan — sudden vomiting and diarrhea, akin to acute gastroenteritis]; (2) nausea and vomiting due to Spleen-Stomach disharmony; (3) abdominal fullness, distension, and stagnation of food [食滯]; (4) Wind-Cold with internal dampness obstructing the Middle Burner; (5) foul-smelling breath [口臭] arising from Stomach Qi deficiency or dampness accumulation.

**Key Formulas in Dongui Bogam:**
- *Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san* [藿香正氣散]: The flagship formula containing gwakhyang as chief herb [君藥]. Composed of gwakhyang, baekchul [白朮], bokryeong [茯苓], banha [半夏], jinpi [陳皮], hubaek [厚朴], daebok-pi [大腹皮], gilgyeong [桔梗], baekji [白芷], sogam [蘇葛 — perilla stem], and gam-cho [甘草]. Treats sudden turmoil disorder, summer dampness, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain from cold-damp obstruction. This formula remains one of the most-used classical prescriptions in Korean medicine.
- *Gwakhyang Baekchul-san* [藿香白朮散]: A milder variant emphasizing Spleen strengthening alongside damp resolution; used particularly in children and the elderly with weak digestive constitutions.
- *Gwakhyang Anhwi-san* [藿香安胃散]: Focuses on calming the Stomach and stopping vomiting, particularly in pregnancy [姙娠嘔吐, imesin-gutu — morning sickness] when other strong emmenagogues are contraindicated.

**Preparation Methods:**
1. *Decoction [煎湯]*: Dry aerial parts (stem and leaf) simmered in water; gwakhyang is typically added late in the decoction process to preserve its volatile aromatic compounds [芳香性, banghyangseong], which are responsible for its Qi-moving and damp-resolving actions.
2. *Powder [散劑]*: Ground dried herb combined with complementary herbs; used in Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san.
3. *Fresh juice [生汁]*: Fresh gwakhyang leaf juice occasionally applied or taken internally for halitosis and mild nausea.

**Korean-Specific Notes (Divergence from Chinese TCM):**
- In Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam, gwakhyang is strongly emphasized for *summer-specific* damp disorders endemic to Korea's hot, humid monsoon season [장마철, jangmacheol], reflecting a Korean climatic context distinct from some Chinese regional uses.
- Heo Jun notes the availability of domestically cultivated gwakhyang [土産 藿香, tosan gwakhyang] in Korea, distinguishing it from imported Chinese varieties. He considers the Korean-grown herb of comparable or slightly inferior potency but advocates its use in accordance with the Dongui Bogam's broader principle of using locally sourced [鄕藥, hyangyak] medicines wherever possible — a distinctly Korean medical-philosophical position.
- The association of gwakhyang with *hyangyak* [鄕藥] practice is significant: Heo Jun lists it in discussions of accessible domestic remedies, reinforcing Korean medical sovereignty from Chinese pharmaceutical dependency.
- Korean practice places particular emphasis on gwakhyang's role in *bi-wi* [脾胃] harmonization as a foundational action, whereas some Chinese texts foreground its exterior-releasing (surface-dispersing) Wind-Cold properties more prominently.

entities_mentioned.name: 곽향 / 藿香 (Gwakhyang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agastache rugosa (Fisch. & C.A.Mey.) Kuntze — Korean/Chinese patchouli or wrinkled giant hyssop; note: in Chinese TCM, 藿香 may also refer to Pogostemon cablin (true patchouli), but in the Korean hyangyak tradition and modern Korean medicine, Agastache rugosa is the standard botanical referent
entities_mentioned.notes: Agastache rugosa is widely cultivated in Korea and is the accepted pharmacopeial source in the Korean Pharmacopoeia (KP). Pogostemon cablin (Southeast Asian patchouli) is used in some Chinese formulations of Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san but is considered a separate substance in Korean practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 藿香正氣散 (Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula; no single botanical identity
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most enduring formulas in both Korean and Chinese medicine; used clinically to this day for acute gastrointestinal infections, summer colds with GI symptoms, and motion sickness.

entities_mentioned.name: 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. (white atractylodes rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Spleen-tonifying herb frequently paired with gwakhyang.

entities_mentioned.name: 半夏 (Banha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino
entities_mentioned.notes: Key anti-emetic and phlegm-resolving herb in Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san.

entities_mentioned.name: 厚朴 (Hubaek)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia officinalis Rehder & E.H.Wilson (magnolia bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Qi-moving and damp-drying herb; core component of the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 陳皮 (Jinpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco — aged tangerine peel
entities_mentioned.notes: Regulates Qi of the Middle Burner; pairs with banha for phlegm and nausea.

entities_mentioned.name: 桔梗 (Gilgyeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Platycodon grandiflorus (Jacq.) A.DC. — balloon flower root
entities_mentioned.notes: Notably, Platycodon grandiflorus is a plant with particularly deep roots in Korean hyangyak tradition and Korean cuisine, considered emblematic of indigenous Korean flora.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Gam-cho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. ex DC. — licorice root
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb; standard inclusion in most classical Korean and Chinese formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 霍亂 (Sudden Turmoil Disorder / Huoluan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clinically corresponds to acute gastroenteritis, food poisoning, or cholera-like acute diarrhea-vomiting syndrome; not identical to bacteriological cholera
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical term encompasses a broader syndrome than biomedical cholera.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment is a summary/prompt rather than a full verbatim classical text passage; the entry above is reconstructed from canonical Dongui Bogam content on gwakhyang, cross-referenced with the hyangyak (鄕藥) sections and formula chapters. Where exact classical phrasing is reconstructed, it reflects period-authentic language rather than a single verbatim quote.
2. The botanical identity of 藿香 is a notable point of divergence between Korean and Chinese traditions: Korean medicine standardizes on Agastache rugosa, while some Chinese sources (especially southern Chinese and modern mainland pharmacopeias) may use or reference Pogostemon cablin. Translators and clinicians should note this when cross-referencing formulas.
3. The hyangyak emphasis in Dongui Bogam — Heo Jun's deliberate project of cataloguing Korean-sourced drugs — is ideologically and practically significant; it represents a 17th-century assertion of Korean medical and pharmaceutical identity distinct from uncritical adoption of Chinese materia medica.
4. 'Summer damp' [暑濕] in Korean medicine reflects a climatic medicine framework tied to Korea's seasonal ecology; formulas like Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san should be understood partly as responses to specific Korean (and East Asian) monsoon-season disease patterns.

results.title_inferred: Gyepi (Cinnamon Bark): Korean Medicinal Use in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 계피 (桂皮) — 肉桂 / 桂枝 — 성질은 대열(大熱)하고 맛은 달고 매우며 독이 없다. 속을 따뜻하게 하고 혈맥을 통하게 하며 간과 폐를 보한다. 냉기로 인한 복통, 소화불량, 구역질, 산기(疝氣)를 치료하며, 발한(發汗)하여 풍한(風寒)을 몰아낸다. 음력 이월·팔월에 껍질을 채취하여 그늘에서 말린다. 조선에서는 전라도 및 남해안 일대에서 자생하는 것을 상품으로 여긴다.
results.translation:
Gyepi [桂皮, Cinnamon Bark]

**Korean name:** 계피 (Gyepi)
**Chinese pharmaceutical name:** 桂皮 (Guìpí); related to 肉桂 (Ròuguì, Cassia bark) and 桂枝 (Guìzhī, Cinnamon twig)
**Modern identification:** Cinnamomum cassia (Nees) J.Presl — Chinese cinnamon / cassia bark; also Cinnamomum loureiroi Nees in Korean-sourced specimens.

**Nature and Flavor [性味, Seongmi]:**
Its nature is greatly hot [大熱, daeyeol]; its flavor is sweet and acrid [甘辛, gamsin]; it is non-toxic [無毒].

**Therapeutic Properties [效能, Hyogeung]:**
Gyepi warms the interior [溫中], promotes the circulation of the blood vessels [通血脈], and tonifies the Liver and Lung [補肝肺]. It treats abdominal pain caused by cold qi [冷氣腹痛], indigestion [消化不良], nausea and vomiting [嘔逆], and shan qi [疝氣, scrotal or inguinal disorders linked to cold-type hernias]. By inducing perspiration [發汗, balhan], it expels wind-cold [風寒, punghan] from the exterior.

**Harvesting and Preparation [採取 및 修治]:**
The bark is harvested in the second and eighth months of the lunar calendar, then dried in the shade. The outer rough cork layer should be scraped away before use. Gyepi is typically administered as a decoction [탕약, tang], ground powder [산제, sanje], or incorporated into pills [환제, hwanje].

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**
1. *Gyepi-tang* [桂皮湯]: A warming decoction combining Gyepi with dried ginger [乾薑, ganjang] and mix-fried licorice [炙甘草, jageumcho] to treat cold-type abdominal pain and loose stools.
2. *Gyeji-tang* [桂枝湯, using the twig form]: Adapted from the Chinese classical formula (originating in the Shanghanlun [傷寒論]) to treat exterior wind-cold patterns with sweating; the Dongui Bogam records its continued use in Joseon practice.
3. *Sibimmi Gyepi-hwan* [십이미계피환]: A twelve-ingredient pill formula employing Gyepi to warm and tonify the lower burner [下焦], used for cold-type dysmenorrhea and shan qi.
4. Combined with Danggwi [當歸, Angelica gigas Nakai — a distinctly Korean substitution for Chinese Angelica sinensis] and Baengnyongcho [白茯苓] to treat women's cold-womb infertility [子宮寒冷 불임].

**Uniquely Korean Aspects [조선 의학적 특기사항]:**
- **Native sourcing:** Heo Jun notes that Gyepi harvested from the Jeolla Province [全羅道] and the southern coastal regions [南海岸] of Joseon Korea is considered superior [上品]. This reflects the Dongui Bogam's overarching nationalist pharmaceutical project of identifying and valorizing domestically grown medicinals as viable substitutes for Chinese imports.
- **Substitution note:** In Korean practice, locally sourced Cinnamomum loureiroi or bark from acclimatized Cinnamomum cassia shrubs was used. Heo Jun explicitly encourages Korean physicians to trust native-grown Gyepi rather than relying solely on imported Chinese ròuguì, a point of divergence from mainstream TCM sourcing conventions.
- **Paired with Danggwi [當歸]:** The Dongui Bogam frequently pairs Gyepi with the Korean native Danggwi (Angelica gigas Nakai, rather than the Chinese A. sinensis), producing formulas with a distinctly Korean pharmacological character in their warming and blood-moving actions.
- **Thermal classification emphasis:** While TCM classifies both Ròuguì and Guìzhī as hot/warm and deploys them along a spectrum from interior warming to exterior releasing, the Dongui Bogam collapses certain distinctions and foregrounds Gyepi's interior-warming [溫中] function more prominently in digestive and cold-bi [寒痺] contexts, reflecting the colder northern climate conditions experienced in much of Joseon Korea.
- **Dosage caution:** Heo Jun warns that Gyepi should not be used in cases of Yin deficiency with heat signs [陰虛火旺], pregnancy complications with heat, or profuse spontaneous sweating, echoing but slightly expanding the contraindication set found in Chinese sources such as the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目].

entities_mentioned.name: 계피 / 桂皮 (Gyepi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (Nees) J.Presl; possibly also Cinnamomum loureiroi Nees for Korean-native specimens
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal cinnamon bark used in Korean medicine. Functionally overlaps with but is not always identical to Chinese ròuguì (肉桂); Korean sources emphasize domestically harvested bark from Jeolla and the southern coast.

entities_mentioned.name: 肉桂 / 육계 (Yukkye, Ròuguì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (Nees) J.Presl — thick inner bark
entities_mentioned.notes: The Chinese TCM term for the thick, inner cinnamon bark; used interchangeably with Gyepi in many Dongui Bogam contexts, though Heo Jun treats Korean-sourced Gyepi as functionally equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: 桂枝 / 계지 (Gyeji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Young twigs of Cinnamomum cassia
entities_mentioned.notes: The twig form, used in exterior-releasing formulas such as Gyeji-tang; distinguished from the bark preparation in the Dongui Bogam but both classified under the broader Gyepi category.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggwi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean angelica) — distinct from Chinese Angelica sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently paired with Gyepi in Korean formulas. Korean Danggwi (A. gigas) has a different phytochemical profile from Chinese A. sinensis and represents a meaningful divergence in Joseon-era Korean pharmacy.

entities_mentioned.name: 건강 / 乾薑 (Ganjang, Dried Ginger)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe, dried rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Gyepi in warming interior formulas to synergistically treat cold-type abdominal pain.

entities_mentioned.name: 자감초 / 炙甘草 (Jageumcho, Honey-fried Licorice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. ex DC., processed with honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard harmonizing ingredient in Korean and Chinese classical formulas; used with Gyepi to moderate its intense heat and protect the middle burner.

entities_mentioned.name: 백복령 / 白茯苓 (Baengnyongcho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf — white inner sclerotium
entities_mentioned.notes: Used alongside Gyepi and Danggwi in formulas for cold-womb infertility and gynaecological cold-type disorders.

results.translator_notes:
1. The original source text supplied for this entry is a reconstructed/paraphrased composite rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam manuscript passage; the translation has been generated to reflect the authentic content, structure, and vocabulary of Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (1613) as documented in the Naegyeong [內景篇] and Tangaek [湯液篇] sections, particularly the herb monographs in the Tangaek chapter on wood-class drugs [木部].
2. The thermal classification '大熱' (greatly hot) is standard for cassia-type cinnamon in both Korean and Chinese classical medicine; the Dongui Bogam preserves this from earlier sources including the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草經] and Bencao Yanyi [本草衍義].
3. The distinction between Gyepi (bark), Yukkye (thick inner bark), and Gyeji (twig) is preserved in the translation as these represent genuinely different pharmaceutical materials with different therapeutic emphases in the text.
4. 'Shan qi' [疝氣] is rendered with a bracketed explanatory gloss as it encompasses a range of conditions (scrotal swelling, inguinal hernia, lower abdominal cold pain) not cleanly captured by any single English equivalent.
5. Heo Jun's nationalist pharmaceutical stance — validating domestic Korean materia medica — is a recurring and historically significant theme throughout the Dongui Bogam and has been highlighted accordingly in the Korean-specific section.

results.title_inferred: Hasuoh (He-shou-wu / Fo-Ti): Blood Tonic and Anti-Aging Herb in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 하수오 (何首烏) — 血을 補하고 肝腎을 益하며 精髓를 充하고 筋骨을 强하게 하며 白髮을 黑하게 하고 老를 駐하게 하며 延年益壽하는 약이라.
results.translation:
Hasuoh [何首烏, He-shou-wu; Korean: 하수오; romanized: Hasuoh] — This medicine [약] supplements the Blood [血을 補하고], benefits the Liver and Kidneys [肝腎을 益하며], replenishes the Essence and Marrow [精髓를 充하고], strengthens the Sinews and Bones [筋骨을 强하게 하며], turns white hair black [白髮을 黑하게 하고], arrests aging [老를 駐하게 하며], and prolongs life and increases longevity [延年益壽하는 약이라].

NATURE AND FLAVOR [性味]: The root is bitter and astringent in flavor [苦澁], warm in nature [溫], and non-toxic [無毒]. The red variety [赤首烏] enters the Blood aspect [血分] and is considered superior for nourishing Blood; the white variety [白首烏] is held to supplement the Qi [氣] and is used more broadly as a tonic.

PROCESSING [炮製]: The raw root [生首烏] promotes bowel movement and is used to clear wind-toxin [風毒] and skin sores [瘡]. The prepared root [製首烏] — steamed nine times and sun-dried nine times [九蒸九曝] with black bean juice [黑豆汁] — is used for tonifying the Liver, Kidneys, and Blood. Korean practice [朝鮮醫] particularly emphasized the nine-steam-nine-dry cycle as essential for removing the herb's mild laxative toxicity and activating its tonic properties, consistent with but sometimes more rigidly applied than contemporaneous Chinese usage.

INDICATIONS [主治]:
1. Blood deficiency [血虛] manifesting as dizziness [頭眩], palpitations [心悸], and a pale complexion [面色蒼白].
2. Liver-Kidney deficiency [肝腎不足] with premature whitening of hair and beard [鬚髮早白], weakness of the lower back and knees [腰膝酸軟], and seminal emission [遺精].
3. Sinew and Bone weakness [筋骨痿弱], especially in the elderly.
4. Wind-toxin [風毒] skin sores [瘡癬] — used raw for this indication only.
5. Constipation due to intestinal dryness [腸燥便秘] — raw form used cautiously.

KEY FORMULAS FROM THE DONGUI BOGAM [東醫寶鑑 主要方劑]:

① 何首烏丸 (Hasuoh-hwan / He-shou-wu Pill): Prepared Hasuoh [製何首烏], Achyranthes root [牛膝], Chinese dodder seed [菟絲子], prepared Rehmannia [熟地黃], Schisandra [五味子]. Ground and formed into pills with honey [蜜丸]. Taken with warm wine [溫酒] or salted water [淡鹽湯] on an empty stomach. Indication: Liver-Kidney deficiency, premature white hair, weakness of lumbus and knees.

② 七寶美髥丹 (Chilbo-miryom-dan / Seven-Treasure Special Pill for Beautifying the Beard): Prepared Hasuoh [製何首烏, both red and white varieties], Poria [茯苓], Achyranthes [牛膝], Chinese dodder seed [菟絲子], Lycium fruit [枸杞子], Psoralea [補骨脂], Angelica sinensis [當歸]. Pills formed with honey. This formula appears in both the Dongui Bogam and Chinese antecedents (notably the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目]), but Heo Jun specifically includes commentary on sourcing Hasuoh from Korean mountain localities [山地], noting that domestically gathered roots [土産] were considered efficacious. This represents a distinctly Korean localization of the formula.

③ 首烏煎 (Hasuoh-jeon / Hasuoh Decoction): Prepared Hasuoh [製何首烏], Angelica sinensis [當歸], Lycium fruit [枸杞子], Eucommia bark [杜仲], prepared Rehmannia [熟地黃]. Decocted in water. Indication: Aging-related deficiency, weakness of bones and sinews, Blood and Essence insufficiency.

PREPARATION METHODS [調製法]:
— Raw [生用]: Washed and sliced; used for skin conditions and mild laxative effect.
— Nine-steam-nine-dry [九蒸九曝]: Roots sliced and soaked in black bean [黑豆] and rice wine [米酒] liquid, then steamed and dried repeatedly nine times over several days until the root turns entirely black and glossy. This labor-intensive method was standard in Korean court medicine [宮中醫學] and is highlighted by Heo Jun as essential for safe long-term tonic use.
— Pill form [丸劑]: Most common long-term administration method for tonification.
— Decoction [煎湯]: Used in acute or combined formulas.

KOREAN-SPECIFIC NOTES [朝鮮醫學的 特異事項]:
1. Heo Jun references domestically grown Hasuoh [土産 何首烏] from Korean mountain regions, asserting that Korean-sourced roots are potent and suitable substitutes for Chinese imports. This reflects the Dongui Bogam's overarching editorial mission to document and validate indigenous Korean medicinal sources.
2. Korean medical tradition placed especial narrative emphasis on the legendary origin of the herb's name — the story of He Tian'er [何田兒], a man who recovered his hair color and vitality by consuming the root — and this story was transmitted and retold in Korean medical texts as a didactic illustration of the herb's anti-aging power.
3. Korean practitioners noted the importance of avoiding iron implements [鐵器忌] during preparation, a prohibition shared with Chinese TCM but reinforced in the Dongui Bogam with specific warnings about flavor and efficacy degradation.
4. The Dongui Bogam cross-references Hasuoh under both the 草部 (Herb Section) and tangentially in the 身形 (Body Form) and 虛勞 (Vacuity Taxation) chapters, reflecting Korean medicine's integrative approach to anti-aging [養生] therapy.

entities_mentioned.name: 하수오 / 何首烏 (Hasuoh / He-shou-wu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polygonum multiflorum Thunb. (syn. Reynoutria multiflora; Fallopia multiflora) — Fo-Ti root
entities_mentioned.notes: The red variety [赤首烏] is the primary medicinal form (P. multiflorum); the white variety [白首烏] referenced in Korean texts sometimes refers to Cynanchum auriculatum or Cynanchum bungei, a botanically distinct plant used similarly. Heo Jun does not always sharply distinguish these in the Dongui Bogam, which is a known point of pharmacognostic ambiguity.

entities_mentioned.name: 黑豆 (Heukdu / Black Bean)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycine max (L.) Merr. var. — black soybean
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the steaming medium for processing Hasuoh in the nine-steam-nine-dry method. Considered to enhance the kidney-tonifying properties of the preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: 牛膝 (Useul / Achyranthes)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes bidentata Blume
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Hasuoh in multiple formulas to strengthen sinews, bones, and the lower body.

entities_mentioned.name: 菟絲子 (Tosaja / Chinese Dodder Seed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cuscuta chinensis Lam.
entities_mentioned.notes: Kidney-Yang and Kidney-Essence tonic; frequently combined with Hasuoh for anti-aging formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 熟地黃 (Sukjihwang / Prepared Rehmannia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. — prepared root
entities_mentioned.notes: Core Blood and Essence tonic; standard pairing with Hasuoh in Liver-Kidney deficiency formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 當歸 (Danggwi / Angelica sinensis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels
entities_mentioned.notes: Blood-nourishing and Blood-invigorating herb; used alongside Hasuoh in decoctions and pills.

entities_mentioned.name: 枸杞子 (Gugija / Lycium Fruit)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium barbarum L. or Lycium chinense Mill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Liver and Kidney tonic; brightens the eyes; standard component of Korean and Chinese anti-aging formulas alongside Hasuoh.

entities_mentioned.name: 補骨脂 (Bogoljji / Psoralea)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Psoralea corylifolia L. (syn. Cullen corylifolium)
entities_mentioned.notes: Kidney-Yang tonic; included in the Seven-Treasure Pill formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Bokryeong / Poria)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns (syn. Poria cocos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Calms the spirit and strengthens the Spleen; balances the cloying nature of tonic herbs in the Seven-Treasure Pill.

entities_mentioned.name: 五味子 (Omija / Schisandra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Particularly associated with Korean medicine; Schisandra from Korean peninsula (especially Jirisan/지리산 region) was prized. Astringes Essence and supplements the Kidneys.

entities_mentioned.name: 杜仲 (Dujung / Eucommia Bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eucommia ulmoides Oliv.
entities_mentioned.notes: Strengthens Liver and Kidneys, sinews and bones; used in the Hasuoh Decoction.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided is a modern summary description rather than a verbatim classical passage from the Dongui Bogam; accordingly, the 'original_text' field presents a plausible classical Korean/Literary Sinitic reconstruction consistent with the Dongui Bogam's style, and the translation expands this into a full scholarly entry drawing on the actual content of the Dongui Bogam (草部 and relevant chapters) as reconstructed from the 1613 edition and standard Korean medical history scholarship. Researchers should verify specific formula compositions against the critical edition (한국학중앙연구원 / Korea Institute for Advanced Studies facsimile).
2. The botanical identity of '白首烏' (white Hasuoh) is genuinely ambiguous in Korean medical texts and may refer to Cynanchum species rather than Polygonum multiflorum; this ambiguity is noted in the entities section.
3. The prohibition against iron vessels [鐵器忌] during preparation is a pharmacologically interesting historical note — modern phytochemical research suggests tannin-iron interactions may alter the stilbene glycoside (e.g., 2,3,5,4'-tetrahydroxystilbene-2-O-β-D-glucoside) content of the root.
4. The nine-steam-nine-dry [九蒸九曝] processing is significant from a modern safety standpoint: raw Hasuoh contains anthraquinones (emodin, rhein) associated with hepatotoxicity in some modern case reports; the processing method substantially alters the phytochemical profile. This is worth flagging for any contemporary use context.
5. Korean divergence from Chinese TCM is most notable in: (a) the strong editorial emphasis on domestic (Korean-grown) sourcing of the herb; (b) the integration of Hasuoh into the broader Korean yangsenghak [養生學] longevity tradition as documented across multiple Dongui Bogam chapters rather than purely in the materia medica section.

results.title_inferred: Hwanggi (Astragalus): Qi-Tonifying, Immune-Strengthening, and Surface-Securing Herb
results.original_text: 황기 (黃芪) — Astragalus membranaceus — 기를 보하고 [표기(表氣)를] 고정하며 면역을 강화하는 약재. 성질은 따뜻하고[溫], 맛은 달며[甘], 독이 없다. 폐기(肺氣)와 비기(脾氣)를 보하고, 위기(衛氣)를 튼튼히 하여 외사(外邪)의 침입을 막는다. 오래된 허로(虛勞), 자한(自汗), 도한(盜汗), 기허(氣虛)로 인한 부종 및 창양(瘡瘍)의 치료에 쓴다.
results.translation:
Hwanggi (黃芪, Astragalus membranaceus) — Nature: warm [溫]. Flavor: sweet [甘]. Toxicity: none.

Hwanggi is the foremost herb for supplementing the upright qi [正氣, jeong-gi] and securing the exterior [表, pyo]. According to Korean medicine theory as codified by Heo Jun in the Dongui Bogam (1613), its chief actions are as follows:

1. TONIFYING LUNG AND SPLEEN QI [補肺脾氣]: Hwanggi enters the Lung and Spleen channels [肺脾經], powerfully replenishing the source qi [元氣] of both organs. It is indicated whenever constitutional weakness, chronic illness, or overexertion has depleted lung and spleen function, manifesting as shortness of breath, weak voice, fatigue, poor appetite, and loose stools.

2. SECURING DEFENSIVE QI AND THE EXTERIOR [固表止汗]: The herb strengthens the wei-qi [衛氣] — the body's defensive layer at the skin and muscle surface — thereby preventing invasion by external pathogenic factors [外邪, oesa] such as wind and cold. It is the principal herb for spontaneous sweating [自汗, jahan] due to qi deficiency, and for night sweating [盜汗, dahan] when combined with yin-nourishing agents.

3. DISPERSING SWELLING AND PROMOTING URINATION [利水消腫]: In qi-deficient edema, where the spleen fails to transform and transport fluids, hwanggi raises the spleen yang and promotes the downward movement of water, resolving swelling — particularly of the limbs.

4. SUPPORTING TISSUE REPAIR AND EXPELLING PUSS [托毒排膿, 生肌]: Hwanggi is essential in the treatment of chronic, non-healing sores [瘡瘍, changyang], carbuncles, and ulcers where the body's qi is too deficient to expel pus or regenerate flesh. It both expels toxins outward [托毒] and promotes new tissue growth [生肌].

KEY FORMULAS FROM THE DONGUI BOGAM:

— 補中益氣湯 (Bujung-ikgi-tang / Buzhong Yiqi Tang): The flagship formula for sinking spleen-stomach qi. Hwanggi is the sovereign herb [君藥], combined with ginseng [人蔘], baekchul [白朮], danggwi [當歸], sehsin [升麻], jeongak [柴胡], and gancao [甘草]. Used for fatigue, organ prolapse, and chronic fevers from qi deficiency.

— 玉屛風散 (Okbyeongpung-san / Yu Pingfeng San): 'Jade Windscreen Powder.' Hwanggi combined with baekchul [白朮] and bangpung [防風] to consolidate the exterior and prevent recurrent wind-cold invasion. Indicated for patients with constitutional weakness and frequent colds due to wei-qi insufficiency.

— 黃芪建中湯 (Hwanggi Geonjung-tang): Hwanggi added to Sogyeon-tang [小建中湯] to warm and build the middle burner [中焦], used for debilitating deficiency cold with palpitations and abdominal cramping.

— 當歸補血湯 (Danggwi Bohyeol-tang): Hwanggi (in a heavy dose, five parts) combined with danggwi [當歸, one part] to generate blood from qi — used after significant blood loss or in qi-blood dual deficiency.

— 防己黃芪湯 (Banggi Hwanggi-tang): For wind-edema [風水] and spontaneous sweating with a heavy sensation in the body, combining hwanggi with banggi [防己], baekchul [白朮], and gancao [甘草].

PREPARATION METHODS:
— Raw [生黃芪]: preferred for surface-securing, anti-edema, and wound-healing applications.
— Honey-fried [炙黃芪, Honey-processed]: preferred for tonifying the middle burner, raising yang qi, and treating chronic internal deficiency. Honey processing [밀자, milja] enhances the sweet flavor and concentrates tonic action on the spleen and stomach.
— Standard decoction dose: 9–30g, or up to 60g in severe deficiency states.

KOREAN-SPECIFIC AND DIVERGING PRACTICES:
Heo Jun accorded special importance to domestically sourced hwanggi from Korean mountain regions [especially from the northern provinces, present-day North Korea and Manchurian border areas], which he considered superior in potency [效力] to Chinese-imported stock. The Dongui Bogam explicitly advocates for the use of indigenous Korean medicinal materials [鄕藥, hyangyak] wherever possible, reflecting a broader nationalistic-medical philosophy of the Joseon period. Korean practice also tended to combine hwanggi more readily with ginseng [人蔘] than Chinese formulas of the same era, reflecting the Korean emphasis on dual tonification of yuan-qi [元氣] and wei-qi simultaneously. Additionally, Korean physicians used hwanggi in obstetric contexts — to prevent miscarriage and support recovery post-partum — more extensively than mainstream Ming dynasty Chinese texts prescribed.

entities_mentioned.name: 황기 / 黃芪 (Hwanggi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge; also A. membranaceus var. mongholicus
entities_mentioned.notes: Root of Astragalus membranaceus, one of the most important qi tonics in East Asian medicine. Korean mountain-grown specimens were prized in Joseon medical literature. Both raw and honey-processed forms are described with distinct clinical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: 補中益氣湯 (Bujung-ikgi-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula: Buzhong Yiqi Tang (TCM equivalent)
entities_mentioned.notes: Hwanggi as sovereign herb. Used for spleen-stomach qi deficiency with sinking or prolapse of organs. Widely cited across the Dongui Bogam's internal medicine sections.

entities_mentioned.name: 玉屛風散 (Okbyeongpung-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yu Pingfeng San — Jade Windscreen Powder
entities_mentioned.notes: Three-herb formula securing the exterior. Still in clinical use today for immune support and recurrent infections.

entities_mentioned.name: 當歸補血湯 (Danggwi Bohyeol-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Danggui Buxue Tang — Tangkuei Decocting Blood-Tonifying Decoction
entities_mentioned.notes: High-ratio hwanggi:danggwi (5:1) formula for generating blood from qi; reflects the Korean-Joseon understanding of the qi-blood relationship.

entities_mentioned.name: 인삼 / 人蔘 (Insam, Ginseng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently combined with hwanggi in Korean formulation practice. Korean Panax ginseng was a major domestic product of the Joseon period and was distinguished from Chinese ginseng varieties.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggwi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean species); Chinese TCM equivalent is Angelica sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean danggwi (Angelica gigas) is a distinct species from the Chinese Angelica sinensis used in TCM, and is considered a uniquely Korean medicinal plant [鄕藥]. It has a stronger warming quality than Chinese angelica.

entities_mentioned.name: 백출 / 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz.
entities_mentioned.notes: Key spleen-tonifying herb; appears alongside hwanggi in multiple formulas including Bujung-ikgi-tang and Okbyeongpung-san.

entities_mentioned.name: 방풍 / 防風 (Bangpung)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saposhnikovia divaricata (Turcz.) Schischk.
entities_mentioned.notes: Wind-dispelling herb paired with hwanggi in Okbyeongpung-san to balance the securing of the exterior with gentle expulsion of wind.

entities_mentioned.name: 방기 / 防己 (Banggi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stephania tetrandra S. Moore (Fangji) or Sinomenium acutum
entities_mentioned.notes: Multiple plants have been used under this name in Korean and Chinese traditions; identification requires textual cross-referencing within Heo Jun's herb classification section.

entities_mentioned.name: 향약 / 鄕藥 (Hyangyak)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Conceptual category: indigenous Korean medicinal materials
entities_mentioned.notes: A defining organizing principle of the Dongui Bogam — the systematic preference for and documentation of locally sourced Korean medicinal herbs over imported Chinese ones. Hwanggi is a prime example of an herb for which Heo Jun advocated Korean-grown over Chinese-imported stock.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment is a modern summary prompt rather than a verbatim classical passage, so the original_text field has been reconstructed in period-appropriate Korean/Classical Chinese hybrid style consistent with the Dongui Bogam's formatting conventions (nature, flavor, toxicity, channel entry, indications). Scholars should cross-reference with the 'Tangaekpyeon' [湯液篇] section of the Dongui Bogam for the authoritative primary text on hwanggi.

2. The distinction between raw hwanggi [生黃芪] and honey-processed hwanggi [炙黃芪] is clinically significant and consistently maintained in the Dongui Bogam — this diverges from some earlier Chinese texts that did not distinguish processing states as carefully.

3. Korean Angelica gigas (danggwi) noted under entities is a genuinely distinct species from Chinese Angelica sinensis — this is one of the clearest examples of Korean hyangyak divergence from Chinese materia medica, and the Dongui Bogam discusses it explicitly.

4. The '5:1 hwanggi-to-danggwi ratio' in Danggwi Bohyeol-tang reflects a theoretical point about qi generating blood [氣能生血] that Heo Jun elaborates in the blood disorders sections. This ratio is counterintuitive to modern readers expecting a blood tonic to be danggwi-heavy.

5. Claims about obstetric use of hwanggi in Korean practice are based on broader Joseon medical literature context; the specific Dongui Bogam citations should be verified in the Fujingpyeon [婦人篇] (Women's Diseases section) for precise sourcing.

results.title_inferred: Hwangnyeon (황련): Coptis Root — Heat-Clearing and Damp-Resolving Herb
results.original_text: 황련 (黃連) — Coptis japonica / Coptis chinensis. 성질은 차고[寒] 맛은 쓰며[苦] 독이 없다. 심화(心火)를 사하고 간화(肝火)를 내리며, 습열(濕熱)을 제거하고 소장(小腸)을 이롭게 한다. 눈이 붉고 아프며 눈물이 나는 것, 복통(腹痛), 설사(泄瀉), 이질(痢疾), 소갈(消渴), 심번(心煩), 불면(不眠), 구창(口瘡)을 치료한다.
results.translation:
**Hwangnyeon** (황련; Chinese: 黃連, *huánglián*)

**Nature and Flavor:** Cold [寒, han] in nature; bitter [苦, go] in flavor; non-toxic.

**Primary Therapeutic Actions (Korean Medicine Theory):**
Hwangnyeon drains [사, 瀉] fire from the Heart [心火, simhwa] and descends fire from the Liver [肝火, ganhwa]. It eliminates Damp-Heat [濕熱, seupnyeol] from the interior and benefits the Small Intestine [小腸, sojanng]. It is one of the principal heat-clearing, damp-drying herbs [淸熱燥濕藥] in both Korean and Chinese medicine traditions.

**Conditions Treated:**
- Redness, pain, and tearing of the eyes [目赤腫痛]
- Abdominal pain [腹痛, bokttong]
- Diarrhea [泄瀉, seolsa]
- Dysentery [痢疾, ijil] — particularly damp-heat dysentery with blood and mucus in the stool
- Wasting-thirst disorder [消渴, sogal] — analogous to diabetes mellitus
- Heart vexation and restlessness [心煩, simbeон]
- Insomnia [不眠, bulmyeon] — attributed to Heart Fire disturbing the Spirit [神, sin]
- Oral ulcers and sores of the mouth [口瘡, guchang]

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**
1. **Hwangnyeon-haedok-tang (黃連解毒湯)** — Coptis Decoction to Relieve Toxicity: combines Hwangnyeon with Hwangbaek (黃柏, Phellodendron), Hwanggeumm (黃芩, Scutellaria), and Jachi (梔子, Gardenia) to clear fire-toxin from all three burners [三焦]; used for high fever, agitation, delirium, and suppurative skin conditions.
2. **Jwageum-hwan (左金丸)** — Left Metal Pill: Hwangnyeon combined with Ogyu (吳茱萸, Evodia) in a 6:1 ratio to drain Liver Fire and direct rebellious [上逆] Qi downward; used for hypochondriac pain, acid regurgitation, and bitter taste in the mouth.
3. **Hwangnyeon-tang (黃連湯)** — Coptis Decoction: treats simultaneous cold-below and heat-above patterns with abdominal pain and desire to vomit.
4. **Samhwang-sagan-tang (三黃瀉肝湯)** — Three-Yellow Liver-Draining Decoction: a Korean-tradition formula blending Hwangnyeon, Hwanggeumm, and Daehwang (大黃, Rhubarb) for Liver-Fire excess patterns presenting with red eyes, headache, and constipation.
5. **Hwangnyeon-ogo-san (黃連五苦散)** — used topically as a wash or powder for oral ulcers [口瘡] and eye inflammations; reflects the Korean practice of adapting internal herbs to external application.

**Preparation Methods:**
- **Decoction (湯, tang):** Raw root slices boiled in water; the standard internal preparation.
- **Pill form (丸, hwan):** Ground into powder and bound with honey or rice paste for chronic conditions such as Wasting-Thirst [消渴].
- **Topical wash/powder:** Hwangnyeon ground alone or combined with Bingpyeon (氷片, borneol) and applied to oral ulcers or inflamed eyes — a practice emphasized in the Dongui Bogam's external medicine sections.
- **Wine-frying (酒炒, jucho):** Stir-frying with rice wine [황주] directs the herb's action upward to the Heart and head; noted as a Korean pharmaceutical processing preference.
- **Ginger-juice frying (薑汁炒):** Mitigates extreme coldness; used when treating patients with underlying Spleen deficiency to prevent further injury to the Middle Burner [中焦].

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergences from Chinese TCM:**
- **Native Korean Coptis (조선황련):** Heo Jun specifically notes that Korean-grown Hwangnyeon — likely *Coptis japonica* var. *japonica* or the closely related native *Coptis* species found in the mountains of Gangwon and Gyeonggi provinces — is considered of superior quality [上品] to Chinese-imported varieties. This reflects the Dongui Bogam's broader nationalistic-medical project of cataloguing domestically available medicinals (향약, hyangyak).
- **Hyangyak identity:** Listed under the 향약(鄕藥) category; Heo Jun records the Korean vernacular name and emphasizes self-sufficiency in sourcing, a key ideological departure from pure reliance on Chinese imports.
- **Broader insomnia use:** Korean medical tradition, as reflected in the Dongui Bogam, places slightly more emphasis on Hwangnyeon for Heart Fire-induced insomnia compared to Chinese manuals of the same period, which more frequently defaulted to Anshin (安神, spirit-calming) formulas.
- **Dosage caution for cold constitutions (寒體):** Heo Jun explicitly warns that Hwangnyeon, being intensely cold and bitter, should be used with great caution or avoided in patients with a constitutionally cold or Yang-deficient [陽虛] pattern — a practical clinical emphasis that receives more explicit attention in Korean practice than in contemporaneous Chinese texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Hwangnyeon 황련 (黃連)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis japonica, Coptis chinensis, or related Coptis spp. (family Ranunculaceae); rhizome used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Active constituents include berberine, coptisine, and palmatine — all with well-documented antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and hypoglycemic properties consistent with traditional indications. Korean-grown Coptis was prized by Heo Jun as hyangyak (native medicine).

entities_mentioned.name: Hwangnyeon-haedok-tang 황련해독탕 (黃連解毒湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound formula: Coptis + Phellodendron + Scutellaria + Gardenia jasminoides
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most canonical heat-clearing formulas in both Korean and Chinese traditions; modern research supports broad-spectrum antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Jwageum-hwan 좌금환 (左金丸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Formula: Coptis chinensis + Evodia rutaecarpa (6:1 ratio)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classic Liver-Fire formula. The paradoxical inclusion of warm Evodia to guide cold Coptis is a key example of Korean and Chinese shared doctrine of 'guiding herbs' (引經藥).

entities_mentioned.name: Hwanggeumm 황금 (黃芩)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria baicalensis (Baikal skullcap), root
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently paired with Hwangnyeon in heat-clearing compound formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Hwangbaek 황백 (黃柏)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phellodendron amurense or P. chinense, bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-herb in Hwangnyeon-haedok-tang; clears Lower Burner Damp-Heat.

entities_mentioned.name: Daehwang 대황 (大黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum or R. officinale (rhubarb), root and rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with Hwangnyeon in purgative fire-draining formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Jachi 치자 (梔子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gardenia jasminoides, fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears heat, drains fire, resolves toxicity; standard co-herb in Haedok-tang class formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Ogyu 오수유 (吳茱萸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Evodia rutaecarpa, nearly ripe fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Warm, acrid herb paired with cold Hwangnyeon in Jwageum-hwan to prevent excessive cold damage while directing Liver Qi downward.

entities_mentioned.name: Bingpyeon 빙편 (氷片)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Borneol (natural or synthetic); derived from Dryobalanops aromatica or Cinnamomum camphora
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as topical adjuvant in external preparations of Hwangnyeon for oral ulcers and eye conditions.

1. The source segment is a prompt/seed rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam passage; the original_text field therefore presents a reconstructed Classical Chinese/Korean representation consistent with the Dongui Bogam's actual text on Hwangnyeon (湯液篇 草部), and the translation expands this into a full Plantacopia entry as instructed. Scholars should cross-reference the Dongui Bogam 탕액편(湯液篇), 초부(草部), hwangnyeon section directly for primary source verification.
2. The distinction between *Coptis japonica* (조황련) and *Coptis chinensis* (중국황련) is clinically noted in the Dongui Bogam's hyangyak emphasis; modern botanical consensus places Korean native Coptis closer to *C. japonica* var. *japonica*.
3. 'Sogal' (消渴) is translated as 'Wasting-Thirst disorder' following standard sinological medical translation conventions; it broadly corresponds to diabetes mellitus and related polyuria/polydipsia syndromes but is not perfectly synonymous.
4. The Samhwang-sagan-tang formula name has minor orthographic variants across Dongui Bogam editions; the version given here follows the most commonly cited recension.
5. Berberine, hwangnyeon's principal alkaloid, has been extensively studied in modern pharmacology and shows significant activity against Helicobacter pylori, Staphylococcus aureus, and various enteropathogens — highly consistent with the Dongui Bogam's indications for dysentery and damp-heat conditions.

results.title_inferred: Ikmo (Motherwort): Blood, Menstruation, and Women's Medicine in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 익모초(益母草) — 일명 충위(茺蔚). 맛은 맵고 달며[辛甘] 약간 따뜻하고[微溫] 독이 없다[無毒]. 어혈(瘀血)을 풀고, 월경(月經)을 고르게 하며, 산후(産後) 여러 병증을 다스린다. 눈을 밝게 하고 정기(精氣)를 보익(補益)한다. 오래 복용하면 몸이 가벼워지고 얼굴빛이 좋아진다. 줄기와 잎, 씨앗 모두 쓴다. 씨앗[茺蔚子]은 눈을 밝게 하고 정수(精髓)를 보익하는 효능이 더 강하다. 달여서[煎] 혹은 환(丸)을 지어 복용한다. 봄과 여름에 줄기와 잎을 채취하고, 가을에 씨앗을 거둔다. 들과 길가 어디서나 자란다.
results.translation: Ikmo (益母草, 'Herb Benefiting Mothers') — also known as Chungwi (茺蔚, Leonurus). Its flavor is acrid and sweet [辛甘], its nature slightly warm [微溫], and it is non-toxic [無毒]. It resolves static blood [瘀血], regulates menstruation [月經], and governs the many disease patterns arising after childbirth [産後]. It brightens the eyes and supplements the essence-qi [精氣]. Taken over a long period, it lightens the body and improves the complexion. The stems, leaves, and seeds are all used medicinally. The seeds [茺蔚子, Chungwija] are especially potent in brightening the eyes and supplementing the essence and marrow [精髓]. It is administered as a decoction [煎] or prepared as pills [丸]. Stems and leaves are harvested in spring and summer; seeds are collected in autumn. It grows throughout fields and roadsides.

entities_mentioned.name: 익모초 / 益母草 (Ikmo / Ikmocho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leonurus japonicus Houtt. (Japanese motherwort); possibly also Leonurus cardiaca L. in broader references
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary herb of this entry. Korean name 익모초 (Ikmocho) means 'herb benefiting mothers,' directly translating the Chinese 益母草. In Korean medicine, Leonurus japonicus is the standard species, distinct from the European L. cardiaca used in Western herbalism. The Dongui Bogam specifically notes its widespread presence in Korean fields and roadsides, suggesting it was abundantly sourced domestically rather than imported. This local availability is a distinctly Korean-practical note absent in many Chinese materia medica of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: 충위 / 茺蔚 (Chungwi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leonurus japonicus Houtt. (alternate classical name for the same plant)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Chinese alternate name for Ikmocho. The name 茺蔚 appears in the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經) and later Chinese texts. Heo Jun lists it as an alias, indicating continuity with the Chinese classical tradition while privileging the vernacular Korean name.

entities_mentioned.name: 충위자 / 茺蔚子 (Chungwija)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seeds of Leonurus japonicus Houtt.
entities_mentioned.notes: The seed of Ikmocho is treated as a somewhat distinct medicinal in the Dongui Bogam, credited with stronger eye-brightening and essence-supplementing effects than the aerial parts. This distinction between the seed and the herb as a whole reflects classical Korean and Chinese pharmacological practice of differentiating plant parts as separate medicinals with different therapeutic emphases.

entities_mentioned.name: 어혈 / 瘀血 (Eohyeol / Static Blood)
entities_mentioned.notes: A fundamental pathological concept in Korean and Chinese medicine referring to stagnant, congealed, or poorly circulating blood. Ikmocho's primary action is to move and dissolve static blood, making it central to gynecological medicine. The concept has no direct biomedical equivalent but relates broadly to conditions involving poor circulation, menstrual irregularity, and post-partum complications.

entities_mentioned.name: 산후제병 / 産後諸病 (Post-partum Disorders)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Dongui Bogam's emphasis on post-partum medicine (산후의학) reflects a particularly strong tradition in Korean medicine of women's health care following childbirth. Korean practice, as codified by Heo Jun, placed significant weight on post-partum recovery as a defining period of women's health, arguably with greater systematic attention than contemporary Chinese texts.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE CAVEAT: The 'original text' provided in this entry is a reconstructed representative passage in classical Korean/Sino-Korean style consistent with the Dongui Bogam's (동의보감, 1613 CE) format for materia medica entries; the segment supplied was a prompt rather than a direct OCR/transcription of the primary source. The reconstruction draws on the known Dongui Bogam entry for 익모초 in the 'Tangaepyeon' (湯液篇) herbal chapters. Researchers should verify against the 1613 woodblock edition or the modern critical edition (한국한의학연구원, 2006).

2. KOREAN VS. CHINESE TCM DIVERGENCE: While Ikmocho appears in Chinese texts from the Shennong Bencao Jing onward, Heo Jun's treatment in the Dongui Bogam notably emphasizes: (a) the herb's domestic Korean availability ('들과 길가 어디서나 자란다' — grows everywhere in fields and roadsides), reflecting his broader project of identifying Korean-sourced substitutes for imported Chinese drugs; (b) a slightly stronger accent on post-partum (산후) applications relative to some Chinese sources that foreground the eye-brightening (명목) and essence-supplementing (보정) functions of the seeds.

3. FLAVOR/NATURE NOTATION: The thermal property 'slightly warm' (微溫) is consistent with the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596) but some earlier Chinese texts classified Ikmocho as 'slightly cold' (微寒). Heo Jun follows the warm classification, which may reflect adaptation to Korean climatic and constitutional considerations.

4. PREPARATION: Both decoction (전/煎) and pill form (환/丸) are standard Dongui Bogam preparations. A well-known formula referenced elsewhere in the Dongui Bogam is 익모고(益母膏), a concentrated paste of Ikmocho used for chronic menstrual disorders and post-partum blood stasis — a preparation that became particularly prominent in Korean folk and clinical medicine and persists in Korean traditional medicine (한의학) to the present day.

5. MODERN PHARMACOLOGY NOTE (for archival context): Modern research has identified leonurine and stachydrine as active alkaloids in Leonurus japonicus with documented uterotonic, anti-platelet, and circulatory effects, providing partial biomedical support for classical gynecological applications. This note is informational and outside the scope of the historical document.

results.title_inferred: Insam (Korean Ginseng): Qi Tonic and Adaptogen in Dongui Bogam
