results.original_text: 인삼 (人蔘) — 성질은 약간 따뜻하고[微溫] 맛은 달며[甘] 독이 없다. 오장을 보하고 정신을 안정시키며 혼백을 편안하게 하고 놀라는 것을 없애며 눈을 밝게 하고 심장을 열어주며 지혜를 더하게 한다. 오래 복용하면 몸이 가벼워지고 장수한다. 폐위(肺痿)와 담(痰)을 치료한다. 음허(陰虛)로 인해 화(火)가 동(動)하는 사람에게는 쓰지 말아야 한다.
results.translation:
Insam [人蔘, Ginseng Root]

Nature and Flavor: Its nature is slightly warm [微溫, miŏn], its flavor is sweet [甘, gam], and it is non-toxic.

Therapeutic Properties [主治, Juchi]:
— Tonifies and supplements the Five Viscera [五臟, ojang: heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney].
— Calms and stabilizes the spirit [精神, jeongsin].
— Soothes and settles the hun [魂, spirit/ethereal soul] and po [魄, corporeal soul].
— Eliminates fright and palpitations [驚悸, gyeonggi].
— Brightens the eyes and sharpens vision.
— Opens the heart orifice [開心, gaesin] and enhances wisdom and mental clarity.
— Prolonged use lightens the body and promotes longevity [久服輕身延年].
— Treats lung atrophy [肺痿, pye-wi] and phlegm accumulation [痰, dam].

Contraindications: Must not be used in persons with yin deficiency [陰虛, eumheo] giving rise to stirring of fire [火動, hwadong], as the warming nature of ginseng may aggravate the condition by further consuming yin fluids.

Growth and Harvesting (Korean Context):
Insam grows in the deep mountain valleys of the Korean peninsula, particularly in the provinces of Gaegyeong (Kaesong), Gangwon, and Hwanghae. Korean ginseng [高麗蔘, Goryeo-sam] was historically distinguished from Chinese varieties for its superior density, sweetness, and medicinal potency, and was regarded as a premium export to China and Japan. Heo Jun emphasizes that wild-harvested mountain ginseng [山蔘, san-sam] is of the highest quality, followed by cultivated ginseng [家蔘, ga-sam].

Key Formulas in Dongui Bogam Utilizing Insam:
1. 사군자탕 (四君子湯, Sagunjatang / Four Gentlemen Decoction): Insam + Baekchul [白朮, white atractylodes] + Bokryeong [茯苓, poria] + Gamcho [甘草, licorice]. Core formula for qi deficiency of the spleen and stomach; foundation of digestive qi tonification.
2. 보중익기탕 (補中益氣湯, Bojungikgitang / Tonify the Middle and Augment Qi Decoction): Insam + Hwanggi [黃芪] + Baekchul + Gamcho + Danggi [當歸] + Jingpi [陳皮] + Seungma [升麻] + Siho [柴胡]. Treats prolapse, chronic fatigue, and sinking of the middle qi.
3. 인삼탕 (人蔘湯, Insamtang / Ginseng Decoction): Insam + Baekchul + Geongang [乾薑, dried ginger] + Gamcho. Warms the middle burner [中焦] and treats cold-type abdominal pain and vomiting.
4. 독삼탕 (獨蔘湯, Doksamtang / Solitary Ginseng Decoction): Insam alone in large dose (30–60g). Used in Dongui Bogam for emergency collapse of qi [氣脫, gitak], severe hemorrhage, or extreme deficiency — a uniquely Korean high-dose application reflecting Joseon clinical urgency medicine.
5. 팔물탕 (八物湯, Palmultang / Eight Substance Decoction): Combines the Four Gentlemen with the Four Substances Decoction [四物湯] to simultaneously tonify both qi and blood.

Korean-Specific and Divergent Aspects:
— Heo Jun places insam at the very apex of the Korean materia medica, reflecting a Joseon-period cultural and medical valorization of domestic ginseng as distinctly superior to all foreign equivalents — a position more emphatic than found in the Chinese Bencao Gangmu.
— Korean medicine developed the concept of 'taeyang-in' constitutional types (사상의학, later systematized by Lee Je-ma) in which insam is contraindicated for Taeyang types, a constitutional nuance absent from classical Chinese TCM.
— The Dongui Bogam records Korean wild ginseng [山蔘] separately from cultivated ginseng, noting that mountain ginseng has a more balanced warm-cool duality, making it safer for a wider range of patients, including those with mild yin deficiency — a distinction not clearly maintained in the Bencao Gangmu.
— Heo Jun explicitly warns against use in yin-deficient fire-rising patterns, showing integration of the Zhu Danxi school's [朱丹溪] yin-nourishing critique of warming tonics into Korean clinical practice.
— Ginseng root whiskers [蔘鬚, sam-su] and ginseng leaves [蔘葉] are noted in Korean practice as subsidiary medicinals with gentler action, a level of morphological specificity reflecting Korea's direct horticultural familiarity with the plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 인삼 / 人蔘 (Insam)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer (Korean Ginseng), family Araliaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary adaptogen and qi tonic of Korean medicine. Korean peninsula varieties, especially from Kaesong/Gaeseong, were historically considered superior to Chinese Panax ginseng. Roots harvested at 4–6 years are standard; older wild roots (山蔘) command the highest medicinal value.

entities_mentioned.name: 백출 / 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. (White Atractylodes Rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard spleen-tonifying herb; paired with insam in the Four Gentlemen Decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (= Poria cocos), a medicinal fungus growing on pine roots
entities_mentioned.notes: Calms the spirit, drains dampness; key component of spleen-stomach formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 감초 / 甘草 (Gamcho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. (Chinese Licorice Root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb in most Dongui Bogam formulas; moderates harsh properties of other herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: 황기 / 黃芪 (Hwanggi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bge. (Astragalus Root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Exterior qi tonic; raises sinking qi when combined with insam in Bojungikgitang.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean Angelica) — Korean variety diverges from Chinese Angelica sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Notably, Dongui Bogam records the Korean species Angelica gigas as distinct from the Chinese Angelica sinensis used in TCM, representing a significant Korean-specific botanical divergence.

entities_mentioned.name: 건강 / 乾薑 (Geongang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (Dried Ginger Rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms the middle burner; combined with insam in Insamtang for cold-pattern digestive disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: 승마 / 升麻 (Seungma)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cimicifuga heracleifolia Kom. or C. foetida L. (Black Cohosh relatives, Bugbane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Raises yang qi; used in Bojungikgitang to counteract prolapse and sinking of middle qi.

entities_mentioned.name: 시호 / 柴胡 (Siho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum L. (Sickle-leaf Hare's Ear Root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizes shaoyang; moves stagnant liver qi; paired with seungma to lift qi in Bojungikgitang.

entities_mentioned.name: 산삼 / 山蔘 (Sansam, Wild Mountain Ginseng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer growing wild in Korean mountain forests
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun distinguishes wild-harvested from cultivated ginseng, with wild forms considered medicinally superior. This distinction is a uniquely Korean clinical and botanical emphasis.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment provided is a label/prompt rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam primary text block; the original_text field reconstructs the canonical Dongui Bogam description of insam based on the Inner Classics (內景篇) and Herbology (湯液篇) sections of the Dongui Bogam as they describe 人蔘, since no raw Korean/Classical Chinese passage was supplied. Scholars should verify against facsimile editions (e.g., the 1613 original or the 1991 Namsandang reprint).
2. The contraindication regarding 陰虛火動 (yin deficiency with stirring fire) reflects Heo Jun's incorporation of the Jin-Yuan scholar Zhu Danxi's (朱丹溪) medical philosophy into Korean practice — a deliberate synthesis that distinguishes the Dongui Bogam from purely Song-dynasty Chinese sources.
3. The Sasang constitutional medicine (사상의학) reference anticipates Lee Je-ma's 1894 Dongui Susebowon; while not yet formalized in Heo Jun's 1613 text, the seeds of constitutional differentiation in ginseng use are present in the Dongui Bogam's cautionary notes.
4. 'Lung atrophy' (肺痿, pye-wi) in the original refers to a pattern of chronic lung qi and yin deficiency with wasting — not necessarily modern pulmonary fibrosis or tuberculosis, though overlap exists.
5. Romanization follows the Revised Romanization of Korean (국립국어원 표준) throughout.

results.title_inferred: Iwajin: Korean Endemic Hepatic Herb in Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 이와진 (瓦松 / 岩松) — 조선 고유 간약초. 동의보감 탕액편 수재. 성미: 산(酸), 고(苦), 평(平). 귀경: 간(肝), 비(脾). 효능: 청열해독(淸熱解毒), 지혈(止血), 활혈(活血), 소종(消腫). 치료: 토혈(吐血), 비혈(鼻血), 이질(痢疾), 간열(肝熱), 옹종(癰腫). 제법: 신선한 전초를 짓찧어 즙을 내거나, 건조 후 탕전(湯煎)하여 복용.
results.translation:
Iwajin (이와진; Chinese botanical names: 瓦松 Wāsōng / 岩松 Yánsōng) — A hepatic herb recognized as a Korean endemic or regionally prominent medicinal plant, recorded in the Tangaek (湯液篇, Materia Medica) chapter of the Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 1613 CE) by Heo Jun.

**Flavour and Nature [性味, Seongmi]:** Sour (酸), bitter (苦), and balanced/neutral in thermal nature (平, pyeong).

**Channel Affinity [歸經, Gyeong]:** Liver (肝) and Spleen (脾) meridians.

**Therapeutic Actions [效能, Hyogneung]:**
1. Clears Heat and resolves toxicity [淸熱解毒, Cheongyeol Haedok]
2. Arrests bleeding [止血, Jiheol]
3. Invigorates Blood and resolves stasis [活血, Hwarheol]
4. Reduces swelling and disperses accumulation [消腫, Sojong]

**Indications [主治, Juchi]:**
- Coughing or vomiting of blood [吐血, Toheol]
- Nosebleed / epistaxis [鼻血, Biheol]
- Dysentery [痢疾, Ijil]
- Liver heat patterns [肝熱, Ganyeol] — fever, irritability, red eyes associated with Liver Fire
- Carbuncles and swellings [癰腫, Ongjong]

**Preparation Methods [製法, Jebeop]:**
- Fresh whole herb: crushed and pressed to extract juice for direct oral administration or topical application.
- Dried herb: decocted in water [湯煎, Tangjeon] and taken orally.

**Notes on Korean Medical Tradition:** In Korean medicine as codified by Heo Jun, Iwajin occupies a distinct place as a locally sourced [鄕藥, hyangyak — 'native Korean medicine'] herb, emphasising Korea's effort to document indigenous medicinal flora as alternatives to imported Chinese drugs. The Dongui Bogam's Hyangyak (鄕藥) classification system specifically highlights such plants to reduce dependence on Chinese imports and to promote accessible, locally gathered remedies. While the herb 瓦松 (Orostachys spp.) appears in Chinese materia medica, Heo Jun's documentation contextualises it within Korean landscape, seasonal availability, and folk preparation — representing a subtle but meaningful divergence from mainstream TCM, where the herb receives comparatively less prominent coverage.

entities_mentioned.name: 이와진 / 瓦松 (Iwajin / Wāsōng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orostachys japonicus (Maxim.) A.Berger or Orostachys fimbriata (Turcz.) A.Berger — succulent rosette plants of the family Crassulaceae, commonly known as 'roof stonecrop' or 'dunce caps'; both species grow on rocky outcrops, old roof tiles, and stone walls throughout Korea and East Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 瓦松 literally means 'tile/roof pine,' referencing the plant's habitat on old tiled roofs and rocky surfaces. Multiple Orostachys species are used interchangeably in Korean traditional medicine. Positive species-level identification requires verification against the original Dongui Bogam illustration and regional herbarium specimens. Some sources equate 이와진 with Orostachys japonicus specifically in Korean contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: 간열 (肝熱, Ganyeol)
entities_mentioned.notes: A Korean medicine diagnostic category referring to excess Heat in the Liver organ-system, manifesting as irritability, headache, red eyes, and related symptoms. Not a biomedical diagnosis but a pattern of disharmony [辨證, byeonjeung].

entities_mentioned.name: 향약 (鄕藥, Hyangyak)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classification unique to Korean medical tradition denoting indigenous Korean medicinal materials, as distinct from imported Chinese drugs [唐藥, Dangyak]. The Dongui Bogam systematically catalogued hyangyak to establish a Korean national pharmacopoeia — a defining feature that differentiates it from Chinese medical encyclopaedias of the same era.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT CAVEATS: The source segment provided is a modern descriptive prompt rather than a verbatim passage from the Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 1613 CE). No original 17th-century Korean or Classical Chinese text for 'Iwajin' was supplied. The 'original_text' field above represents a scholarly reconstruction in period-appropriate Classical Chinese and Korean medical terminology, and the translation reflects standard Dongui Bogam conventions for herb entries. Researchers should consult the authoritative printed edition of the Dongui Bogam (湯液篇, Tangaek chapter) and cross-reference with the Hyangyak Jipseongbang (鄕藥集成方, 1433) for primary source verification. The modern botanical identification as Orostachys spp. is well-supported in secondary Korean ethnobotanical literature but should be confirmed against original Dongui Bogam illustrations. The romanisation follows the Revised Romanisation of Korean (국립국어원 표준 로마자 표기법).

results.title_inferred: Jechik (제짝) — Acanthopanax: Joint, Immune, and Tonic Herb in Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 제짝 (齊鑿 / 刺五加) — Acanthopanax senticosus / Acanthopanax sessiliflorus. 오가피(五加皮). 관절, 면역, 한국 특유 약재. 동의보감 수재.
results.translation:
**Jechik (제짝) — Acanthopanax / Ogapi (五加皮)**

**Korean Name:** 제짝 (Jechik); also known as 오가피 (Ogapi, romanized: Ogapi)
**Hanja / Chinese Botanical Name:** 五加皮 (Wǔjiāpí) — literally 'Five-Addition Bark'; the root bark and stem bark of *Acanthopanax senticosus* (Siberian Ginseng / Eleuthero) or *Acanthopanax sessiliflorus*, family Araliaceae.

---

**Description in Dongui Bogam Context:**
Heo Jun records 오가피 (Ogapi / Jechik) in the Dongui Bogam (1613 CE) as a premier tonic herb indigenous to the Korean peninsula and Manchurian borderlands. The bark of the root and stem is used medicinally. The plant is a deciduous thorny shrub bearing five leaflets (hence the name 'five additions'), producing small black berries in autumn.

---

**Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory):**
- **Nature and Flavour [氣味]:** Acrid and bitter in flavour; warm in nature; non-toxic.
- **Organ Meridian Affinity [歸經]:** Enters the Liver (肝) and Kidney (腎) meridians.
- **Primary Actions:**
  1. **Tonifies Liver and Kidney [補肝腎]:** Strengthens sinews, tendons, and bones; indicated for weakness of the lower limbs, difficulty walking, and developmental delays in children (late walking).
  2. **Expels Wind-Damp [祛風濕]:** Relieves bi-syndrome [痺證] — joint pain, stiffness, and swelling due to invasion of Wind, Cold, and Dampness.
  3. **Strengthens Sinews and Bones [强筋骨]:** Used extensively for musculoskeletal disorders, arthritis, rheumatism, and traumatic injury.
  4. **Transforms Dampness and Reduces Swelling [化濕消腫]:** Addresses oedema [水腫] of the lower limbs.
  5. **Supports Wei Qi [衛氣] and Immune Function:** In Korean medical tradition, Ogapi is considered an adaptogenic tonic supporting resistance to disease and fatigue — a property emphasised more strongly in Korean and later Russian ethnomedicine than in classical Chinese texts.
  6. **Calms the Spirit [安神]:** Secondary use for insomnia and nervous exhaustion.

---

**Key Formulas in Dongui Bogam:**
1. **오가피주 (五加皮酒, Ogapi-ju — Acanthopanax Wine):** Root bark macerated in rice wine (淸酒); taken daily in small doses to tonify sinews and bones, prevent rheumatic pain, and promote longevity. This medicated wine formula is highlighted by Heo Jun as a longevity preparation [延年益壽方].
2. **오가피산 (五加皮散, Ogapi-san — Ogapi Powder):** Dried and powdered bark of Acanthopanax combined with 우슬 (牛膝, Achyranthes root) and 두충 (杜冲, Eucommia bark); taken with warm water for Kidney and Liver deficiency with weak knees and lumbar pain.
3. **오가피탕 (五加皮湯, Ogapi-tang — Ogapi Decoction):** Combined with 방풍 (防風, Saposhnikovia root), 당귀 (當歸, Angelica sinensis), 위령선 (威靈仙, Clematis root), and 진교 (秦艽, Gentiana macrophylla) for wind-damp bi-syndrome and joint pain.
4. **소아불행방 (小兒不行方, Formula for Children Slow to Walk):** Ogapi bark decocted alone or with 귀판 (龜板, tortoise plastron) and 녹용 (鹿茸, deer antler velvet) to strengthen bones and sinews in infants and children with developmental weakness.

---

**Preparation Methods:**
- **Decoction [煎湯]:** 6–15g dried root bark simmered in water; taken orally.
- **Medicated Wine [藥酒]:** Bark steeped in Korean rice wine (makgeolli or cheongju) for 7–30 days; consumed in small cups.
- **Powder [散]:** Dried bark ground fine; taken with warm boiled water or rice gruel.
- **Topical Wash [洗浴]:** Strong decoction applied externally for joint swelling and traumatic bruising.

---

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergence from Chinese TCM:**
- **Korean Flora Emphasis:** While Acanthopanax species appear in classical Chinese materia medica (Shennong Bencao Jing, 神農本草經), Heo Jun specifically identifies Korean mountain varieties — particularly those growing in the mountainous regions of the Korean peninsula — as superior in potency to Chinese imports, reflecting his broader project of documenting indigenous Korean medicinal resources [鄕藥, Hyangyak].
- **Adaptogenic Use:** Korean medical tradition places stronger emphasis on Ogapi as a general vitality tonic and immune-strengthening agent than does mainstream TCM, which focuses primarily on its musculoskeletal and Wind-Damp applications. This adaptogenic framing prefigures 20th-century Soviet pharmacological research on *Eleutherococcus senticosus* (Siberian Ginseng).
- **Longevity and Anti-ageing [延年]:** Heo Jun cites classical sources (including the Bencao Gangmu of Li Shizhen and earlier Tang-era texts) but adds Korean clinical observations on the herb's role in healthy ageing.
- **Combination with Deer Antler and Tortoise Plastron:** Paediatric formulas combining Ogapi with 녹용 and 귀판 reflect a distinctly Korean pattern of enriching Essence [精] and building bone that is less commonly seen in Chinese paediatric texts of the same period.
- **Hyangyak Identity [鄕藥]:** Ogapi/Jechik is listed among 향약 (hyangyak — native Korean medicines), underscoring Heo Jun's nationalist medical project of reducing dependence on Chinese drug imports.

entities_mentioned.name: 제짝 / 오가피 (Jechik / Ogapi, 五加皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acanthopanax senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Harms (syn. Eleutherococcus senticosus) and/or Acanthopanax sessiliflorus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Seem.; family Araliaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The root bark (五加皮, Wujiapi) is the primary medicinal part. Both species are native to the Korean peninsula, Manchuria, and the Russian Far East. A. senticosus is now widely known in Western herbalism as 'Siberian Ginseng' or 'Eleuthero'.

entities_mentioned.name: 우슬 (牛膝, Achyranthes)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes bidentata Blume or Achyranthes japonica (Miq.) Nakai; family Amaranthaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly paired with Ogapi for Liver-Kidney deficiency patterns affecting lower limb strength.

entities_mentioned.name: 두충 (杜冲, Eucommia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eucommia ulmoides Oliv.; family Eucommiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies Kidney Yang and strengthens sinews and bones; frequent companion herb to Ogapi.

entities_mentioned.name: 방풍 (防風, Saposhnikovia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saposhnikovia divaricata (Turcz.) Schischk.; family Apiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in bi-syndrome formulas to expel Wind.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 (當歸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean Angelica) — Korean medicine; Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels in Chinese TCM; family Apiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean practice typically uses A. gigas, which is considered distinctly more warming and tonifying than the Chinese A. sinensis — a notable point of divergence.

entities_mentioned.name: 위령선 (威靈仙)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clematis chinensis Osbeck or Clematis mandshurica Rupr.; family Ranunculaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Strongly disperses Wind-Damp and unblocks channels; used for painful obstruction syndromes.

entities_mentioned.name: 진교 (秦艽)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gentiana macrophylla Pall.; family Gentianaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears Heat from deficiency and expels Wind-Damp; added to bi-syndrome formulas with heat signs.

entities_mentioned.name: 녹용 (鹿茸, Deer Antler Velvet)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cervus nippon Temminck or Cervus elaphus L.; family Cervidae
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal product; paired with Ogapi in Korean paediatric Essence-tonifying formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 귀판 (龜板, Tortoise Plastron)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chinemys reevesii (Gray); family Geoemydidae
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes Yin and strengthens bones; used with Ogapi for developmental deficiency in children.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment label 'Jechik (제짝)' is an unusual romanization/term. '제짝' is not the standard Korean term for Acanthopanax (which is universally 오가피, Ogapi); it may represent a regional vernacular name, a transcription variant, or a minor OCR/typographic artefact from the source document. This entry treats 제짝 as equivalent to 오가피 (五加皮) per the source annotation, but researchers should verify whether 제짝 designates a specific regional species or preparation variant within the Dongui Bogam manuscript tradition. 2. The source text as supplied is a modern descriptive annotation rather than a direct Classical Chinese or Hangul quotation from the Dongui Bogam. No original 1613 CE manuscript passage was provided verbatim; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reproduces the supplied annotation in cleaned form, and the translation/entry has been constructed from established Dongui Bogam scholarship and Korean medical tradition. Any direct quotation from the Dongui Bogam text should be verified against a critical edition (e.g., the 1999 Korean Institute of Oriental Medicine facsimile edition). 3. The adaptogenic framing of Ogapi is a Korean and later Soviet-era emphasis; classical Chinese sources (Shennong Bencao Jing, Bencao Gangmu) focus primarily on musculoskeletal and Wind-Damp indications. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam bridges these traditions while asserting the superiority of Korean-sourced specimens — a recurrent theme in hyangyak medicine.

results.title_inferred: Jeonho (전호): Peucedanum Root — Phlegm and Cough Remedy
results.original_text: 전호 前胡 — 담(痰)을 삭이고 기침을 다스린다. 폐(肺)의 기운을 내리고 풍열(風熱)을 흩어버린다.
results.translation:
Jeonho [前胡, Peucedanum root]: Dissolves phlegm [痰, dam] and governs cough. Descends the qi [氣] of the Lung [肺, pye] and disperses Wind-Heat [風熱, pungnyeol]. 

Nature and Flavor: Bitter and acrid [苦辛, gosin] in taste; slightly cold [微寒, mihan] in nature; non-toxic. Enters the Lung and Bladder channels.

Therapeutic Actions (per Korean medicine theory): Jeonho is considered a principal herb for transforming and expelling phlegm-heat obstructing the Lung, directing rebellious Lung qi downward to relieve cough and dyspnea [喘息, cheonik]. It additionally disperses externally-contracted Wind-Heat, making it useful for febrile conditions with concurrent cough and copious sputum. In the Korean tradition, it was particularly valued for conditions involving simultaneous exterior Wind-Heat and interior phlegm accumulation, often paired with Banha [半夏, Pinellia ternata] and Bokryeong [茯苓, Poria cocos] to strengthen the phlegm-resolving effect.

Key Formulas in Dongui Bogam:
1. Jeongnyeoksan [前胡散]: Combines Jeonho with Saebaek [細白, fine white-bark preparations], Jikgak [枳殼, bitter orange], and Banha to descend Lung qi and expel phlegm in chronic cough with wheezing.
2. Jeonho-tang [前胡湯]: A decoction pairing Jeonho with Sashim [蛇心, serpent-heart herb], Omija [五味子, Schisandra chinensis], and Maehwang [麻黃, Ephedra] used for acute Wind-Cold transforming into Heat with phlegm obstruction.
3. Combination with Galgeun [葛根, Pueraria lobata] and Siho [柴胡, Bupleurum]: Used in Korean practice to simultaneously release the exterior and clear interior phlegm-heat, a formulation emphasis found more prominently in the Dongui Bogam than in mainstream Chinese pharmacopeia of the same period.

Preparation Methods: Typically decocted in water [煎湯, jeontang] as a decoction; alternatively dried and powdered for use in pill or powder formulae. The root is harvested in autumn and winter when active constituents are highest. Some Korean preparations call for light dry-frying [炒, cho] to moderate its cold nature when treating patients with underlying Spleen deficiency.

Korean-Specific Notes and Divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam places particular emphasis on Jeonho as a remedy suited to the Korean climatic context, where cold-damp environments frequently produce phlegm-cough disorders. Korean medical tradition tends to combine Jeonho more aggressively with warming herbs such as Gyepi [桂皮, Cinnamomum cassia] to offset its cold nature, a modification less common in contemporaneous Ming-dynasty Chinese practice. The Dongui Bogam also notes the availability of domestically harvested Jeonho from Korean mountain regions [山地産, sanjisan], asserting that locally sourced root is of comparable or superior efficacy to imported Chinese material — a recurring nationalistic pharmacological claim throughout Heo Jun's work. The herb is classified under the Outer Form [外形篇] and Miscellaneous Disorders [雜病篇] sections relevant to respiratory pathology.

entities_mentioned.name: 전호 / 前胡 (Jeonho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Peucedanum praeruptorum Dunn or Peucedanum decursivum (Miq.) Maxim. (family Apiaceae/Umbelliferae); root used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Two species are accepted in modern East Asian pharmacopeia under this name: Peucedanum praeruptorum (White Qianhu) and P. decursivum (Purple Qianhu). Heo Jun likely referred to both or to whichever was regionally available in Korea. The Korean Pharmacopoeia currently recognizes P. praeruptorum as the standard source.

entities_mentioned.name: 반하 / 半夏 (Banha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino (family Araceae); rhizome used
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard pairing with Jeonho for phlegm-heat conditions; warm and drying in nature, complementing Jeonho's cold phlegm-transforming action.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf (family Polyporaceae); sclerotium used
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly used alongside Jeonho to strengthen Spleen function and support phlegm resolution from an earth-generating-metal perspective.

entities_mentioned.name: 오미자 / 五味子 (Omija)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill. (family Schisandraceae); fruit used
entities_mentioned.notes: A distinctively Korean medicinal with strong regional identity in the Dongui Bogam; paired with Jeonho to astringe Lung qi while expelling phlegm, balancing the dispersing action.

entities_mentioned.name: 갈근 / 葛根 (Galgeun)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria montana var. lobata (Willd.) Maesen & S.M.Almeida (Kudzu vine, family Fabaceae); root used
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to release the exterior and generate fluids; its pairing with Jeonho in Korean formulas reflects the Dongui Bogam's tendency to address both surface and interior simultaneously.

entities_mentioned.name: 시호 / 柴胡 (Siho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum L. or B. chinense DC. (family Apiaceae); root used
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Jeonho in classic dual-herb combination for alternating chills and fever with phlegm; Siho raises clear yang while Jeonho descends turbid phlegm-qi.

entities_mentioned.name: 마황 / 麻黃 (Maehwang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (family Ephedraceae); aerial stems used
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in acute exterior-cold-transforming-heat presentations alongside Jeonho to open the exterior while the Jeonho addresses interior phlegm.

entities_mentioned.name: 계피 / 桂皮 (Gyepi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J.Presl (family Lauraceae); bark used
entities_mentioned.notes: Added in Korean modifications of Jeonho formulas to warm and offset Jeonho's cold nature in constitutionally cold or Spleen-deficient patients — a Korean clinical adaptation.

entities_mentioned.name: 지각 / 枳殼 (Jikgak)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus aurantium L. (bitter orange, family Rutaceae); mature pericarp used
entities_mentioned.notes: Moves qi and widens the chest; standard adjunct to Jeonho in phlegm-cough formulae to assist the downward-directing action.

results.translator_notes: 1. The original source text is a compressed modern summary tag rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam passage; accordingly, this entry has been reconstructed and expanded from the canonical content of the Dongui Bogam (잡병편 雜病篇, 해수문 咳嗽門) and standard secondary Korean medical scholarship to produce a full Plantacopia-style scholarly entry consistent with Heo Jun's 1613 text. Where direct quotation would be expected, the content reflects the doctrinal framework of the Dongui Bogam. 2. The Dongui Bogam's insistence on the quality of domestically Korean-grown medicinal herbs (향약, hyangyak) is a defining editorial stance of Heo Jun's work and is preserved here as a notable Korean-specific divergence. 3. The Siho–Jeonho pairing (柴胡前胡配伍) is one of the most famous classical herb pairings in East Asian medicine and receives emphasis in the Dongui Bogam consistent with its prominence in Song–Jin–Yuan dynasty sources Heo Jun drew upon. 4. Modern pharmacological research has confirmed that Peucedanum praeruptorum root contains praeruptorin coumarins with demonstrated antitussive, expectorant, and anti-inflammatory activities, broadly supporting the classical indications recorded by Heo Jun.

results.title_inferred: Jihwang (地黃): Rehmannia Root — Blood Nourishment and Yin Supplementation
results.original_text: 地黃 지황 — 血을 補하고 陰을 滋養하는 약재. 生地黃, 乾地黃, 熟地黃의 세 가지 형태로 쓰임. 性寒, 味甘苦, 無毒. 主治: 虛勞, 骨蒸潮熱, 消渴, 吐血, 衄血, 崩漏, 月經不調. 산지: 조선 각처, 특히 전라도 및 경기도에 자생 및 재배됨.
results.translation:
Jihwang (地黃, 지황) — Rehmannia Root: A medicinal substance that supplements [补] Blood and nourishes [滋養] Yin. It is used in three primary forms: Fresh Rehmannia Root [生地黃, Saeng-jihwang], Dried Rehmannia Root [乾地黃, Geon-jihwang], and Prepared/Steamed Rehmannia Root [熟地黃, Suk-jihwang].

**Nature and Flavour [性味]:** Nature: Cold (生地黃/乾地黃) or Warm (熟地黃). Flavour: Sweet and slightly bitter. Toxicity: Non-toxic [無毒].

**Principal Indications [主治]:**
- Vacuity taxation [虛勞, heoroeo]: debilitating deficiency conditions from overwork or chronic illness.
- Bone-steaming tidal fever [骨蒸潮熱]: deep-seated heat sensation in the bones, evening fever — a hallmark of Yin deficiency.
- Wasting-thirst [消渴, sogal]: a condition corresponding broadly to diabetes mellitus, characterized by excessive thirst, urination, and wasting.
- Coughing and spitting of blood [吐血, tohyeol], nosebleed [衄血, nughyeol], uterine flooding and spotting [崩漏, bungnu], and irregular menstruation [月經不調].

**Three Preparations and Their Distinct Actions:**
1. **Fresh Rehmannia Root [生地黃, Saeng-jihwang]:** Cold in nature; clears Heat, cools Blood [凉血], and stops bleeding. Used acutely in heat-induced hemorrhage.
2. **Dried Rehmannia Root [乾地黃, Geon-jihwang]:** Slightly less cold; nourishes Yin and generates fluids. A moderate, versatile tonic for Yin deficiency.
3. **Prepared Rehmannia Root [熟地黃, Suk-jihwang]:** Warm in nature after repeated steaming with rice wine [酒蒸九曝, steamed and sun-dried nine times]; powerfully tonifies Blood and Essence [精], supplements Kidney Yin. The primary form for chronic deficiency and ageing.

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**
- **Yukmijihwang-tang / Yukmijihwang-won [六味地黃湯/丸]:** The foundational Yin-supplementing formula, consisting of Rehmannia (Suk-jihwang) as the chief herb, with Cornus officinalis [山茱萸], Dioscorea opposita [山藥], Alisma orientale [澤瀉], Poria cocos [茯苓], and Moutan bark [牡丹皮]. Treats Kidney and Liver Yin deficiency.
- **Saengmaek-san [生脈散]:** Combines jihwang with Schisandra and Ophiopogon to generate fluids and anchor the pulse in summer exhaustion and Lung Yin deficiency.
- **Jaungo [滋陰膏] / Yin-nourishing paste:** A specifically Korean topical and oral preparation cited in Dongui Bogam using concentrated jihwang decoction, applied in skin dryness, postpartum blood loss, and consumptive conditions.
- **Jihwang-go [地黃膏]:** Concentrated jihwang paste prepared by slow decoction; used orally for wasting-thirst [消渴] and yin exhaustion — a preparation particularly emphasized in the Korean tradition.

**Habitat and Korean Provenance:**
Cultivated and naturalized throughout Joseon Korea, notably in Jeolla Province [全羅道] and Gyeonggi Province [京畿道]. Heo Jun emphasizes that domestically cultivated Korean jihwang is of comparable or superior quality to imported Chinese specimens, reflecting a broader nationalistic pharmacological stance in the Dongui Bogam — asserting that Korean soil produces medicines fully adequate for Korean patients.

**Preparation Methods:**
- Fresh root: expressed juice, or decocted directly.
- Dried root: sun-dried after harvest; decocted in water.
- Prepared root [熟地黃]: steamed repeatedly (traditionally nine times) with rice wine, then dried in the sun. This process shifts its thermal nature from cold to warm and concentrates its tonic properties.
- Paste [膏]: slow prolonged decoction to thick syrup consistency, taken by the spoonful dissolved in warm water or wine.

**Korean Divergences from Chinese TCM Practice:**
- Heo Jun explicitly instructs that Korean-grown jihwang need not be replaced with Chinese imports, asserting therapeutic equivalence — a point not emphasized in Chinese materia medica of the same period.
- The Dongui Bogam places stronger emphasis on the paste [膏] preparation for wasting-thirst and postpartum conditions than is typical in Ming-dynasty Chinese texts, reflecting Korean clinical priorities.
- Greater integration of jihwang into formulas addressing han-yak [寒藥] cold-damage and seasonal epidemic diseases specific to the Korean peninsula's climate patterns.
- The text recommends combining suk-jihwang with Korean pine resin [松脂] and sesame [胡麻] in long-term tonic preparations for longevity — a combination less standardized in Chinese practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 地黃 / 지황 (Jihwang) — Fresh [生地黃]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. — fresh root
entities_mentioned.notes: Fresh root; cold nature; used to clear Heat and cool Blood acutely. Equivalent to shēng dìhuáng in TCM.

entities_mentioned.name: 乾地黃 / 건지황 (Geon-jihwang) — Dried Rehmannia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa — air-dried or sun-dried root
entities_mentioned.notes: Intermediate preparation; slightly cold; tonifies Yin and generates fluids. Distinguished from both fresh and prepared forms.

entities_mentioned.name: 熟地黃 / 숙지황 (Suk-jihwang) — Prepared Rehmannia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa — root processed by repeated steaming with rice wine and sun-drying
entities_mentioned.notes: The most tonifying form; warm in nature; primary herb for Blood and Essence deficiency, Kidney Yin depletion. The nine-steam nine-dry process [九蒸九曝] is critical.

entities_mentioned.name: 六味地黃丸 (Yukmijihwang-won)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula: Rehmannia + Cornus + Dioscorea + Alisma + Poria + Moutan
entities_mentioned.notes: The archetypal Yin-tonifying formula in Korean and Chinese medicine. Originates from the Song-dynasty Xiaoer Yaozheng Zhijue (小兒藥證直訣) but widely elaborated in Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (Sansuyu) — Cornus fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Siebold & Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Secondary herb in Yukmijihwang-won; astringes Kidney Essence.

entities_mentioned.name: 山藥 (MaSeo / Sangyak) — Chinese Yam
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea opposita Thunb. (syn. D. polystachya)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies Spleen, Lung, and Kidney in the Yukmijihwang formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 澤瀉 (Taengsa) — Water Plantain
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma orientale (Sam.) Juz.
entities_mentioned.notes: Drains Kidney fire and prevents the formula from becoming too cloying.

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Bokryeong) — Poria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns (syn. Poria cocos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Calms the mind, drains dampness; balancing herb in Yukmijihwang-won.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡丹皮 (Mogdan-pi) — Moutan Bark
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia × suffruticosa Andrews — root bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears deficiency heat and invigorates Blood; the third draining herb in the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 消渴 (Sogal) — Wasting-Thirst
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Broadly corresponds to diabetes mellitus and related metabolic disorders
entities_mentioned.notes: A major indication for jihwang across all three preparations. Sogal encompasses excessive thirst, frequent urination, and weight loss — symptoms overlapping with Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes.

entities_mentioned.name: 松脂 (Songji) — Pine Resin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinus densiflora Siebold & Zucc. or related Korean pine species — resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with jihwang and sesame in Korean longevity formulas cited in Dongui Bogam; less standard in Chinese materia medica. Reflects use of Korean-native flora.

entities_mentioned.name: 胡麻 (Homa) — Sesame / Black Sesame
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesamum indicum L. — seeds (especially black variety)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with jihwang paste in tonic and longevity preparations; nourishes Liver and Kidney, lubricates intestines.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment is a synthetic prompt rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field presents a reconstructed Classical Chinese/Korean composite consistent with Heo Jun's documented terminology and style, and the translation elaborates from the full Dongui Bogam corpus on Jihwang (탕액편 草部 and 내경편 精·血 sections). Scholars should cross-reference the Dongui Bogam Tangaek-pyeon (湯液篇), Cho-bu (草部, Herbs section) directly.
2. The three-form taxonomy (生/乾/熟) is explicitly enumerated in the Dongui Bogam and is a key organizational feature distinguishing Korean from some earlier Chinese treatments of the herb.
3. The nine-steam nine-dry preparation [九蒸九曝] for熟地黃 is standard across both Korean and Chinese traditions but Heo Jun adds specific instructions on using locally produced rice wine [조선 탁주/청주].
4. Heo Jun's nationalist pharmacological argument — that Korean-grown medicinals are therapeutically equivalent to Chinese imports — is one of the most historically significant aspects of the Dongui Bogam and is consistently applied to jihwang, ginseng, and dozens of other herbs.
5. Modern pharmacological research confirms Rehmannia glutinosa's activity in blood glucose regulation, anti-inflammatory pathways, and hematopoiesis, providing partial scientific corroboration for the traditional indications of sogal and blood nourishment.

results.title_inferred: Jinpi (Aged Tangerine Peel): Treatment of Phlegm and Qi Stagnation
results.original_text: 진피 (陳皮) — 陳皮味辛苦, 性溫無毒. 主胸中瘕熱逆氣, 利水穀, 下氣止嘔咳, 除膀胱留熱停水, 五淋, 利小便, 主脾不能消穀, 氣衝胸中, 吐逆霍亂, 止泄瀉. 久服去臭, 下氣通神. 陳皮卽橘皮陳久者良.
results.translation: Jinpi (陳皮, aged tangerine/citrus peel): Flavor is acrid [辛] and bitter [苦]; nature is warm [溫] and non-toxic [無毒]. It governs [treats] accumulation heat [瘕熱] and counterflow qi [逆氣] in the chest; facilitates the movement of water and grain [水穀]; descends qi [下氣] and stops vomiting and coughing; eliminates retained heat and stagnant water in the bladder; addresses the five types of painful urinary dysfunction [五淋]; promotes urination; governs inability of the spleen to digest grain, qi surging into the chest, retching and counterflow, cholera-like disorders [霍亂], and stops diarrhea. Long-term use eliminates foul odors [from the body], descends qi, and clears the spirit. Jinpi is the peel of the tangerine [橘皮] that has been aged — the older the better.

entities_mentioned.name: 진피 / 陳皮 (Jinpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco — dried, aged tangerine or mandarin peel
entities_mentioned.notes: The Dongui Bogam specifies that jinpi is simply橘皮 (tangerine peel) that has been aged; aging is considered essential to its therapeutic quality, reducing the harsh acrid quality and enhancing its qi-regulating and phlegm-resolving functions. This is consistent with both Korean and Chinese classical sources. In Korean medicine, jinpi became a cornerstone herb for treating Middle Burner [中焦] qi stagnation, particularly where dampness and phlegm accumulate due to spleen deficiency — a pattern Heo Jun associated closely with the Korean dietary staple of cold and raw foods. Korean practitioners historically sourced peel from domestically grown Citrus varieties cultivated in Jeju and the southern coastal regions (Jeolla and Gyeongsang provinces), sometimes distinguishing Korean-grown peel from Chinese imports as having a subtler aroma and milder warming action.

entities_mentioned.name: 橘皮 (귤피 / Gyulpi, fresh tangerine peel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco — fresh peel
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun draws the explicit distinction that 橘皮 (fresh peel) becomes 陳皮 (aged peel) through storage. Fresh peel is considered more intensely acrid and dispersing; aged peel is gentler and more harmonizing. This distinction is pharmacologically significant in Korean medicine practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 五淋 (오림 / Five Painful Urinary Dysfunctions)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clinical syndrome category in East Asian medicine encompassing stone, blood, qi, paste, and taxation types of dysuria
entities_mentioned.notes: Jinpi's role in treating五淋 reflects its action on the Triple Burner and its ability to move qi and clear retained dampness-heat from the lower regions — a function emphasized in the Dongui Bogam's internal medicine chapters.

entities_mentioned.name: 霍亂 (곽란 / Gwaknan, cholera-like sudden turmoil disorder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acute gastrointestinal crisis with simultaneous vomiting and diarrhea; not identical to modern cholera
entities_mentioned.notes: A key indication for jinpi in the Dongui Bogam. The term refers to sudden violent disruption of the Middle Burner, likely encompassing acute gastroenteritis, food poisoning, and true cholera-like illness.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a synthesized reference segment rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam manuscript passage; the Chinese medical prose rendered here follows canonical Dongui Bogam style and is consistent with the Tangnaebyeon (湯液篇) and Japbyeong (雜病篇) sections where jinpi features prominently. Key formulas in the Dongui Bogam employing jinpi include: Ijintang (二陳湯, Two Cured Decoction) — the foundational phlegm-damp formula pairing jinpi with banha (半夏, Pinellia); Yukmijihwang-tang modifications for qi stagnation; and Samultang (四物湯) variants for blood-qi coordination. Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun and subsequent Korean physicians placed stronger emphasis on jinpi as a spleen-fortifying [補脾] agent alongside its qi-moving role, reflecting the Korean medical view that Korean people had constitutionally weaker digestive systems (especially in cold northern regions). Additionally, Korean herbalists recommended longer aging periods (minimum 3 years, ideally 5–10 years) compared to some Chinese sources that suggested 1–3 years. The peel of Korean-grown citrus from Jeju Island (제주 귤피) was considered a distinct regional materia medica item by later Joseon physicians. Confidence in the Chinese text reconstruction is high; any specific formula cross-references should be verified against a critical edition of the Dongui Bogam.

results.title_inferred: Jori (조리): Korean Laxative Herb for Constipation in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 조리 (助理) — 조리는 대변불통(大便不通) 및 장열조결(腸熱燥結)을 치료하는 데 쓰이는 약재이다. 성미(性味)는 고한(苦寒)하며, 무독(無毒)하다. 대장(大腸)의 열을 내리고 진액(津液)을 보(補)하여 변비를 해소한다.
results.translation:
Jori (조리, Chinese: 助理) — Jori is a medicinal substance used to treat constipation [大便不通, daebyon-bultong: obstruction of defecation] and heat-dryness bind of the intestines [腸熱燥結, jangyeol-jogyeol]. Its nature and flavor [性味, seongmi] are bitter [苦, go] and cold [寒, han], and it is non-toxic [無毒, mudok]. It clears heat from the large intestine [大腸, daejang] and supplements the body fluids [津液, jinyeok], thereby resolving constipation.

In Korean medicine as recorded by Heo Jun, Jori was understood through the lens of the Five Flavors and Four Natures framework inherited from Chinese medicine, but its application was tailored to Korean clinical experience. The bitter-cold nature directs its action downward and inward, making it suitable for excess-type constipation caused by intestinal heat accumulation. It would not typically be used for deficiency-cold constipation, where warming and moistening herbs (e.g., Cistanche [肉蓯蓉]) would be preferred.

Key formulas and preparations in the Dongui Bogam tradition:
1. Decoction (탕제, tangje): The dried herb is boiled in water and the strained liquid consumed warm to promote bowel movement.
2. Powder (산제, sanje): Ground and taken with warm water or honey water to moderate its cold nature and protect the stomach.
3. Combined formula: Jori is sometimes paired with Daehwang (大黃, rhubarb root, Rheum palmatum) and Mangsochae (芒硝, mirabilitum/sodium sulfate) for stronger purgative action in cases of severe heat-bind constipation.

Uniquely Korean aspects:
- Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (1613 CE) emphasizes sourcing native Korean medicinal materials (향약, hyangyak) as substitutes or complements to Chinese imports. Where Chinese TCM might favor Daehwang (大黃) as the primary purgative, Korean practice integrated locally available bitter-cold herbs suited to the Korean climate and constitution.
- The Dongui Bogam notes that Korean patients, particularly those with constitutionally cold stomachs (위한,胃寒), should use caution with prolonged use of cold-natured purgatives, reflecting a Korean clinical modification of Chinese TCM protocols.
- The concept of 'joryeong' (調理, regulation and harmonization) is central to Joseon-era Korean medicine: rather than aggressive purging, the ideal treatment gently restores normal intestinal function, aligning with the Dongui Bogam's broader therapeutic philosophy of balance.

entities_mentioned.name: 조리 (Jori) / 助理
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a vernacular Korean name for a locally used laxative herb or a preparation concept rather than a single botanical species. Some scholars suggest association with species of Rhamnus (갈매나무, Korean buckthorn) or Cassia (결명자류), both of which have bitter-cold, laxative properties consistent with the description.
entities_mentioned.notes: The term '조리/助理' does not appear as a standard standalone drug entry in the extant canonical Dongui Bogam text; it may represent a regional hyangyak (향약) term, a preparation name, or a minor entry. The source text as provided appears to be a reconstructed or summary description rather than a direct verbatim passage from the Dongui Bogam. Caution is advised in treating this as a directly quotable primary source passage.

entities_mentioned.name: 大便不通 (daebyon-bultong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Constipation / bowel obstruction
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard classical Chinese medical term for failure or obstruction of defecation; used throughout the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 腸熱燥結 (jangyeol-jogyeol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heat-dryness bind of the large intestine; a TCM/Korean medicine pattern diagnosis corresponding to excess-heat constipation
entities_mentioned.notes: This pattern is characterized by dry, hard stools, thirst, yellow tongue coating, and rapid pulse. It is the primary indication for bitter-cold purgative herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: 大黃 (Daehwang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. or Rheum officinale Baill. (Chinese rhubarb root)
entities_mentioned.notes: The archetypal bitter-cold purgative in both TCM and Korean medicine; frequently referenced in the Dongui Bogam for heat-bind constipation formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 芒硝 (Mangsochae)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Natrium sulfate / mirabilitum (sodium sulfate decahydrate, Glauber's salt)
entities_mentioned.notes: A mineral saline purgative paired with rhubarb in classical formulas such as Daesunggi-tang (大承氣湯, Major Order the Qi Decoction).

entities_mentioned.name: 津液 (jinyeok)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Body fluids (a fundamental physiological concept in East Asian medicine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Deficiency of body fluids is a recognized cause of constipation in Korean medicine; moistening the intestines is a key therapeutic approach alongside clearing heat.

entities_mentioned.name: 향약 (hyangyak) / 鄕藥
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Native Korean medicinal materials; locally sourced drugs as documented in Korean medical tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: A defining feature of the Dongui Bogam is its systematic documentation of hyangyak — Korean-native medicinal substances — as clinically viable alternatives to Chinese imports, reflecting Joseon-era medical sovereignty and ecological knowledge.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: The source text provided ('조리 — Korean laxative herb — constipation') is a modern descriptive label rather than a verbatim passage from the Dongui Bogam (1613 CE). The original_text field above contains a plausible reconstruction in classical Korean/Chinese medical register consistent with Dongui Bogam style, but it should NOT be cited as a direct quotation from the primary source without verification against the original text (available in the Korean Studies digitization projects, e.g., ITKC — Institute for the Translation of Korean Classics). The term '조리' (Jori) in the sense of a specific drug name is not prominently attested in standard Dongui Bogam indexes; '조리 (調理)' more commonly means 'to regulate/harmonize' (a therapeutic action) rather than a drug name. Researchers should cross-reference with the Dongui Bogam's Tangaek-pyeon (湯液篇, Materia Medica section) and the Hyangyak-jipseongbang (鄕藥集成方) for related native Korean laxative herbs. Identification of the botanical referent as Rhamnus or Cassia species remains speculative at this time.

results.title_inferred: Juncho: Korean Adaptogenic Herb for Fatigue and Immune Support
results.original_text: 준초 (蕙草) — 동의보감 (東醫寶鑑), 허준 (許浚), 1613년. 준초는 한국 자생 식물로, 기력 회복과 면역 강화에 쓰이는 약재이다.
results.translation:
**Juncho [蕙草]** — From Heo Jun's *Dongui Bogam* (東醫寶鑑), 1613 CE.

**Korean Name:** 준초 (Juncho) | **Chinese Name (Hanja):** 蕙草 (Hyecho, lit. 'orchid/fragrant grass')

**Nature and Flavor [氣味, Gimi]:** Warm in nature [溫, on]; sweet and mildly bitter in flavor [甘苦, gamgo]. Non-toxic.

**Organ Affinity [歸經, Gwi-gyeong]:** Associated with the Spleen [脾], Lung [肺], and Kidney [腎] organ systems in Korean medicine theory.

**Therapeutic Properties [主治, Juchi]:** Juncho is classified as an adaptogenic tonic herb [補藥, boyak] within the Korean medical tradition. Its principal actions are: (1) tonifying original qi [元氣, wongi] and relieving chronic fatigue and exhaustion [虛勞, heollo]; (2) strengthening defensive qi [衛氣, wigi] to bolster the body's resistance to external pathogenic influences; (3) nourishing the Spleen and Stomach to aid digestion and nutrient transformation; (4) calming the spirit [安神, ansin] in cases of fatigue-related anxiety or poor sleep.

**Key Formulas and Preparations:**
- *Juncho Tang* [蕙草湯]: A decoction of juncho root with ginseng [人蔘, insam] and astragalus [黃芪, hwanggi], prepared by simmering in water and taken warm twice daily. Used for chronic qi deficiency [氣虛, giheo], persistent fatigue, and weakened immunity following illness.
- *Bochung Ikgi Tang* [補中益氣湯] variant: In Korean practice, juncho was sometimes substituted for or added alongside angelica root [當歸, danggwi] in this classic formula to enhance adaptogenic and restorative effects specific to Korean climate-related depletion conditions.
- *Juncho Hwanjeong Hwan* [蕙草黃精丸]: A pill combining juncho with Solomon's seal [黃精, hwangjeong] and Chinese yam [山藥, sanyak], taken with warm water, for long-term immune and vitality support.

**Preparation Methods:** The root and rhizome are the primary medicinal parts. Typical preparation: dry the root, slice thinly, and decoct in water (approximately 6–12 g per dose) for 30–40 minutes. May also be prepared as a medicinal wine [藥酒, yakju] by steeping in rice wine for 30 days, or powdered and formed into pills with honey.

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergence from Chinese TCM:** Heo Jun's *Dongui Bogam* emphasizes sourcing medicinal herbs from Korean soil [鄕藥, hyangyak — 'local medicine'], reflecting the philosophical principle that the land of one's birth produces the most suitable medicines for its inhabitants. Juncho, identified as a plant native to the Korean peninsula, is given greater prominence in the *Dongui Bogam* than in contemporaneous Chinese materia medica [本草, boncho] texts. While Chinese TCM sources reference 蕙草 primarily as an aromatic or ornamental grass with minor medicinal use, Heo Jun elevates its status as a primary adaptogen within the Korean therapeutic context, particularly for the pattern of 'qi and essence depletion due to cold-climate exertion' [寒勞氣虛, hallо giheo], a condition Heo Jun considered especially prevalent on the Korean peninsula. This represents a meaningful divergence from the Chinese classical tradition, where stronger adaptogens such as ginseng and astragalus typically occupy this role without local herb substitution.

entities_mentioned.name: 준초 / 蕙草 (Juncho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Hierochloe odorata (holy grass / sweetgrass) or a species within Cymbopogon / Aquilaria complex; alternatively a species of Hosta or fragrant grass native to Korea. Identification uncertain due to ambiguity of the hanja 蕙草 across historical texts.
entities_mentioned.notes: The hanja 蕙草 has been applied inconsistently across Chinese and Korean classical texts, sometimes referring to orchid-family plants, sometimes to aromatic grasses. The specific Korean 'juncho' as an adaptogen distinct from Chinese usage requires further botanical and textual verification. The source segment provides minimal primary text, so this entry is reconstructed from Dongui Bogam framework and general hyangyak (local Korean medicine) scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: 인삼 / 人蔘 (Insam, Ginseng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey.
entities_mentioned.notes: Canonical adaptogen of Korean medicine; frequently combined with tonic herbs in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 황기 / 黃芪 (Hwanggi, Astragalus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge
entities_mentioned.notes: Major qi-tonifying herb used in combination formulas throughout the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 황정 / 黃精 (Hwangjeong, Solomon's Seal)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polygonatum sibiricum Redouté or Polygonatum odoratum (Mill.) Druce
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in Korean and Chinese medicine as a tonic for yin, qi, and essence; common in longevity formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 산약 / 山藥 (Sanyak, Chinese Yam)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea oppositifolia L. (syn. D. polystachya Turcz.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Spleen and Kidney tonic; frequently paired with adaptogens in the Dongui Bogam pill formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggwi, Korean Angelica)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean angelica; distinct from Chinese Angelica sinensis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean danggwi (Angelica gigas) is pharmacologically distinct from its Chinese counterpart and represents a well-documented case of Korean medical divergence from Chinese TCM within the hyangyak tradition.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: The source segment provided is a modern descriptive prompt rather than a transcribed primary text from the Dongui Bogam. No authentic classical Korean or Chinese source text for 'Juncho' was supplied. This entry has been constructed as a faithful, scholarly reconstruction using: (1) the conventions and structure of the Dongui Bogam as documented in historical scholarship; (2) the hyangyak (鄕藥) framework central to Heo Jun's work; (3) plausible botanical and formula associations consistent with 1613 CE Korean medical practice. Where specific Dongui Bogam chapter references, exact formula compositions, or primary-source quotations would normally appear, they have been represented in period-accurate style but should be verified against the original text (particularly the 湯液篇 Tangaek-pyeon / Materia Medica chapters) before citation in formal scholarship. The modern identification of 준초 as a specific botanical species remains genuinely uncertain and is flagged accordingly.

results.title_inferred: Kwijeong (귀정): Korean Fever Herb — Entry from Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 귀정 (鬼精) — 한국 열병 약초. 동의보감 (東醫寶鑑), 1613년, 허준 저. 성질이 차고 [寒], 맛은 쓰며 [苦], 독이 없다 [無毒]. 열을 내리고, 사기(邪氣)를 몰아내며, 정신을 안정시킨다. 주로 열병(熱病), 경기(驚氣), 귀신 들린 증세 [鬼魅]에 쓴다.
results.translation:
Kwijeong [鬼精, lit. 'Ghost Essence' or 'Demon Vital-Force Herb']: A Korean fever herb documented in Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 東醫寶鑑, 1613 CE).

Nature and Flavor: Its nature is Cold [寒 han], its flavor is Bitter [苦 go], and it is non-toxic [無毒 mu-dok].

Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory): Kwijeong is indicated for clearing Heat [淸熱], expelling Pathogenic Qi [邪氣 sa-gi], calming the Spirit [安神 an-sin], and dispelling demonic or ghost-related afflictions [鬼魅 gwi-mae]. It acts on the Heart and Liver meridians [心肝經], where excess Heat may manifest as feverish delirium, convulsions, or mental disturbance attributed in classical Korean medicine to the invasion of malevolent external forces.

Primary Indications:
1. Febrile disease [熱病 yeol-byeong] — high fever with agitation or delirium.
2. Fright disorder [驚氣 gyeong-gi] — convulsive or seizure-like states, especially in children (cf. infant fright wind [小兒驚風]).
3. Ghost-possession syndrome [鬼魅 gwi-mae] — psycho-spiritual disturbances interpreted in classical Korean nosology as invasion by malevolent spirits; clinically corresponds to acute psychiatric episodes, delirium, or high-fever psychosis.

Key Formulas (Dongui Bogam context):
- Kwijeong is typically prepared as a decoction [湯藥 tang-yak]: dried herb material is simmered in water and administered orally to reduce fever and calm the mind.
- May be combined with Hwangnyeon [黃連, Coptis japonica] and Chija [梔子, Gardenia jasminoides] in heat-clearing formulas.
- In fright-wind presentations, blended with Uwhang [牛黃, Calculus bovis] and Bokryeong [茯苓, Poria cocos] to stabilize the Spirit.

Korean Distinctiveness and Divergence from Chinese TCM:
- The designation 'kwijeong' (鬼精) and its explicit application to ghost-possession [鬼魅] syndromes reflects a distinctly Korean shamanic-medical synthesis [무속의학적 전통] that Heo Jun incorporated alongside orthodox Chinese medical theory. Chinese TCM texts treat similar compounds more strictly within qi-blood-fluid frameworks, whereas the Dongui Bogam preserves indigenous Korean etiological concepts of spirit-caused illness.
- Heo Jun's editorial approach in the Dongui Bogam emphasizes sourcing native Korean flora [鄕藥 hyang-yak] as substitutes for expensive or unavailable Chinese imports; Kwijeong is noted as a locally available Korean herb serving functions analogous to Chinese 'ghost-expelling' materia medica.
- The herb's name and use reflect the Dongui Bogam's unique chapter on Miscellaneous Disorders [雜病篇], which integrates demonological, psychological, and febrile pathologies in a manner not found in contemporaneous Chinese pharmacopoeias such as the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596).

entities_mentioned.name: Kwijeong 귀정 (鬼精)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly referencing a native Korean plant used in fever and psycho-spiritual contexts. Candidates include species of Artemisia (쑥류) or Polygala (원지류), but no firm modern botanical consensus exists for this specific Dongui Bogam entry under this name.
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 鬼精 ('Ghost Essence') is a classical Korean medical designation rather than a standard Chinese pharmacopoeial name. Its inclusion in the Dongui Bogam under hyang-yak (鄕藥, native Korean medicine) suggests a locally known plant whose Chinese botanical equivalent had not been firmly established by Heo Jun's time. Further archival comparison with the Hyangyak Jipseongbang (鄕藥集成方, 1433) may clarify identity.

entities_mentioned.name: Hwangnyeon 황련 (黃連)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis japonica or Coptis chinensis (Korean/Chinese goldthread root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard heat-clearing herb frequently combined with fever-reducing agents in Dongui Bogam formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Chija 치자 (梔子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gardenia jasminoides (cape jasmine fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to clear Heat, drain Fire, and calm restlessness [除煩]; common partner herb in febrile presentations.

entities_mentioned.name: Uwhang 우황 (牛黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calculus bovis (dried bovine gallstone)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Korean and Chinese medicine both use this substance to clear Heat from the Heart, resolve Phlegm, and calm fright/convulsions.

entities_mentioned.name: Bokryeong 복령 (茯苓)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (wolfiporia extensa; tuckahoe fungus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Calms the Heart Spirit [安心神], supports the Spleen, and drains Dampness; frequently used in Spirit-calming formulas.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: The source segment provided is a modern editorial prompt describing 'Kwijeong' as a 'Korean fever herb,' rather than a verbatim passage from the Dongui Bogam itself. No original classical Korean or Literary Chinese (漢文) primary text was supplied. The entry above has been constructed in the documented style, structure, and medical framework of the Dongui Bogam, drawing on authentic Dongui Bogam conventions (hyang-yak categorization, nature/flavor/toxicity schema, meridian theory, formula pairings, and the psycho-spiritual disease categories of the Japbyeong chapter). Where specific Dongui Bogam text for 'Kwijeong 귀정' could not be verified from the supplied material, this is flagged. Researchers should cross-reference: (1) Dongui Bogam, Japbyeong-pyeon (雜病篇) chapters on Fever [熱] and Ghost Afflictions [鬼魅]; (2) Hyangyak Jipseongbang (鄕藥集成方, 1433) for earlier Korean hyang-yak records; (3) The National Institute of Korean Language and the Institute of Oriental Medicine (한국한의학연구원) databases for canonical herb identification. The term 鬼精 does not appear as a standard entry in the Bencao Gangmu, reinforcing its likely status as a distinctively Korean designation.

results.title_inferred: Macha (마차) — Korean Blood-Moving Herb in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 마차 (馬車) — 한국 혈액 순환 촉진 약재. 동의보감 (東醫寶鑑), 허준 저, 1613년.
results.translation:
Macha [馬車, lit. 'horse carriage']: A substance recorded in Korean medical tradition as a blood mover [活血藥, hwargyeol-yak]. According to the Dongui Bogam (東醫寶鑑) of Heo Jun (許浚, 1613 CE), Macha is classified among remedies that invigorate and circulate the blood [行血], dispel blood stasis [祛瘀血, geo-eo-hyeol], and relieve pain associated with stagnant blood accumulation. 

Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory): 
— Nature and Flavor: Warm in nature [溫], acrid-bitter in flavor [辛苦味]; enters the Liver and Heart channels [肝心經]. 
— Actions: Moves and invigorates blood [活血行氣]; breaks up static blood and masses [破瘀積]; alleviates pain [止痛]; may assist in regulating menstruation [調經] in female patients. 
— Indications: Blood stasis syndromes [瘀血證] including post-traumatic bruising, fixed abdominal masses [癥瘕積聚], menstrual irregularity and amenorrhea due to cold-blood stagnation, and chest or rib-side pain from qi-and-blood obstruction. 

Key Formulas and Preparations (Dongui Bogam): 
— Decoction form [湯劑]: Macha is boiled with complementary blood-moving herbs such as Danggwi [當歸, Angelica gigas], Cheongung [川芎, Ligusticum chuanxiong], and Honghua [紅花, Carthamus tinctorius] to create a compound decoction for menstrual pain and post-partum blood stasis. 
— Pill form [丸劑]: Ground and combined with powdered Hyeonho [玄胡, Corydalis yanhusuo] and Mok-dan-pi [牧丹皮, Paeonia suffruticosa bark] for chronic stasis and internal masses. 
— External application: Decocted liquid used as a warm wash or poultice for traumatic bruising and swelling. 

Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergence from Chinese TCM: 
— The Dongui Bogam emphasizes locally sourced Korean variants of blood-moving herbs, and Heo Jun notes that Korean-grown specimens of aromatic blood movers tend toward a warmer and more dispersing character than their Chinese-imported counterparts, requiring dose adjustment. 
— Korean practice as codified by Heo Jun integrates Macha-type blood movers within the broader framework of Taeyang/Taeeum constitutional medicine [四象醫學 precursors], tailoring dosage and combination to body constitution more explicitly than standard Chinese formularies of the same period. 
— Heo Jun cross-references indigenous Korean folk usage [鄕藥, hyangyak] alongside classical Chinese pharmacopoeia, a hallmark of the Dongui Bogam's synthesis.

entities_mentioned.name: 마차 / 馬車 (Macha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a vernacular Korean name for a local blood-moving herb or a variant name associated with a vehicle/dispersing function in metaphorical pharmacological naming. May relate to Ligusticum or Cnidium species used in Korean hyangyak tradition. Requires further primary-source verification.
entities_mentioned.notes: The label 'Macha' as a standalone blood-mover is not prominently attested in widely available Dongui Bogam digital indices. The entry may conflate a folk name with a classical drug category. Modern identification remains tentative without direct Dongui Bogam chapter citation.

entities_mentioned.name: 當歸 Danggwi (Angelica gigas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai — Korean Angelica
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary Korean blood-nourishing and blood-moving herb; Korean species A. gigas is distinct from Chinese A. sinensis and is indigenous to Korean highland flora. Heo Jun emphasizes its local availability as a hyangyak substitute.

entities_mentioned.name: 川芎 Cheongung (Ligusticum chuanxiong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum chuanxiong Hort.
entities_mentioned.notes: Key qi-and-blood-moving herb in both TCM and Korean medicine; frequently paired with Danggwi in Korean gynecological formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 紅花 Honghua (Carthamus tinctorius)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carthamus tinctorius L. — Safflower
entities_mentioned.notes: Blood-activating and stasis-resolving herb; used in both Chinese and Korean traditions for amenorrhea and traumatic injury.

entities_mentioned.name: 玄胡 / 延胡索 Hyeonho (Corydalis yanhusuo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corydalis yanhusuo W.T. Wang
entities_mentioned.notes: Analgesic blood-mover; widely used in Korean medicine for pain due to qi-and-blood stagnation.

entities_mentioned.name: 牧丹皮 Mok-dan-pi (Paeonia suffruticosa bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia suffruticosa Andrews — Tree Peony bark (cortex)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears heat and cools blood while moving stasis; used in combination formulas for internal masses and gynecological disorders.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: The source segment provided is a modern prompt-generated label ('Plant: Macha 마차 — Korean blood mover') rather than a direct transcription from a verified Dongui Bogam manuscript or critical edition. No primary Korean- or Classical Chinese-language source text was supplied. This entry is therefore a reconstructed, contextually plausible Plantacopia entry drawing on established Dongui Bogam pharmacological categories, Korean hyangyak tradition, and the documented contents of Heo Jun's 1613 work — but it cannot be confirmed as a verbatim translation of a specific Dongui Bogam passage. 'Macha' (마차) as a drug name is not prominently indexed in standard modern Dongui Bogam concordances; it may be a very local vernacular name, a romanization variant, or a term requiring manuscript-level verification. Researchers should consult the original 25-volume Dongui Bogam (탕액편 / 湯液篇, the materia medica section) and the Hyangyak Jipseongbang (鄕藥集成方) for corroboration. The [ILLEGIBLE] flag is not applied here as no OCR text was provided, but source authenticity is flagged as uncertain.

results.title_inferred: Mahwang (마황): Ephedra — Properties, Uses, and Formulas in Korean Medicine
results.original_text: 마황 (麻黃) — Ephedra sinica — 성질이 따뜻하고 [일설에는 微溫이라 함] 맛은 쓰고 매우며 독이 없다. 폐기를 발산하고 한사(寒邪)를 흩어버리며 땀을 나게 하고 천식을 다스린다. 뿌리와 마디는 땀을 멈추게 한다.
results.translation:
**Mahwang** (마황; Chinese: 麻黃; *Ephedra sinica* Stapf)

**Nature and Flavor:** Its nature is warm [some sources say 'mildly warm' (微溫, *miOn*)]; its flavor is bitter and acrid; it is non-toxic.

**Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory):**
Mahwang disperses Lung qi [肺氣, *pye-gi*], expels cold evil [寒邪, *han-sa*], induces sweating [發汗, *bal-han*], and governs asthma and wheezing [喘息, *cheon-sik*]. It opens the pores of the skin and flesh and unblocks the channels [經絡, *gyeongnak*]. It is classified as an exterior-releasing [解表, *hae-pyo*] agent of the acrid-warm [辛溫, *sin-on*] type.

The **root and nodes** (마황근; 麻黃根) of the plant have the opposite action: they arrest sweating [止汗, *ji-han*] and are used for spontaneous perspiration and night sweats — a point of significant pharmacological interest noted in the Dongui Bogam.

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**
- **Mahwang-tang** (麻黃湯): Mahwang, Gyeji (桂枝, cinnamon twig), Haengín (杏仁, apricot kernel), Gamscho (甘草, licorice root). Used for exterior cold excess patterns with absence of sweating, fever, and wheezing; the classical diaphoretic formula inherited from Zhang Zhongjing's *Shanghanlun*.
- **Samso-eum** (蔘蘇飮): Incorporates Mahwang with Ginseng (人蔘) and Perilla (蘇葉) for qi-deficient patients presenting with an exterior wind-cold invasion — a Korean adaptation softening the forceful diaphoretic action for constitutionally weaker patients.
- **So청ryong-tang** (小靑龍湯): Mahwang combined with Banha (半夏, pinellia), Geongang (乾薑, dried ginger), and Ogomi (五味子, schisandra) for cold-rheum [寒飮] obstructing the Lung, with wheezing and profuse thin sputum.
- **Mahwang Buja Sesin-tang** (麻黃附子細辛湯): Mahwang with Buja (附子, aconite) and Sesin (細辛, Asiasarum) for Shaoyin-stage [少陰] exterior cold patterns with yang deficiency — cold body, deep pulse, and fatigue.

**Preparation Methods:**
The aerial stems (地上莖) are harvested before flowering, dried in the shade. Before use, the **nodes must be removed** [去節, *geo-jeol*], as the nodes are held to inhibit the diaphoretic effect. The herb is typically decocted [煎湯, *jeon-tang*] in water. In some preparations it is first boiled briefly and the foam skimmed off to reduce harsh or irritating properties.

**Conditions Treated:**
- Wind-cold exterior excess [太陽傷寒]: fever without sweating, chills, headache, body aches, nasal congestion, wheezing
- Cold-type asthma and wheezing [寒喘]
- Edema from Lung qi obstruction [風水, wind-water]
- Bi-syndrome [痺證] with cold predominance (joint pain from cold-damp invasion)

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergence from Chinese TCM:**
1. **Constitutional Medicine Integration:** Heo Jun and subsequent Korean physicians, particularly Lee Je-ma (李濟馬) in the Sasang [四象] constitutional framework, noted that Mahwang's powerful diaphoretic action must be calibrated to constitution. It is considered especially suited to the Taeyang-in [太陽人] and Taeeum-in [太陰人] types, and should be used cautiously or avoided in Soeum-in [少陰人] individuals prone to sweating and yang deficiency.
2. **Root vs. Stem distinction:** The Dongui Bogam explicitly emphasizes the medicinal divergence between stem (induces sweat) and root (arrests sweat) — a duality that reflects the Korean tradition's meticulous attention to plant-part specificity, elaborated with greater clinical commentary than many Chinese sources of the period.
3. **Korean Climate Context:** Heo Jun notes that Korea's cold northern climate [北方寒地] makes wind-cold exterior invasions particularly prevalent, lending Mahwang formulas heightened clinical relevance compared to southern Chinese contexts where wind-heat patterns dominate.
4. **Sourcing:** Wild Korean Ephedra (토마황, *to-mahwang*) was harvested from the mountainous northern regions of the peninsula (particularly Hamgyeong and Pyeongan provinces). Heo Jun emphasizes the use of domestically sourced herbs [鄕藥, *hyang-yak*] wherever possible, and Mahwang is listed among the achievable domestic drugs [鄕藥集成方, *Hyangyak Jipseongbang*].

entities_mentioned.name: 마황 / 麻黃 (Mahwang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (and related species E. intermedia, E. equisetina)
entities_mentioned.notes: Aerial stems contain ephedrine and pseudoephedrine alkaloids, providing pharmacological basis for bronchodilatory and diaphoretic actions described. Listed in Korean Pharmacopoeia. Wild populations historically harvested in northern Korean peninsula.

entities_mentioned.name: 마황근 / 麻黃根 (Mahwang-geun, Ephedra root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Root of Ephedra sinica
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacologically distinct from the stem; root contains ephedradines which paradoxically inhibit sweating. The Dongui Bogam's explicit differentiation of root from stem is clinically and pharmacologically accurate.

entities_mentioned.name: 계지 / 桂枝 (Gyeji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia — cinnamon twig
entities_mentioned.notes: Key synergist in Mahwang-tang; warms channels and enhances diaphoresis.

entities_mentioned.name: 행인 / 杏仁 (Haengin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus armeniaca — apricot seed kernel
entities_mentioned.notes: Descends and diffuses Lung qi; paired with Mahwang to address wheezing component.

entities_mentioned.name: 감초 / 甘草 (Gamscho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis — licorice root
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb in virtually all classical formulas; moderates harshness of Mahwang.

entities_mentioned.name: 오미자 / 五味子 (Ogomija)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis — five-flavor berry
entities_mentioned.notes: Particularly associated with Korean medicine; Schisandra grown in Korea (especially Jiri-san region) was considered of superior quality. Paired with Mahwang in Small Blue Dragon Decoction to astringe Lung qi while Mahwang opens it — a dynamic counterbalance.

entities_mentioned.name: 부자 / 附子 (Buja)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii — prepared aconite lateral root
entities_mentioned.notes: Toxic if unprepared; must be processed (포제, *poje*). Used with Mahwang only in yang-deficiency cold patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 세신 / 細辛 (Sesin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum sieboldii or A. heterotropoides — wild ginger/Asiasarum
entities_mentioned.notes: A. heterotropoides var. mandshuricum is native to the Korean peninsula and was sourced domestically as a hyang-yak herb. Strongly warms interior and opens orifices.

entities_mentioned.name: 반하 / 半夏 (Banha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata — pinellia rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Dries dampness and transforms phlegm; essential in cold-rheum formulas alongside Mahwang.

entities_mentioned.name: 건강 / 乾薑 (Geongang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale — dried ginger rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms Lung and Spleen; disperses cold-rheum in combination with Mahwang.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text provided is a condensed botanical label rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam passage; the translation and entry have been reconstructed and substantially expanded from canonical Dongui Bogam content (湯液篇, Tangaek section; 雜病篇, Jabbyeong section) and the Hyangyak Jipseongbang tradition to produce a complete Plantacopia entry consistent with Heo Jun's actual text and method. Quoted classical formula names are attested in the Dongui Bogam. 2. The bidirectional action of stem vs. root (diaphoretic vs. anti-hidrotic) is one of the most frequently cited clinical pearls in Korean traditional medicine education and is explicitly present in the Dongui Bogam. 3. 'Acrid-warm exterior-releasing' classification follows the Dongui Bogam's own drug classification system (藥性歌). 4. The Sasang constitutional medicine reference (Lee Je-ma) postdates the Dongui Bogam by ~280 years (Dongeuisusebowon, 1894 CE) and is flagged here as later Korean tradition building upon Heo Jun's foundation, not as Heo Jun's own text. 5. Modern pharmacological notes (ephedrine content, etc.) are translator additions for scholarly cross-reference and are not claims made in the original source.

results.title_inferred: Mokdan (Moutan Peony Bark) — Treatment of Blood Heat and Stasis
results.original_text: 목단 (牧丹) — 모란 뿌리껍질. 성질은 차고[寒] 맛은 쓰고 매우며[苦辛] 독이 없다. 혈열(血熱)을 내리고 어혈(瘀血)을 흩으며 통경(通經)하고 복중의 징가(癥瘕)를 치료한다. 또한 옹종(癰腫)과 창양(瘡瘍)을 치료하며 골증열(骨蒸熱)을 내린다. 산지는 주로 충청도, 경상도이며 봄·가을에 뿌리껍질을 채취하여 음건(陰乾)한다.
results.translation:
Mokdan [牧丹, moutan peony root bark]. Its nature is cold [寒], its flavor is bitter and acrid [苦辛], and it is non-toxic. It clears blood-heat [血熱], disperses static blood [瘀血, congealed/stagnant blood], opens the menstrual passages [通經], and treats abdominal masses and accumulations [癥瘕]. It also treats abscesses and swellings [癰腫] and sores and ulcerations [瘡瘍], and reduces steaming bone fever [骨蒸熱 — a deep, consumptive heat sensation felt as if rising from the bones, associated with yin deficiency]. The herb is principally found in Chungcheong-do and Gyeongsang-do provinces. The root bark is gathered in spring and autumn and dried in the shade [陰乾].

Key formulas in which Mokdan appears:
1. Yukmijihwang-tang [六味地黃湯] — A foundational yin-supplementing formula in which Mokdan serves to drain ministerial fire [相火] from the kidney channel and clear lurking blood-heat; this formula is central to Korean internal medicine [內科] practice.
2. Gyeji-bokryeong-hwan [桂枝茯苓丸] — Used to gently move blood stasis and dissolve fixed abdominal masses [癥瘕], particularly in gynecological conditions. Mokdan is paired with Jakyak [芍藥], Bokryeong [茯苓], Doin [桃仁], and Gyeji [桂枝].
