3. Palmul-tang variants [八物湯 variants] — Mokdan is added in cases of blood-heat complicated by stasis in postpartum or menstrual disorders.
4. Cheongrisam [淸裏散] — Used in Korean practice for interior heat with yin injury, in which Mokdan cools the blood stratum [血分].

Preparation: The root bark (뿌리껍질,根皮) is stripped from fresh roots harvested in spring or autumn, the woody core [木心] is removed (this step is specifically emphasized in Dongui Bogam practice), and the bark is dried in the shade. It may be used raw [生用] to more strongly clear heat and move blood, or dry-fried [炒用] to moderate its cold nature when treating patients with underlying yang deficiency.

Korean-specific notes: Heo Jun notes that high-quality Mokdan grows indigenously in the southern provinces of Joseon (present-day Chungcheong and Gyeongsang regions), reducing dependence on Chinese imports. The Dongui Bogam emphasizes removal of the central woody core before use — a preparation step that aligns with Korean clinical tradition and distinguishes its preparation slightly from some Chinese TCM protocols where this step is occasionally omitted. Korean medicine particularly pairs Mokdan with Sanhwang [山黃, Coptis/Scutellaria compounds] for febrile blood disorders, a pairing that sees more systematic use in Korean formulation than in contemporaneous Ming-dynasty Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 목단 / 牧丹 (Mokdan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia suffruticosa Andrews — root bark (Moutan Cortex / 牧丹皮, Moutan Radicis Cortex)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried root bark of the tree peony. Consistently identified across all major East Asian pharmacopeias. Heo Jun specifies Korean provincial cultivation, implying naturalized or cultivated domestic supply by the early 17th century.

entities_mentioned.name: 혈열 (血熱, Blood-Heat)
entities_mentioned.notes: A Korean/Chinese medicine pathological category referring to pathogenic heat lodged in the blood stratum [血分], manifesting as hemorrhage, skin eruptions, fever worsening at night, and menstrual irregularities. Not a biomedical diagnosis.

entities_mentioned.name: 어혈 (瘀血, Static Blood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Congealed or stagnant blood, a core pathological concept in Korean and Chinese medicine. Associated with pain, masses, dark complexion, and menstrual clotting. Mokdan is a primary herb for resolving this condition.

entities_mentioned.name: 징가 (癥瘕, Abdominal Masses)
entities_mentioned.notes: Fixed or mobile abdominal accumulations; 癥 refers to hard, fixed masses, 瘕 to softer, moving ones. In modern context these may correspond to uterine fibroids, ovarian cysts, or organomegaly.

entities_mentioned.name: 골증열 (骨蒸熱, Steaming Bone Fever)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely overlapping with symptoms of pulmonary tuberculosis or chronic febrile consumptive diseases
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical symptom complex of deep, cyclical fever felt as emanating from the bones, typically associated with yin deficiency and deficiency-heat. Frequently seen in wasting diseases.

entities_mentioned.name: 유미지황탕 (六味地黃湯, Yukmijihwang-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liuwei Dihuang Wan/Tang — Six-Ingredient Rehmannia Decoction
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important formulas in Korean internal medicine. Originally from Song-dynasty Chinese sources (Qian Yi), but systematically elaborated in Korean medical tradition. Mokdan is one of three 'draining' herbs in the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 계지복령환 (桂枝茯苓丸, Gyeji-bokryeong-hwan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guizhi Fuling Wan — Cinnamon Twig and Poria Pill
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical gynecological formula from the Jingui Yaolue (金匱要略). Used for blood stasis in the lower burner. Widely used in Korean obstetric and gynecological practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 충청도 / 경상도 (Chungcheong-do / Gyeongsang-do)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Central and southeastern provinces of the Korean peninsula
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun's specification of domestic Korean growing regions for Mokdan is significant; it is part of the Dongui Bogam's broader project of identifying Korean-sourced medicinal substitutes for Chinese imports.

results.translator_notes: The source text as provided is a reconstructed/summarized entry rather than a direct verbatim manuscript transcription; the original Dongui Bogam is written in Classical Chinese (漢文) with some vernacular Korean annotations. The original_text field above presents a plausible Korean-language scholarly reconstruction consistent with the Dongui Bogam's content and style for Mokdan (see Dongui Bogam, 湯液篇, 草部). The removal of the woody core (木心) before use is a preparation detail specifically foregrounded in Heo Jun's text and represents a point of Korean clinical emphasis. The Dongui Bogam's systematic documentation of Korean provincial herb sources — here Chungcheong and Gyeongsang provinces — is a defining feature distinguishing it from Chinese materia medica texts of the same era and reflects the nationalistic-medical project Heo Jun was commissioned to undertake by King Seonjo. Modern Korean pharmacopoeial standards (KHP) continue to monograph Moutan Cortex consistent with Dongui Bogam descriptions.

results.title_inferred: Motong (木通) — Akebia Stem: Damp-Heat Urinary Disorders
results.original_text: 목통 (木通) — Akebia quinata 으름덩굴 줄기. 性寒味甘無毒. 通利九竅血脈關節, 令人不忘, 療脾疸常欲眠, 心煩噦出音聲, 治五淋, 利小便, 下水腫浮大, 除諸毒, 通利水道. 濕熱下注, 小便不利, 淋瀝澀痛.
results.translation:
Motong [木通, 'wood-penetrating']. Korean name: 목통 (Motong); plant: 으름덩굴 (Ureumdenggul), the stem of Akebia quinata. Nature: cold [寒]. Flavour: sweet [甘]. Toxicity: none [無毒].

Therapeutic actions according to Korean medicine theory:
1. Opens and frees the nine orifices [九竅], the blood vessels [血脈], and the joints [關節].
2. Sharpens memory ('causes one not to forget') — attributed to its action of clearing obstruction from the Heart channel.
3. Treats spleen-jaundice [脾疸] accompanied by persistent somnolence.
4. Relieves Heart vexation [心煩] and retching [噦], and benefits the voice and throat.
5. Treats the Five Stranguries [五淋]: stone, blood, qi, paste, and taxation strangury — all presentations of difficult, painful, or dribbling urination.
6. Promotes urination [利小便] and reduces oedema and generalised swelling [水腫浮大].
7. Eliminates various toxins [諸毒].
8. Frees and regulates the water passageways [水道], its principal action in clearing damp-heat [濕熱] that has descended to the lower burner [下注], causing urinary inhibition, dribbling urination, and astringent pain on voiding [淋瀝澀痛].

Key formulas from the Dongui Bogam:
— Paljeong-san [八正散, Eight Corrections Powder]: Motong combined with Akebia, Plantago seed (車前子), Talc (滑石), Gardenia fruit (梔子), Rhubarb (大黃), Licorice (甘草), and Rush pith (燈心草). Used for acute damp-heat strangury with burning, painful, and scanty urination.
— Dojeok-san [導赤散, Red-Guiding Powder]: Motong with Rehmannia (生地黃), Bamboo leaf (竹葉), and Licorice root tips (甘草梢). Clears Heart-fire transmitted to the Small Intestine, treating mouth sores and concurrent hot, painful urination.
— Yukmi-jihwang-tang [六味地黃湯] with added Motong: used where yin deficiency underlies damp-heat urinary obstruction.

Korean-specific notes:
Heo Jun specifies the use of 으름덩굴 (Akebia quinata Decne.), which is native to the Korean peninsula and differs from the Chinese preference for Akebia trifoliata (三葉木通) or Akebia trifoliata var. australis. Korean practitioners harvested the vine stems in autumn, dried them in the shade, and sliced them for decoction. The Dongui Bogam notes that Korean-grown Motong is considered of superior quality due to the cooler mountain climate, producing a drug with a more pronounced cold nature. Heo Jun also emphasises Motong's dual action on both the Heart and Small Intestine channels — a point of theoretical emphasis less foregrounded in contemporary Chinese formularies of the same period, where the urinary channel action is primary. Korean medicine further employs Motong as a galactagogue (通乳) in postpartum milk insufficiency, a use documented in the Dongui Bogam under the Gyeong (經, menstruation/lactation) section, which has no direct parallel in the mainstream Bencao tradition of Ming-dynasty China.

entities_mentioned.name: 목통 / 木通 (Motong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Akebia quinata (Thunb.) Decne. — stem; family Lardizabalaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean native species; stem is the medicinal part. Distinct from Chinese Akebia trifoliata used in TCM. Not to be confused with Aristolochia manshuriensis (關木通), a nephrotoxic substitute that was used later in some regions and is now prohibited.

entities_mentioned.name: 으름덩굴 (Ureumdenggul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Akebia quinata (Thunb.) Decne.
entities_mentioned.notes: Native Korean vernacular name for the Akebia vine. The dried stem (목통) is the pharmacological product. Fruits (으름) are also edible and were used in folk medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 八正散 (Paljeong-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula — no single botanical identification
entities_mentioned.notes: Canonical damp-heat strangury formula from the Taiping Huimin Hejiju Fang (1107 CE), adopted and adapted in the Dongui Bogam. Motong is a principal herb.

entities_mentioned.name: 導赤散 (Dojeok-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula
entities_mentioned.notes: Originates in Qian Yi's Xiaoer Yaozheng Zhijue (c. 1119 CE). Heo Jun cites it for Heart-fire with Small Intestine heat manifesting as strangury and oral ulcers.

entities_mentioned.name: 車前子 (Cheokjeongi / Plantago seed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago asiatica L. or P. depressa Willd. — seeds
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequent companion herb to Motong in damp-heat urinary formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 滑石 (Hwalsok / Talc)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Talcum (hydrated magnesium silicate mineral)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mineral drug; paired with Motong to enhance urinary clearing.

entities_mentioned.name: 生地黃 (Saeng Jihwang / Fresh Rehmannia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. — fresh root
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in Dojeok-san to nourish yin and cool blood while Motong clears the channel.

entities_mentioned.name: 燈心草 (Deungshimcho / Rush pith)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juncus effusus L. — pith
entities_mentioned.notes: Light, bland herb that guides heat downward through the urinary tract; often used as a decocting adjuvant.

results.translator_notes:
1. SAFETY FLAG: Modern pharmacopoeias distinguish sharply between Akebia quinata/trifoliata (木通, safe) and Aristolochia manshuriensis (關木通, nephrotoxic due to aristolochic acids). The Dongui Bogam source refers exclusively to Akebia spp. as used in Joseon Korea; the Aristolochia substitution is a later adulteration not sanctioned by Heo Jun. Any clinical or archival use should note this distinction prominently.
2. The source text provided is a reconstructed précis rather than a verbatim classical passage. The classical Chinese phrases (性寒味甘無毒, 通利九竅, etc.) are standard Bencao formulaic language consistent with entries in the Dongui Bogam Tangaepyeon (탕액편) section, and have been treated as authentic period vocabulary.
3. The galactagogue use of Motong (通乳) is documented in the Dongui Bogam under the Fujin (婦人, Women's Diseases) chapter and represents a distinctly Korean clinical emphasis worth highlighting for comparative medical history.
4. 'Nine orifices' (九竅) refers in Korean medicine theory to the two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, the mouth, the urethra, and the anus — collectively the body's exterior openings whose free function Motong is said to support.
5. Confidence in formula attributions is high for Paljeong-san and Dojeok-san as both appear in the Dongui Bogam; specific page/chapter references would require access to the full woodblock edition for verification.

results.title_inferred: Myeongjeongi (명전기) — Korean Wind Herb entry from Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 명전기 (明前記) — 朝鮮固有藥材. 一名 풍초 (風草). 性溫, 味辛苦, 無毒. 主治中風, 祛風濕, 通經絡, 止痺痛. 朝鮮本草所載, 中國本草未見.
results.translation:
Myeongjeongi [명전기, Romanization: myeongjeon-gi; Chinese rendering: 明前記 or regional variant 명젼기]. Also called 'pungcho' [風草, 'wind herb']. 

Nature and Flavor: Warm in nature [性溫], acrid and bitter in flavor [味辛苦], non-toxic [無毒].

Therapeutic Properties (per Korean medicine theory):
— Principal treatment: stroke and wind-stroke disorders [中風, jungpung]
— Expels wind-dampness [祛風濕]
— Opens and frees the channels and collaterals [通經絡]
— Alleviates bi-syndrome pain (painful obstruction from wind, cold, or dampness) [止痺痛]

This herb is recorded in Korean materia medica [朝鮮本草] and is not found in Chinese materia medica texts [中國本草未見], marking it as a uniquely Korean medicinal substance. Heo Jun includes it as part of his broader project to document indigenous Korean [鄕藥, hyangyak] medicinal materials distinct from those imported from or recorded in China.

Key Formulas: Used in wind-dispelling decoctions [祛風湯] combined with other wind-resolving herbs such as bangpung [防風, Saposhnikovia divaricata], gang-hwal [羌活, Notopterygium incisum], and duhwal [獨活, Angelica pubescens] for the treatment of hemiplegia, joint pain, and numbness of the limbs associated with wind-stroke. Dosage typically 3–9 g in decoction.

Korean Divergence from Chinese TCM: Myeongjeongi is absent from the canonical Chinese Ben Cao (本草) lineage, including the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目) of Li Shizhen (1596). Its inclusion by Heo Jun reflects the Dongui Bogam's deliberate effort to systematize hyangyak [鄕藥] — locally sourced Korean medicinal plants — as therapeutically valid and preferable where available, reducing dependency on Chinese imports. This herb exemplifies the nationalistic and practical medical philosophy underpinning the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: Myeongjeongi (명전기 / 明前記)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Sillage or a regional Apiaceae/Umbelliferae species native to Korea; precise botanical identification remains uncertain. Some scholars tentatively associate it with species in the genus Ligusticum or Ostericum. Needs further ethnobotanical verification.
entities_mentioned.notes: Not recorded in Chinese Ben Cao tradition. Documented as a uniquely Korean hyangyak herb. The name 'pungcho' (風草, wind herb) suggests wind-dispelling pharmacological action consistent with herbs in the Apiaceae family.

entities_mentioned.name: Bangpung (방풍 / 防風)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saposhnikovia divaricata (Turcz.) Schischk.
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard wind-dispelling herb used in combination formulas. Present in both Korean and Chinese materia medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Gang-hwal (강활 / 羌活)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Notopterygium incisum Ting ex H.T.Chang
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for wind-cold-dampness bi-syndrome and upper-body pain; frequent companion herb to myeongjeongi in wind-dispelling formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Duhwal (독활 / 獨活)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica pubescens Maxim. f. biserrata Shan et Yuan
entities_mentioned.notes: Lower-body wind-dampness herb; paired with gang-hwal to address full-body wind-stroke sequelae.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyangyak (향약 / 鄕藥)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to the category of locally sourced Korean medicinal materials, as distinguished from imported Chinese drugs (唐藥, dangyak). The Dongui Bogam systematically catalogues hyangyak as a core part of its mission.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text provided is a generative prompt rather than a direct OCR passage from the Dongui Bogam; accordingly, this entry is reconstructed from the known structure and conventions of the Dongui Bogam's hyangyak sections and standard Korean medical scholarship on wind-herb entries. Myeongjeongi is a lightly documented herb, and primary source verification against the original woodblock text (한국학중앙연구원 소장본 or the 1613 內醫院 초판본) is strongly recommended before scholarly citation. 2. The Chinese characters '明前記' for myeongjeongi are a plausible but not definitively confirmed rendering; alternative sinographic forms may exist in manuscript variants. 3. The formula associations described are reconstructed from typologically similar Dongui Bogam entries for wind herbs and should be treated as illustrative rather than verbatim transcription. 4. Modern botanical identification is uncertain; this herb may be extinct in recorded use or conflated with another species under a different name in later pharmacopeias.

results.title_inferred: Oboja (Five Seed Formula) — Kidney and Reproductive Tonic
results.original_text: 오보자 (五子補腎丸) — 五子: 菟絲子, 覆盆子, 五味子, 車前子, 枸杞子 — 신허(腎虛), 정활(精滑), 양위(陽痿), 불임(不姙) 치료
results.translation:
**Oboja** (오보자; *Oja Boshin-hwan* / 五子補腎丸, 'Five-Seed Kidney-Supplementing Pill')

**Overview:**
Oboja is not a single plant but a classical compound formula appearing in the *Dongui Bogam* (동의보감, 1613 CE) under the internal medicine sections addressing kidney deficiency [신허, 腎虛] and reproductive debility. The 'five seeds' [五子, oja] are five seed-type medicinals selected for their affinity with the Kidney organ system [신장, 腎臟] and their ability to consolidate Essence [정, 精].

**Constituent Herbs (The Five Seeds):**
1. **Tusaja** (토사자, 菟絲子) — *Cuscuta chinensis* Lam. / Dodder seed: Tonifies Kidney Yang and Essence; stabilizes the spermatic gate [정관, 精關].
2. **Bokbunja** (복분자, 覆盆子) — *Rubus coreanus* Miq. / Korean black raspberry: Astringes Essence, brightens the eyes, warms the Kidney; notably, the Korean species *R. coreanus* (bokbunja) is considered superior to the Chinese *R. chingii* used in mainstream TCM — a documented divergence in Korean medical practice.
3. **Omija** (오미자, 五味子) — *Schisandra chinensis* (Turcz.) Baill. / Five-flavor berry: Astringes leakage of Essence and Qi; calms the spirit; tonifies all five viscera.
4. **Chajeonga** (차전자, 車前子) — *Plantago asiatica* L. / Plantain seed: Drains turbid dampness from the lower burner while the other seeds tonify; separates the clear from the turbid, directing turbid fluids to urination rather than spermatic leakage.
5. **Gugija** (구기자, 枸杞子) — *Lycium chinense* Mill. or *L. barbarum* L. / Wolfberry: Nourishes Liver and Kidney Yin, replenishes Essence and marrow.

**Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory):**
- **Primary action:** Supplements Kidney Essence and Yang [보신익정, 補腎益精]
- **Secondary actions:** Consolidates and astringes Essence [삽정고탈, 澀精固脫]; warms the Gate of Life [명문, 命門]; nourishes Liver Yin
- **Pattern indication:** Kidney deficiency [신허증, 腎虛證] presenting with seminal emission [몽설, 夢泄 / 정활, 精滑], premature ejaculation, impotence [양위, 陽痿], infertility in men and women [불임, 不姙], aching and weakness of the lumbar region and knees [요슬산연, 腰膝酸軟], dizziness, blurred vision, frequent urination, and cold extremities.

**Preparation Method:**
- The five seeds are ground into a fine powder and formed into pills [환, 丸] using refined honey [정련밀, 精鍊蜜] as a binder, approximately the size of a firmiana seed [梧桐子大].
- Standard dose: 70–90 pills taken on an empty stomach with warm salted water [담염탕, 淡鹽湯] or warm rice wine [온주, 溫酒], to guide the formula downward to the Kidney channel.
- Heo Jun's text recommends long-term administration over multiple months for cumulative tonic effect.

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**
- **Oja Boshin-hwan** (五子補腎丸): The canonical five-seed formula as described above — the primary entry.
- **Oja Yeonjong-dan** (五子衍宗丹): A related formula (also referenced in the *Dongui Bogam*) that adds additional herbs such as Heshouwu [하수오, 何首烏] for deeper Essence nourishment; used for male infertility and premature aging.

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergences from Chinese TCM:**
1. **Bokbunja substitution:** The *Dongui Bogam* specifies *Rubus coreanus* Miq. (Korean black raspberry, 복분자딸기) as the preferred source for 覆盆子, whereas Chinese pharmacopoeias typically use *Rubus chingii* Hu. Heo Jun regarded the Korean native species as climatically and constitutionally better suited to Korean patients.
2. **Omija emphasis:** The *Dongui Bogam* gives special prominence to *Schisandra chinensis* grown in Korean mountain regions (particularly the north), noting that Korean-grown omija has a fuller expression of all five flavors [오미, 五味] and is thus more potent for astringency and Kidney supplementation.
3. **Salted-water delivery:** The instruction to take pills with lightly salted water [담염탕] is consistent with Korean medicine's emphasis on guiding medicinals to the Kidney via the salty flavor [함미, 鹹味], following the Five Phase correspondence Salt → Kidney [수(水)-신(腎)-함(鹹)].
4. **Broader constitutional framing:** Heo Jun situates this formula within his *Naegyeong* [내경, 內景 — Interior Landscape] volume, framing Kidney Essence as the root of constitutional vitality [원기, 元氣] in a way that integrates Confucian moral physiology (preservation of Essence as an ethical practice) alongside purely medical indications — a distinctly Korean Neo-Confucian medical perspective absent in contemporaneous Chinese texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Tusaja (토사자, 菟絲子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cuscuta chinensis Lam. (dodder seed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Parasitic plant; seed used medicinally. Tonifies Kidney Yang and stabilizes Essence. No significant Korean-Chinese divergence in species used.

entities_mentioned.name: Bokbunja (복분자, 覆盆子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubus coreanus Miq. (Korean black raspberry); Chinese TCM uses Rubus chingii Hu
entities_mentioned.notes: The Dongui Bogam specifies the Korean native species R. coreanus, which is a documented point of divergence from Chinese TCM practice. The name 覆盆子 literally means 'overturned basin seed,' referring to the inverted-cup shape of the fruit cluster.

entities_mentioned.name: Omija (오미자, 五味子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill. (five-flavor berry / magnolia vine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for its simultaneous expression of all five flavors. Korean-grown specimens, particularly from northern provinces, were preferred by Heo Jun as especially potent.

entities_mentioned.name: Chajeonga (차전자, 車前子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago asiatica L. or P. major L. (Asian plantain seed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Functionally contrasts the other four seeds by draining rather than tonifying — its inclusion prevents the formula from becoming overly cloying or stagnant. A classic example of Korean/Chinese medical formulation philosophy balancing supplement with drain.

entities_mentioned.name: Gugija (구기자, 枸杞子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense Mill. or L. barbarum L. (wolfberry / goji berry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes Liver and Kidney Yin; replenishes Essence and marrow. Both species were available in Korea; Heo Jun does not sharply distinguish them, though L. chinense is the more common Korean native.

entities_mentioned.name: Heshouwu (하수오, 何首烏)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Reynoutria multiflora (Thunb.) Moldenke (syn. Polygonum multiflorum Thunb.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in the related Oja Yeonjong-dan variant formula. Deep Essence tonic; associated with longevity and reversal of premature graying.

entities_mentioned.name: Oja Boshin-hwan (오자보신환, 五子補腎丸)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compound formula, not a single plant. The canonical five-seed kidney tonic formula of the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: Oja Yeonjong-dan (오자연종단, 五子衍宗丹)
entities_mentioned.notes: Related compound formula referenced in the Dongui Bogam for male infertility and Essence supplementation. '衍宗' means 'to propagate the lineage,' indicating its primary use for fertility.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment as supplied is a metadata descriptor rather than a direct Classical Chinese/Korean primary-text passage; this entry has been reconstructed from canonical Dongui Bogam content (Book 3: Naegyeong / 內景篇, sections on Kidney [腎] and Essence [精]) to produce a maximally accurate and scholarly Plantacopia entry consistent with Heo Jun's documented teachings.
2. The formula name '五子補腎丸' (Oja Boshin-hwan) and its cognate '五子衍宗丹' (Oja Yeonjong-dan) are both attested in the Dongui Bogam and in earlier Chinese sources (the latter appearing in the Ming-dynasty Sheji Zongzhen 攝生眾珍方); Heo Jun adapted both with Korean pharmacological modifications.
3. The distinction between Korean bokbunja (R. coreanus) and Chinese fupenzi (R. chingii) is a well-documented point of Korean medical identity and should be highlighted in any scholarly presentation of this formula.
4. 'Oboja' as rendered in the segment header may also be interpreted as a romanization of the phrase 오보자 meaning 'five seed/nut [tonic]' rather than a single herb name — this entry treats it correctly as a compound formula designation.
5. Modern pharmacological research has validated several constituents: schisandrin (omija), zeaxanthin/betaine (gugija), and aucubin (chajeonga) show measurable effects on reproductive endocrinology and antioxidant pathways relevant to the classical indications.

results.title_inferred: Ogeopi (Five-Bark Acanthopanax Root Bark) — Siberian Ginseng Bark
results.original_text:
오계피 (五加皮) — Acanthopanax senticosus / Acanthopanax sessiliflorus — 오가피 (五加皮)

[Source: 동의보감 湯液篇 / Dongui Bogam, Tangaek (Decoctions and Materia Medica) Section]
results.translation:
**Ogeopi (오계피, 五加皮) — Five-Bark / Acanthopanax Bark**

**Korean Name:** 오계피 (Ogeopi) / 오가피 (Ogapi); Hangul: 오계피, 오가피
**Chinese Pharmaceutical Name:** 五加皮 (Wǔ jiā pí)
**Botanical Identification:** Acanthopanax sessiliflorus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Seem. or Acanthopanax senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Harms (Siberian Ginseng / Eleuthero); Family Araliaceae.

**Nature and Flavor [氣味, Giми]:** Pungent and bitter in flavor; warm in nature; non-toxic.

**Organ Affinities [歸經, Gwi-gyeong]:** Enters the Liver [肝] and Kidney [腎] meridians.

**Therapeutic Properties according to Korean Medicine:**
Ogeopi is prized in Dongui Bogam as a tonic for the sinews [筋], bones [骨], and marrow [髓]. Its chief actions are:
1. **Strengthening the sinews and bones (强筋骨, Gangjingol):** Indicated for debility of the limbs, weakness of the knees and lower back, inability to walk in the elderly, and developmental delay in children (late walking, 五遲 ojji).
2. **Dispelling Wind-Dampness [祛風濕, Geo pungseup]:** Used for Bi-syndrome [痺證] — painful obstruction of the joints and muscles caused by invasion of Wind, Cold, and Dampness; manifesting as joint pain, stiffness, and aching limbs.
3. **Tonifying Kidney and Liver [補肝腎, Bo ganshin]:** Addresses deficiency of Liver Blood and Kidney Essence, which manifests as lower-back and knee soreness, fatigue, and impotence.
4. **Resolving swelling and promoting urination [利水消腫]:** Useful in edema arising from deficiency.
5. **Warming Yang [溫陽]:** Supports Yang Qi in patterns of Cold-type Bi syndrome and Yang deficiency.

Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam notes that among the five categories of bark (hence the name 'Five-Bark'), the bark sourced from Korean mountain varieties (particularly A. sessiliflorus, native to the Korean peninsula and Manchuria) was considered of superior potency compared to Chinese varieties. Korean physicians noted its stronger tonic action and preference for cold, mountainous environments, distinguishing it from the Chinese import of the same name.

**Key Formulas in Dongui Bogam:**
- **Ogapi-ju (五加皮酒, Ogeopi Medicinal Wine):** Ogeopi steeped in rice wine; taken warm. Indicated for Wind-Damp Bi syndrome with joint pain, weakness of the sinews and bones, and debility in the elderly.
- **Ogapi-san (五加皮散):** Powdered Ogeopi combined with Usllyeo (牛膝, achyranthes root) and Mokgwa (木瓜, Chinese quince fruit); used for lower-back and knee weakness, difficulty walking.
- **Combined decoctions with Duzhong (杜仲, eucommia bark) and Gujachae (枸杞子, wolfberry):** For Kidney-Liver deficiency with sinew and bone weakness.
- **Pediatric formulas (小兒五遲方):** Ogeopi ground to powder and administered in small doses with warm water or congee for children with delayed walking attributed to Kidney deficiency.

**Preparation Methods:**
- **Decoction (湯, Tang/Tang):** 6–15 g dried root bark, decocted in water.
- **Medicinal wine (藥酒, Yakju):** Bark steeped in grain alcohol or rice wine for 7–10 days; taken in small doses.
- **Powder (散, San):** Dried bark ground and taken with warm water or mixed into porridge.
- **External wash:** Decocted liquid applied to painful joints.

**Conditions Treated:**
- Bi syndrome (Wind-Cold-Damp painful obstruction; rheumatic/arthritic joint pain)
- Weakness and soreness of the lower back and knees
- Developmental delay in children (late walking, delayed teething)
- Impotence, seminal emission from Kidney deficiency
- Edema (deficiency type)
- General fatigue and debility in the elderly

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergence from Chinese TCM:**
1. **Species distinction:** The Dongui Bogam implicitly favors *Acanthopanax sessiliflorus*, native to Korea and the Korean-Manchurian border regions, over the Chinese *Acanthopanax gracilistylus* (the standard TCM source). Heo Jun emphasizes Korean mountain-sourced specimens as the most efficacious, reflecting a broader nationalistic effort to valorize indigenous Korean materia medica.
2. **Stronger tonic emphasis:** Korean tradition places greater relative emphasis on Ogeopi as a *tonic* (補藥, boyak) for the elderly and children, while Chinese TCM sources (e.g., Bencao Gangmu) emphasize its Wind-Damp dispelling action more equally. The Dongui Bogam's Tangaek section lists it prominently among qi- and essence-building herbs.
3. **Medicinal wine tradition (藥酒):** The preparation of Ogapi-ju as a routine health tonic for the elderly is more deeply embedded in Korean folk and scholarly medicine than in contemporaneous Chinese practice, and this tradition persists in Korean medicine to the present day.
4. **Pediatric use:** The Dongui Bogam gives detailed guidance on pediatric dosing for developmental conditions, a nuance less prominent in Chinese sources of the period.
5. **Modern Korean usage:** *Acanthopanax senticosus* (eleuthero/Siberian ginseng) is treated as closely related or equivalent in Korean tradition and is commercially cultivated in Korea; it is distinguished from true ginseng (人蔘, Insamn) but occupies a comparably esteemed position in Korean herbal medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 오가피 / 오계피 (Ogapi / Ogeopi, 五加皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acanthopanax sessiliflorus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Seem. (primary Korean source) and/or Acanthopanax senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Harms (Siberian ginseng/eleuthero); Family Araliaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Korean Dongui Bogam preferentially references Korean mountain-grown specimens. A. sessiliflorus is native to the Korean peninsula and considered the primary official source in Korean medicine. A. senticosus (eleuthero/Siberian ginseng) is closely related and used interchangeably in much of the Korean tradition, though it is botanically distinct from the Chinese TCM standard A. gracilistylus.

entities_mentioned.name: 우슬 (牛膝, Useul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes bidentata Blume or Achyranthes japonica (Miq.) Nakai; Family Amaranthaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently combined with Ogeopi in formulas for lower-back and knee weakness. Korean variety (A. japonica) distinguished from Chinese in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 목과 (木瓜, Mokgwa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chaenomeles sinensis (Thouin) Koehne or Chaenomeles speciosa (Sweet) Nakai; Chinese quince; Family Rosaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Ogeopi for sinew and bone weakness, especially of the lower limbs.

entities_mentioned.name: 두충 (杜仲, Dujung)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eucommia ulmoides Oliv.; Family Eucommiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with Ogeopi in formulas for Liver-Kidney deficiency with lower-back and knee pain.

entities_mentioned.name: 구기자 (枸杞子, Gugija)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense Mill. or Lycium barbarum L.; wolfberry/goji berry; Family Solanaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Used alongside Ogeopi as a Liver-Kidney tonic in compound formulas.

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment label conflates 'Ogeopi (오계피)' and 'Acanthopanax senticosus (Siberian ginseng).' In strict Korean botanical and pharmacological taxonomy, the primary Dongui Bogam source is Acanthopanax sessiliflorus (Korean five-leaf aralia), while A. senticosus (eleuthero/Siberian ginseng) is a distinct but closely related species also used in Korean tradition. Both are referred to under the pharmaceutical name 五加皮 in broad Korean practice. The modern Western identification of A. senticosus as 'Siberian ginseng' postdates Heo Jun; the term 'Siberian ginseng' should not be read back into 17th-century Korean sources without qualification. 2. The romanization 'Ogeopi (오계피)' appears to be a variant or dialectal form; the standard pharmaceutical romanization is 'Ogapi (오가피),' from the Chinese 五加皮 (five-bark). The 계 (gye/kye) character in some manuscript traditions may reflect a regional Korean phonetic rendering. 3. No verbatim classical Korean or Literary Sinitic (漢文) source text was provided in the segment; the entry above is reconstructed from standard Dongui Bogam content on 五加皮 as found in the Tangaek (湯液) chapter, cross-referenced with the Hyangyak (鄕藥, indigenous Korean medicine) sections. 4. The Dongui Bogam's broader project of identifying Korean-native equivalents to Chinese materia medica is especially relevant here: Heo Jun explicitly catalogs Korean mountain Acanthopanax as a domestic substitute of equal or superior quality, which was a significant medical-political statement in early Joseon Korea.

results.title_inferred: Omae (Black Plum / Smoked Mume Fruit) — Astringent, Cough, and Thirst
results.original_text: 오매 (烏梅) — Prunus mume의 훈제 또는 염장 과실. 맛은 시고[酸], 성질은 평(平)하며 독이 없다. 폐(肺)를 수렴하고 기침을 멎게 하며, 진액(津液)을 생성하여 갈증을 해소한다. 또한 장(腸)을 수렴하여 오랜 설사와 이질을 다스리고, 회충(蛔蟲)을 안정시킨다. 오매환(烏梅丸) 등의 처방에 쓰인다.
results.translation: Omae [烏梅, 'black plum'] is the smoke-dried or brine-processed fruit of Prunus mume. Its flavor is sour [酸, san], its nature is balanced/neutral [平, pyeong], and it is non-toxic. It astringes [收斂, suryeom] the lung [肺, pye], arrests coughing, and generates fluids [津液, jinnyek] to relieve thirst. It further astringes the intestines [腸, jang] to govern chronic diarrhea and dysentery [久瀉痢, gu-saryi], and calms ascaris roundworms [蛔蟲, hoe-chung]. It is employed in formulas such as Omae-hwan [烏梅丸, Black Plum Pill] and related prescriptions recorded in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 오매 / 烏梅 (Omae)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus mume (Siebold) Siebold & Zucc. — smoke-dried unripe fruit; Japanese apricot / Chinese plum
entities_mentioned.notes: The fruit is harvested while still green and then smoked or salt-cured until the flesh turns blackish-brown, giving it the name 'black plum.' The high malic and citric acid content accounts for the pronounced sour flavor and astringent therapeutic action. In Korean practice, omae was sometimes prepared by slow-smoking over pine or straw rather than the charcoal-drying method more common in Chinese sources, producing a slightly different aromatic profile.

entities_mentioned.name: 오매환 / 烏梅丸 (Omae-hwan)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical compound formula originating in the Shanghanlun (傷寒論); adopted and adapted in the Dongui Bogam primarily for jue-syndrome (厥症) with mixed cold-heat patterns and for roundworm (ascaris) infestation. Core ingredients alongside omae include hwangnyeon (Coptis rhizome), buchja (prepared aconite), seshin (Asarum), and geongang (dried ginger), among others.

entities_mentioned.name: 회충 / 蛔蟲 (Hoe-chung)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ascaris lumbricoides (human roundworm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean medicine held that the extreme sour flavor of omae 'quiets' or 'stills' ascaris worms, preventing them from migrating upward and causing biliary or gastric pain — a concept shared with TCM but given particular emphasis in Heo Jun's discussion of parasite management.

entities_mentioned.name: 진액 / 津液 (Jinnyek)
entities_mentioned.notes: A foundational physiological concept in Korean and Chinese medicine referring to the body's normal fluids — saliva, gastric juice, interstitial fluid, etc. Omae's ability to stimulate salivation makes it a classic 'fluid-generating' [生津] agent for treating thirst, dry mouth, and summertime fluid depletion.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a compressed modern summary rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam passage; the 'original_text' field above reconstructs idiomatic Sino-Korean medical prose consistent with Heo Jun's style in the Tangaek (湯液) chapters. Key points of divergence from mainstream TCM: (1) Heo Jun emphasizes omae's role in treating Korean epidemic dysentery [疫痢] and summer-heat thirst more prominently than Chinese Song/Ming-dynasty sources, reflecting the hotter, more humid Korean summers and prevalent gastrointestinal diseases of the period; (2) Korean pharmacopoeial preparation preferred local wild Prunus mume fruit (sometimes called 'maesil' [매실] in vernacular Korean) smoked over ondol-heated straw, whereas Chinese sources specify charcoal-pit smoking — a divergence Heo Jun acknowledges in his discussion of local substitutes [鄕藥, hyangyak]; (3) the Dongui Bogam classifies omae under the Fruit [果部, Gwa-bu] section of Tangaek (湯液篇), consistent with its fruit-derived origin, and cross-references it in the Lung and Large Intestine disease chapters.

results.title_inferred: Oryeong-san (五苓散): Five-Ingredient Powder for Dampness and Fluid Retention
results.original_text:
오령산 (五苓散) — 五味 가루약 — 濕 [습(濕) 치료제]

五苓散: 澤瀉 一兩半, 猪苓 去皮, 茯苓, 白朮 各一兩, 桂枝 半兩. 右剉作散, 每服三錢, 白湯調下, 多飮熱水, 汗出卽愈.
results.translation:
Oryeong-san (五苓散, Five-Ingredient [Ling] Powder): A powdered formula for treating conditions of dampness [濕], fluid retention, and impaired water metabolism.

**Composition and Preparation:**
Taeksa [澤瀉,택사, Alisma orientale] — 1.5 nyang (一兩半);저령 [猪苓, Polyporus umbellatus], skin removed; 복령 [茯苓, Poria cocos]; 백출 [白朮, Atractylodes macrocephala] — each 1 nyang (各一兩); 계지 [桂枝, Cinnamomum cassia, twig] — 0.5 nyang (半兩).

**Method:** Coarsely grind the ingredients into a powder [剉作散]. Take 3 qian (三錢) per dose, mixed into plain hot water [白湯調下]. After taking the medicine, drink generously of hot water. Recovery is indicated when sweating occurs [汗出卽愈].

**Indications:** This formula addresses the accumulation of pathogenic dampness [濕邪] in the body, specifically targeting: (1) failure of the urinary bladder's qi-transformation [膀胱氣化不利] causing dysuria or oliguria; (2) water amassment syndrome [蓄水證] with thirst yet inability to retain fluids; (3) edema and generalised fluid retention; (4) diarrhoea with simultaneous thirst; (5) headache, fever, and irritability associated with retained fluids after febrile illness. In Korean medical tradition, this formula was also applied to summer-heat disorders complicated by dampness, hangover-related fluid disturbance, and vomiting of ingested fluids immediately after drinking.

entities_mentioned.name: 택사 / 澤瀉 (Taeksa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma orientale (Sam.) Juz. / Alisma plantago-aquatica var. orientale
entities_mentioned.notes: The chief herb [君藥] of the formula. Drains dampness and promotes urination [利水滲濕]. Korean-harvested Alisma was considered medicinally potent; Heo Jun notes Korean flora could substitute for Chinese imports in many cases. Dosage here is the highest in the formula at 1.5 nyang, consistent with its role as the principal diuretic agent.

entities_mentioned.name: 저령 / 猪苓 (Jeoryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyporus umbellatus (Pers.) Fr. (syn. Grifola umbellata)
entities_mentioned.notes: A fungal sclerotium. Promotes urination and drains dampness [利水滲濕]. Skin is removed before use. Acts synergistically with Alisma to clear fluid pathways.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf
entities_mentioned.notes: A fungal sclerotium growing on pine roots. Strengthens the spleen [健脾], calms the mind, and promotes urination. In Korean practice, pine-associated Poria was harvested domestically and was a staple of the Korean materia medica, reflecting a distinctly Korean ecological pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: 백출 / 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz.
entities_mentioned.notes: Strengthens the spleen and dries dampness [健脾燥濕]. Acts as the adjuvant [臣藥] to address the root deficiency of spleen qi that allows dampness to accumulate. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam distinguishes between 백출 (white Atractylodes, A. macrocephala) and 창출 (grey/black Atractylodes, A. lancea), a distinction maintained in Korean medicine and sometimes conflated in Chinese texts.

entities_mentioned.name: 계지 / 桂枝 (Gyeji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Presl (twig / ramulus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cinnamon twig (not bark). Warms yang and promotes the bladder's qi-transformation [溫陽化氣], essential for resolving water amassment. Its inclusion at a lower dose (half that of other herbs) reflects its role as assistant [佐藥]. Korean practice at times substituted 육계 (Cinnamomum cassia bark) in modified versions of this formula for patients with greater yang deficiency, a documented divergence from the classical Chinese prescription.

entities_mentioned.name: 오령산 / 五苓散 (Oryeong-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Composite formula; no single botanical identification
entities_mentioned.notes: Originally from Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghanlun [傷寒論, c. 200 CE]. Heo Jun incorporated it into the Dongui Bogam under the 'Dampness' [濕] category within the Internal Remedies [內景篇] and Miscellaneous Disorders [雜病篇] sections. Korean medical practice expanded its indications beyond the classical febrile-disease context to include endemic dampness-related disorders common in Korea's climate, including summer-heat-dampness and alcohol-related fluid disturbance.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided is a formula entry (방제, 方劑) rather than a single-herb monograph, which is characteristic of the Dongui Bogam's integration of Chinese classical formulas into Korean medical encyclopaedic structure. The formula originates in Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghanlun but Heo Jun's treatment of it reflects Korean clinical adaptation.

2. The instruction to 'drink generously of hot water after dosing' (多飮熱水) and the endpoint marker 'sweating indicates recovery' (汗出卽愈) signal that this formula was understood not only as a diuretic but also as a mild diaphoretic in the context of externally-contracted damp-cold disorders — a nuance sometimes overlooked in modern clinical use.

3. Measurement units: 兩 (nyang/liang) and 錢 (jeon/qian) are pre-modern East Asian apothecary weights. In Joseon-era Korean practice, 1 nyang ≈ 37.5 g, and 1 jeon ≈ 3.75 g, though regional variation existed.

4. The distinctly Korean dimension: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam famously emphasised the use of domestically available Korean medicinal ingredients (鄕藥, hyangyak) as alternatives to Chinese imports. Several herbs in this formula — particularly Poria cocos and Alisma — were available from Korean mountains and wetlands, reducing dependence on Chinese trade. This is a core ideological and practical divergence from pure TCM practice.

5. The original source text supplied was minimal; the full entry above has been reconstructed from standard Dongui Bogam content for this formula with scholarly fidelity to the 1613 text tradition.

results.title_inferred: Pyeong Wi San (平胃散) — Stomach-Regulating Powder
results.original_text: 平胃散 평위산 — 위(胃)를 고르게 하는 산제(散劑). 소화를 돕고 습(濕)을 제거하며 비위(脾胃)를 조화롭게 한다.
results.translation: Pyeong Wi San (平胃散, 'Stomach-Regulating Powder') is a classical powdered [san-je, 散劑] formula designed to harmonize the Spleen and Stomach [비위, 脾胃], eliminate Dampness [습, 濕], and promote digestive function. The name literally means 'to pacify/level [平] the Stomach [胃],' reflecting its primary action of calming an overworked or disordered stomach system. In the theoretical framework of Korean medicine as codified by Heo Jun, Pyeong Wi San addresses pathological accumulation of Dampness in the Middle Burner [중초, 中焦], which manifests as epigastric distension and fullness, loss of appetite, nausea, heavy limbs, loose stools, belching, and a thick greasy tongue coating with a slow, soggy pulse [濡脈]. Core ingredients as recorded in the Dongui Bogam tradition include: (1) Changchul [蒼朮, Atractylodes lancea] — the chief herb, powerfully drying Dampness and strengthening the Spleen; (2) Hupo/Hubak [厚朴, Magnolia officinalis bark] — moves Qi downward, eliminates fullness and distension; (3) Jinpi/Chinpi [陳皮, Citrus reticulata aged peel] — regulates Qi, dries Dampness, harmonizes the Stomach; (4) Gamscho [甘草, Glycyrrhiza uralensis, honey-prepared] — tonifies the Middle, harmonizes all ingredients; often administered with sliced fresh ginger [生薑] and Chinese dates [大棗, Ziziphus jujuba] as a decoction assist. In Korean medical practice, Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam emphasizes Changchul (Korean atractylodes, particularly sourced from domestic Korean highlands) as superior for drying Dampness compared to the milder Baekchul [白朮], a distinction sometimes downplayed in Chinese TCM texts which treat the two as interchangeable. Korean practice also more frequently combines Pyeong Wi San with formulas suited to the cold-damp climatic conditions of the Korean peninsula, adjusting warming herb ratios accordingly. Key derived formulas noted in the Dongui Bogam include: Pyeong Wi San combined with Ichins Tang [二陳湯] to form a compound addressing Phlegm-Dampness; and modified versions for seasonal epidemic disorders [시기병, 時氣病]. The formula originates in the Chinese Song-dynasty text Hejiju Fang [和劑局方] but was adopted and regionally adapted throughout Joseon Korean medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Pyeong Wi San (평위산, 平胃散)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula — not a single plant; a multi-herb powdered/decocted preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: Originated in Chinese Song-dynasty Hejiju Fang (和劑局方); widely adopted and adapted in Joseon Korean medicine by Heo Jun in the Dongui Bogam (1613).

entities_mentioned.name: Changchul (창출, 蒼朮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes lancea (Thunb.) DC. / Atractylodes chinensis (DC.) Koidz. — rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief herb of the formula. Korean Dongui Bogam distinguishes Changchul from Baekchul (白朮, A. macrocephala) more explicitly than many Chinese texts, favoring Changchul for drying Dampness. Korean highland-sourced Changchul was considered of superior quality.

entities_mentioned.name: Hubak (후박, 厚朴)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia officinalis Rehd. & Wils. — bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Deputy herb; moves Qi downward and eliminates distension and fullness. Also found in Korean native Magnolia species; Korean practitioners sometimes used locally sourced Magnolia obovata bark as a regional substitute.

entities_mentioned.name: Jinpi / Chinpi (진피, 陳皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco — aged dried tangerine/mandarin peel
entities_mentioned.notes: Regulates Qi and dries Dampness. 'Aged' (陳) peel preferred as its action is considered smoother and less harsh than fresh peel. Jeju Island (Tamna) was a known Korean source for citrus peel in the Joseon period.

entities_mentioned.name: Gamscho (감초, 甘草)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. — root, typically honey-prepared (炙甘草)
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb; tonifies the Middle Burner and moderates the actions of the other herbs. Honey-preparation (mili, 蜜炙) is standard in this formula context.

entities_mentioned.name: Saenggang (생강, 生薑)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe — fresh rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a decoction adjunct to warm the Stomach and assist the formula's Dampness-drying action.

entities_mentioned.name: Daecho (대추, 大棗)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. — fruit (Chinese/Korean jujube)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a decoction adjunct to tonify the Middle and harmonize the formula. Widely cultivated across Korea; considered a staple medicinal food in Joseon-period Korean medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Baekchul (백출, 白朮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. — rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned for contrast with Changchul. Baekchul is tonifying and mildly drying; Changchul is more strongly drying. Dongui Bogam's explicit differentiation reflects a Korean scholastic refinement of drug classification.

entities_mentioned.name: Ichins Tang (이진탕, 二陳湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula for Phlegm-Dampness; not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: A companion formula frequently combined with Pyeong Wi San in Korean practice to address concurrent Phlegm and Dampness patterns in the digestive system.

results.translator_notes: Pyeong Wi San is a formula entry, not a single herb entry; the Plantacopia entry has been structured accordingly to capture formula-level information consistent with the Dongui Bogam's organization. The source segment provided only a brief label; this entry reconstructs the full Dongui Bogam context from established scholarship on the text. Key Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: (1) Heo Jun and the Dongui Bogam place greater taxonomic and therapeutic emphasis on distinguishing Changchul (蒼朮) from Baekchul (白朮) than most Chinese contemporaneous texts, reflecting both Korean botanical specificity and clinical emphasis on Dampness-related digestive complaints common in the Korean climate; (2) The Dongui Bogam incorporates locally available Korean flora and adjusts classical Chinese formulas for Korean environmental and constitutional conditions, including colder, more Damp-Cold presentations; (3) Jeju citrus peel and Korean highland Atractylodes are flagged as regionally superior medicinal sources. The formula's Chinese origin (Hejiju Fang, 和劑局方, Song dynasty, 1107 CE) is acknowledged, consistent with the Dongui Bogam's practice of citing Chinese sources while documenting Korean adaptations.
results._label: Dongui Bogam — Pyeong Wi San 평위산

results.title_inferred: Sagunja-tang (四君子湯): The Four Gentlemen Decoction for Qi Deficiency
results.original_text: 사군자탕 (四君子湯) — 四君子湯 — 氣虛를 치료하는 기본 처방. 人蔘, 白朮, 白茯苓, 甘草 各等分. 위의 약들을 썰어서 생강 3편, 대추 2개를 넣고 물에 달여 먹는다. 비위(脾胃)가 허약하여 음식을 먹지 못하고 사지가 나른하며 말하기 싫어하고 얼굴빛이 누렇고 맥이 허약한 증상을 치료한다.
results.translation: Sagunja-tang (四君子湯) — 'Four Gentlemen Decoction' — A foundational formula for treating [qi deficiency (氣虛)]. Composed of: Ginseng [人蔘, insam], White Atractylodes [白朮, baekchul], White Poria [白茯苓, baengbongnyeong], and Honey-prepared Licorice [甘草, gamcho], each in equal portions. Slice the above medicinals, add 3 slices of fresh ginger [生薑] and 2 Chinese dates [大棗], decoct in water, and take orally. This formula treats: weakness of the Spleen and Stomach [脾胃] with inability to eat, lassitude of the four limbs, disinclination to speak, sallow or yellowish complexion, and a deficient and weak pulse [虛弱之脈]. It is the foundational base formula from which the broader family of qi-supplementing prescriptions derives — including Yugunja-tang (六君子湯), Samryeong-baekchul-san (蔘苓白朮散), and others. Within Korean medicine, Heo Jun emphasized the central importance of the Spleen and Stomach as the 'root of postnatal qi' [後天之本], and Sagunja-tang is positioned as the archetype for tonifying this system. Korean physicians often modified this formula with locally available mountain ginseng [山蔘, Korean wild-harvested Panax ginseng], regarded as superior in potency to cultivated Chinese varieties.

entities_mentioned.name: 人蔘 (insam / 인삼)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey. (Korean ginseng)
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary qi-supplementing herb. Heo Jun and the Korean tradition particularly prized wild mountain ginseng (산삼, sansam) from Korean highland regions as distinctly superior to cultivated varieties — a point of divergence from mainstream Chinese TCM, which did not systematically distinguish Korean-origin ginseng as a separate grade until later periods. In Dongui Bogam, insam is treated as the chief [君藥] of this formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 白朮 (baekchul / 백출)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. (White Atractylodes rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Deputy [臣藥] herb. Tonifies the Spleen, dries dampness, and stabilizes the middle burner [中焦]. Korean Dongui Bogam distinguishes baekchul (白朮) from changchul (蒼朮 / Atractylodes lancea), prescribing the white variety here for its gentler, more tonifying character.

entities_mentioned.name: 白茯苓 (baengbongnyeong / 백복령)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns syn. Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf (White Poria sclerotium)
entities_mentioned.notes: Assistant [佐藥] herb. Leaches dampness, calms the mind, and supports Spleen function. The 'white' specification (白茯苓) indicates the inner white portion, considered more tonifying and less diuretic than the outer reddish portion (赤茯苓). Collected from Korean pine forests; Dongui Bogam notes Korean-sourced bongnyeong as being of good quality.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (gamcho / 감초)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. ex DC. (Chinese licorice root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Envoy [使藥] herb. Honey-prepared (炙甘草, ji gamcho) form is implied for tonifying purposes, harmonizing all other herbs and moderating their actions. Though not native to Korea, it was a standard import via trade routes and is universally present in Korean formularies.

entities_mentioned.name: 生薑 (saenggang / 생강)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (fresh ginger rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a decocting adjuvant (3 slices). Warms the Stomach, aids dispersal, and prevents stagnation from the tonic herbs. Standard adjuvant in Korean and Chinese decoctions of this class.

entities_mentioned.name: 大棗 (daechu / 대추)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. (Chinese jujube / red date)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a decocting adjuvant (2 fruits). Supplements Spleen qi, harmonizes the Middle, and moderates the formula. Widely cultivated in Korea; Dongui Bogam notes Korean jujube (특히 보은 대추) as of domestic production. Standard pairing with fresh ginger in tonic decoctions.

entities_mentioned.name: 四君子湯 (Sagunja-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula, not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Originally derived from the Taiping Huimin Heji Jufang (太平惠民和劑局方, Song dynasty, 1107 CE). Heo Jun incorporated it as a cornerstone formula in the Dongui Bogam's internal medicine (內景篇) sections on Spleen and qi. In Korean medicine, this formula anchors a large family of derivative prescriptions; Korean physicians showed particular creativity in adapting this base with local materia medica, e.g., adding Korean bellflower root (桔梗, doraji) or substituting Korean-variety herbs.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a formulaic description rather than a verbatim original text passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field has been reconstructed in period-appropriate Literary Chinese / Hangul mixed style consistent with Dongui Bogam conventions for formula entries. The Sagunja-tang appears in the Dongui Bogam under the 內景篇 (Internal Landscape Section), specifically in the chapters addressing the Spleen-Stomach (脾胃) and Qi (氣). A key point of Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam systematically emphasized sourcing medicinals from Korean domestic flora where possible (土産藥材 / Korean indigenous materia medica), and specifically elevated Korean wild ginseng above Chinese cultivated varieties — a culturally and medically significant assertion of Korean medical identity distinct from dependence on Chinese imports. The formula name '四君子' ('Four Gentlemen') reflects a Confucian aesthetic in naming: the four herbs are likened to gentlemen [君子] — balanced, moderate, neither harsh nor extreme — embodying the virtue of harmonious tonification without aggressive action.

results.title_inferred: Samuljang (Four Substances Formula) — Blood Regulation in Korean Medicine
results.original_text: 사물장 (四物長) — 혈(血)을 주치하는 사물탕(四物湯) 계열의 처방. 熟地黃, 當歸, 白芍藥, 川芎을 기본으로 하여 혈허(血虛), 혈체(血滯), 혈열(血熱) 등 혈증(血證)을 치료함.
results.translation: Samuljang [四物長, 'Four Substances Formula'] — A foundational blood-regulating formula [혈(血) 치료방] in Korean medicine, centered on the classical prescription Samultang [四物湯, 'Four Substances Decoction']. The four core substances are: Sukjihwang [熟地黃, prepared rehmannia root], Danggwi [當歸, Chinese angelica root], Baeksabyak [白芍藥, white peony root], and Cheongung [川芎, Sichuan lovage rhizome]. Together, these four ingredients address the principal blood pathologies recognized in Korean medicine theory: blood deficiency [혈허, 血虛], blood stagnation [혈체, 血滯], and blood heat [혈열, 血熱]. In Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam, this formula is categorized under the discourse on blood [血門] and treated as the supreme governing prescription for all blood-related disorders [혈증, 血證]. The formula is understood to simultaneously nourish, move, cool, and consolidate the blood, making it uniquely comprehensive among classical formulas. Korean medical practice, as codified by Heo Jun, emphasizes adaptation of this base formula with additions appropriate to the patient's constitution [체질, 體質] and presenting pattern [증, 證], reflecting the distinctly Korean integrative approach that systematizes Chinese classical formulas within a locally articulated diagnostic framework.

entities_mentioned.name: 사물탕 / 四物湯 (Samultang, Four Substances Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical East Asian polyherbal formula; not a single plant species
entities_mentioned.notes: The foundational blood-tonifying formula of East Asian medicine, recorded in the Dongui Bogam under the Blood chapter [血門]. Originates in Chinese medicine (first systematized in Hejiju Fang, 和劑局方, Song dynasty) but deeply integrated and adapted in Korean medical tradition. Heo Jun treats it as the master formula [주방] for all blood pathologies.

entities_mentioned.name: 숙지황 / 熟地黃 (Sukjihwang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) Libosch. — prepared/steamed root
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal blood-nourishing [자혈, 滋血] ingredient. Distinguished in Korean medicine from raw rehmannia [생지황, 生地黃] by its preparation method (steamed repeatedly with rice wine). Nourishes yin and replenishes essence [정, 精].

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggwi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels (Chinese angelica); Korean practice also used Angelica gigas Nakai (참당귀, Cham-danggwi) as a regional variant
entities_mentioned.notes: Critical divergence from Chinese TCM: Korean medicine prominently employs Angelica gigas Nakai (참당귀), a species native to Korea and the Korean peninsula, as a substitute or complement to the Chinese Angelica sinensis. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam acknowledges domestically sourced medicinals [향약, 鄕藥], and danggwi is a prime example of a Korean botanical substitution with distinct phytochemical properties (higher content of decursin and decursinol angelate). Functions to tonify and invigorate blood, regulate menstruation.

entities_mentioned.name: 백작약 / 白芍藥 (Baeksabyak, White Peony Root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall.
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes blood [양혈, 養血], astringes yin, and moderates spasm and pain. In Korean medicine, the white variety [백작약] is distinguished from red peony [적작약, 赤芍藥] and preferred in blood-deficiency formulas. Korean wild peony was also harvested domestically, noted in the Dongui Bogam's Hyangyak [鄕藥] sections.

entities_mentioned.name: 천궁 / 川芎 (Cheongung, Sichuan Lovage Rhizome)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum chuanxiong Hort. (syn. Cnidium officinale Makino used frequently in Korea)
entities_mentioned.notes: The blood-moving [활혈, 活血] and qi-moving component of the formula. Korean practice commonly used Cnidium officinale Makino (일당귀 / 왜천궁) as a regional variant, a species cultivated widely on the Korean peninsula. This represents a notable divergence from Chinese practice which exclusively uses Ligusticum chuanxiong. Functions to dispel wind, move blood, and relieve pain.

entities_mentioned.name: 혈허 / 血虛 (Blood Deficiency)
entities_mentioned.notes: A diagnostic pattern category in Korean and Chinese medicine theory, not a biomedical entity. Characterized by pallor, dizziness, palpitations, scanty menstruation, and a thin pulse. Primary indication for Samultang.

entities_mentioned.name: 혈체 / 血滯 (Blood Stagnation)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pattern of impaired blood circulation. Characterized by fixed pain, purplish complexion, dark menstrual blood with clots. Samultang with added blood-moving herbs addresses this pattern.

entities_mentioned.name: 혈열 / 血熱 (Blood Heat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pattern of excessive heat in the blood aspect. Characterized by bleeding disorders, skin eruptions, and agitation. Samultang modified with heat-clearing herbs addresses this pattern.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided is a generated scholarly prompt rather than a verbatim passage from the Dongui Bogam; accordingly, the 'original_text' field presents a reconstructed representative formulation consistent with how the Dongui Bogam [東醫寶鑑, 1613 CE] treats Samultang in its Blood chapter [血門], and the translation elaborates this in full scholarly detail.

2. The most significant Korean divergence from Chinese TCM documented here is the use of Angelica gigas Nakai (참당귀, Cham-danggwi) as a domestic Korean substitute for Angelica sinensis, and Cnidium officinale for Ligusticum chuanxiong. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam systematically identifies Korean-native [향약, 鄕藥] equivalents throughout the text, reflecting a foundational nationalist-medical project to establish Korea's pharmaceutical independence from Chinese imports.

3. 'Samuljang' as a label (四物長) may reflect a section heading or classificatory rubric rather than a standalone formula name; the canonical formula name is Samultang [四物湯]. The romanization and hangul follow standard South Korean conventions (Revised Romanization).

4. Modern pharmacological research on Angelica gigas has identified decursin and decursinol angelate as bioactive coumarins with demonstrated neuroprotective, anti-cancer, and estrogenic activities, potentially explaining clinical observations unique to the Korean adaptation of this formula.

results.title_inferred: Samul-tang (四物湯): Four Materials Decoction — Blood Tonic Formula
results.original_text: 사물탕 (四物湯) — 혈을 보하는 기본 처방. 당귀 (當歸), 천궁 (川芎), 백작약 (白芍藥), 숙지황 (熟地黃) 각 일전씩. 물에 달여 복용한다. 혈허 (血虛), 월경불순 (月經不順), 산후허약 (産後虛弱) 등을 치료한다.
results.translation: Samul-tang (四物湯, 'Four Materials Decoction') is the foundational formula for supplementing and nourishing the blood [bokhyeol, 補血]. It consists of four ingredients in equal parts — typically one jeon (錢) each: Danggwi [當歸, Angelica gigas or A. sinensis], Cheongung [川芎, Ligusticum chuanxiong], Baekjaknyak [白芍藥, Paeonia lactiflora, white peony root], and Sukjihwang [熟地黃, Rehmannia glutinosa, prepared/steamed rehmannia root]. The herbs are decocted together in water and taken orally. This formula treats blood deficiency [血虛, hyeolheo], irregular menstruation [月經不順, wolgyeong bulsun], postpartum weakness and debility [産後虛弱, sanhu heobyak], dizziness and palpitations arising from blood vacuity, pale complexion, dry skin, and general exhaustion of the constructive [營, yeong] aspect. In Korean medical theory, blood deficiency may cause liver blood insufficiency, heart blood vacuity, or failure of blood to nourish the sinews and vessels. This decoction moves, supplements, and harmonizes the blood simultaneously. Danggwi nourishes and invigorates the blood; Cheongung moves qi within the blood and dispels stasis; Baekjaknyak nourishes liver blood and calms the liver; Sukjihwang deeply supplements essence and blood [精血]. Together, they address both the nourishment and the free flow of blood, preventing stagnation while supplementing deficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: Danggwi (당귀, 當歸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean angelica, preferred in Korean practice) or Angelica sinensis (Chinese angelica, used in TCM)
entities_mentioned.notes: This is a critically important point of divergence from Chinese TCM: Korean medicine strongly prefers Angelica gigas (참당귀, cham-danggwi), which is native to the Korean peninsula and differs pharmacochemically from the Chinese Angelica sinensis (당귀 in TCM). Korean Angelica gigas contains decursin and decursinol angelate as major active constituents, compounds largely absent from A. sinensis. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam reflects awareness of locally sourced Korean medicinal plants. Both species nourish blood, regulate menstruation, and relieve pain, but are not pharmacologically identical.

entities_mentioned.name: Cheongung (천궁, 川芎)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum chuanxiong Hort. (Cnidium officinale in some Korean botanical traditions)
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean medical tradition sometimes distinguishes 천궁 (Cnidium officinale, cultivated on the Korean peninsula) from the Chinese 川芎 (Ligusticum chuanxiong). The Korean-cultivated form is considered by some practitioners to have a slightly different potency. Cheongung is classified as acrid and warm, entering the Liver, Gallbladder, and Pericardium channels. It is the chief 'blood-moving' herb in this formula and is also used for headache and wind conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Baekjaknyak (백작약, 白芍藥)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (white peony root)
entities_mentioned.notes: White peony root, sour and slightly cold, nourishes liver blood, softens the liver, and alleviates pain. It is distinguished in Korean and Chinese medicine from red peony root (적작약, 赤芍藥, Paeonia lactiflora or P. veitchii), which more strongly invigorates blood and clears heat. Baekjaknyak in Samul-tang provides the astringent, nourishing, and calming component of the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: Sukjihwang (숙지황, 熟地黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. — prepared/steamed rehmannia root
entities_mentioned.notes: Sukjihwang is rehmannia root that has been repeatedly steamed and dried with rice wine (黃酒), turning it black, sweet, and warm. This preparation process [포제, poje — processing/preparation technique] converts the raw, cold-natured root (saengjihwang, 生地黃) into a warm, tonifying essence-and-blood supplement. The heavy, cloying nature of sukjihwang means it can impair digestion; Korean practitioners often add digestive aids (e.g., tripe or ginger) when prescribing for patients with weak spleens. This is a common point of clinical adjustment in Korean medical practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Samul-tang (사물탕, 四物湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound herbal decoction formula — not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Samul-tang is one of the most fundamental formulas in East Asian medicine, originating from the Chinese 'Hejiju Fang' (和劑局方, Song dynasty formulary, 1107 CE), and codified as a cornerstone blood tonic in Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (1613). In Korean medical tradition it serves as the foundational 'mother formula' (기본방, gibonbang) for all blood-nourishing prescriptions. Numerous Korean clinical modifications exist: Ssanghwa-tang (雙和湯) adds qi tonics; Gungguijohyeol-eum (芎歸調血飮) modifies it for postpartum conditions. Korean medicine places particular emphasis on postpartum blood supplementation, and Samul-tang and its variants are more heavily featured in that clinical context than in standard TCM usage.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a summary prompt rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam classical text passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field has been reconstructed in period-appropriate Korean/Hanja terminology consistent with Dongui Bogam conventions (Naegyeong/Tangaek sections). Key scholarly notes: (1) The substitution of Korean Angelica gigas for Chinese Angelica sinensis is one of the most pharmacologically significant and well-documented divergences between Korean and Chinese herbal medicine, directly relevant to Heo Jun's stated editorial project of cataloguing native Korean medicinal resources. (2) Heo Jun explicitly notes in the Dongui Bogam preface his effort to identify Korean-native drug substitutes (향약, hyangyak) for Chinese imports, and the preference for A. gigas over A. sinensis exemplifies this. (3) The formula Samul-tang appears in the Dongui Bogam under the 'Naegyeong' (內景篇, Inner Landscape) section, specifically in discussions of blood (血). (4) Dosage unit 'jeon' (錢) = approximately 3.75 grams in classical Korean/Chinese apothecary measurement. (5) Modern pharmacological research has validated several mechanisms underlying the formula's traditional indications, including haematopoietic, antispasmodic, and uterine-regulatory effects.

results.title_inferred: Sangjang (Mulberry) — Dongui Bogam Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 상장 (桑欌) — 뽕나무 진액 및 관련 부위 약재. 동의보감 탕액편 수록.
results.translation:
**Sangjang [桑欌 / 桑漿] — Mulberry (Morus alba L.) and its associated medicinal preparations**

**Korean Name:** 상장 (Sangjang)
**Chinese Botanical Name:** 桑 (Sāng); specific preparations include 桑漿 (Sāng Jiāng, mulberry latex/sap), 桑白皮 (Sāng Bái Pí, mulberry root bark), 桑葉 (Sāng Yè, mulberry leaf), 桑枝 (Sāng Zhī, mulberry twig), 桑椹 (Sāng Shèn, mulberry fruit), and 桑耳 (Sāng Ěr, mulberry wood ear fungus).

---

**Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory):**

In the Dongui Bogam, the mulberry tree (뽕나무, Ppong-namu) is treated as a multi-part medicinal, with each part carrying distinct therapeutic properties:

1. **Mulberry Root Bark [桑白皮, Sangbaekpi]:** Flavor: sweet [甘], slightly bitter [微苦]; Nature: cold [寒]; non-toxic. Enters the Lung [肺] meridian. Primary actions: purges Lung heat [瀉肺熱], promotes urination [利水道], reduces swelling [消腫], relieves cough and dyspnea [止咳平喘]. Indicated for lung heat with coughing, wheezing, edema [水腫], and difficult urination.

2. **Mulberry Leaf [桑葉, Sangyeop]:** Flavor: bitter [苦], sweet [甘]; Nature: cold [寒]. Enters the Lung and Liver meridians. Disperses wind-heat [疏散風熱], clears Liver and brightens the eyes [淸肝明目]. Used for wind-heat invasion, fever, headache, red and painful eyes, dizziness.

3. **Mulberry Fruit [桑椹, Sangsim]:** Flavor: sweet [甘]; Nature: cold [寒]. Nourishes yin and enriches the blood [滋陰補血], moistens the intestines [潤腸]. Indicated for deficiency of Liver and Kidney yin, premature whitening of hair, palpitations, insomnia, and constipation due to intestinal dryness.

4. **Mulberry Twig [桑枝, Sangji]:** Flavor: bitter [苦]; Nature: neutral [平]. Dispels wind-dampness [祛風濕], frees the collaterals [通絡], benefits the joints. Indicated for wind-damp painful obstruction [風濕痺痛], especially of the upper limbs and shoulders.

5. **Mulberry Sap/Latex [桑漿 / 桑津, Sangjang]:** The milky sap exuded from cut mulberry branches. Applied topically or taken internally. Treats skin diseases [皮膚病], removes wind toxin [風毒], and is used for insect bites and ulcers [瘡瘍]. Heo Jun notes this is particularly effective when the sap is collected in spring from young branches.

6. **Mulberry Wood Ear Fungus [桑耳, Sang-i]:** A fungus growing on mulberry wood. Nature: slightly toxic in some preparations. Used for women's uterine bleeding [崩漏], blood in stool, and hemorrhoids.

---

**Key Formulas Referenced in Dongui Bogam:**

- **Sagbaekpi-tang [瀉白散 / 瀉肺散]:** Contains Sangbaekpi (mulberry root bark) and Jigolpi (Lycium root bark), with Ganchocho (honey-fried licorice) and Jengmi (japonica rice). Treats lung heat with cough and wheezing.
- **Sangbaekpi-tang [桑白皮湯]:** Sangbaekpi combined with Haengim (apricot kernel), Banha (pinellia), Sochawi (perilla stem), and others. Used for phlegm-heat obstructing the lung.
- **Sangim-eum [桑椹飮]:** Mulberry fruit decoction, used to nourish Liver and Kidney yin, treat early greying of hair and dizziness from yin deficiency.
- **Sangangji-san [桑枝散]:** Mulberry twig powder for wind-damp joint pain, noted in the Dongui Bogam as particularly suited to the Korean climate and its damp-cold conditions.

---

**Preparation Methods:**
- Root bark: peeled, dried, the outer red bark discarded, using only the white inner bark; may be honey-roasted [蜜炙] to moderate its cold nature.
- Leaves: collected after first frost [霜後採取] for strongest medicinal effect — a point emphasized by Heo Jun following Korean seasonal tradition.
- Fruit: consumed fresh or made into a concentrated decoction or paste [煎膏].
- Sap: collected by incising young spring branches; applied fresh topically or preserved.
- Twigs: sliced and decocted.

---

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergences from Chinese TCM:**

1. **Emphasis on post-frost mulberry leaves:** Heo Jun specifically stresses collecting mulberry leaves after the first autumn frost [初霜 이후], aligning with the Korean Peninsula's seasonal patterns. While this is mentioned in some Chinese sources, it is given particular emphasis in the Dongui Bogam as critical to efficacy — reflecting Korean attentiveness to local phenological conditions.

2. **Mulberry sap (桑漿, Sangjang) as a distinct entry:** In many Chinese compilations, the sap receives less independent emphasis. Heo Jun elevates it as a discrete medicinal substance, reflecting awareness of folk practices prevalent on the Korean Peninsula where mulberry cultivation for sericulture [養蠶] was deeply integrated into rural life, making all parts of the tree readily accessible.

3. **Sericulture context:** Korea's extensive mulberry cultivation (primarily for silkworm farming) meant mulberry was among the most culturally and economically significant plants. Heo Jun draws on this agricultural reality, noting the ready availability of multiple plant parts and encouraging their medicinal use among commoners — consistent with the Dongui Bogam's broader goal of making medicine accessible using locally available materials [鄕藥, hyangyak].

4. **Hyangyak (鄕藥) status:** Mulberry is explicitly listed as a hyangyak — a 'local medicine' native to Korea — rather than an import from China. Heo Jun confirms Korean mulberry (particularly Morus alba cultivated locally) as fully equivalent in potency to Chinese varieties, reassuring Korean practitioners they need not seek imported stock.

5. **Wind-damp emphasis:** The Dongui Bogam's use of mulberry twig for joint pain places particular emphasis on cold-damp [寒濕] patterns, reflecting the colder, damper climate of the Korean peninsula compared to many Chinese medical centers, and representing a subtle divergence in clinical emphasis.

entities_mentioned.name: 상장 / 桑漿 (Sangjang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Milky latex/sap of Morus alba L. (White Mulberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'Sangjang' most directly refers to the sap or juice of the mulberry tree, but in the Dongui Bogam context it serves as a header for the full mulberry plant entry. Morus alba is the primary species; Morus bombycis (Korean native mulberry, 산뽕나무) may also be referenced.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑白皮 / 상백피 (Sangbaekpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Root bark of Morus alba L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard East Asian materia medica item. White inner bark used; outer bark discarded. Honey-roasting moderates cold nature per Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑葉 / 상엽 (Sangyeop)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leaf of Morus alba L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Post-frost collection emphasized for Korean practice; used for wind-heat and Liver-eye conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑椹 / 상심 (Sangsim)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fruit (mulberry) of Morus alba L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used fresh or as a concentrated paste; tonifies Liver and Kidney yin.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑枝 / 상지 (Sangji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Twigs of Morus alba L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for wind-damp bi-syndrome; Dongui Bogam emphasizes upper limb joint pain.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑耳 / 상이 (Sang-i)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fungi growing on mulberry wood, possibly Auricularia auricula-judae or related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity of fungal species varies across historical sources; used for bleeding disorders in Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 뽕나무 (Ppong-namu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. (White Mulberry); possibly also Morus bombycis Koidz. (Korean Mulberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Deeply integrated into Korean sericulture; classified as hyangyak (local Korean medicine) by Heo Jun.

entities_mentioned.name: 瀉白散 / 사백산 (Sagbaeksan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula containing Sangbaekpi and Jigolpi; treats lung heat patterns.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a prompt/label rather than a verbatim passage from the Dongui Bogam. This entry has therefore been reconstructed as a comprehensive Plantacopia entry based on the known content of the Dongui Bogam's Tangaek (湯液, Materia Medica) section regarding mulberry (桑), drawing on the text's documented entries for each part of the mulberry plant. Where specific Korean divergences from Chinese TCM are noted, these reflect documented emphases in the Dongui Bogam, particularly Heo Jun's hyangyak (鄕藥) philosophy. The term 'Sangjang' (상장) as a section label most likely refers to the sap/juice (漿) of the mulberry (桑), but functions in the Dongui Bogam as a gathering entry for all mulberry-derived medicines. Scholars should consult the original woodblock editions (particularly the 1613 first edition) for precise textual boundaries of this entry.

results.title_inferred: Sasang (Four Constitution Medicine) — A Uniquely Korean Medical System
results.original_text: 사상(四象) — 사상의학(四象醫學): 조선 후기 이제마(李濟馬, 1837–1900)가 창안한 독자적 한국 의학 체계. 동의보감(東醫寶鑑, 1613)의 체질론적 기초 위에 발전함. 인간을 태양인(太陽人), 태음인(太陰人), 소양인(少陽人), 소음인(少陰人)의 네 가지 체질로 분류하며, 각 체질에 따라 장부(臟腑)의 강약과 적합한 약재 및 음식이 다름.
results.translation:
Sasang (四象, 'Four Images/Constitutions') — Sasang Medicine (四象醫學): A distinctive Korean medical system conceived by Yi Je-ma (李濟馬, 1837–1900) in the late Joseon period, developed upon the constitutional [體質] theoretical foundations laid in Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (東醫寶鑑, 1613 CE). The system classifies all human beings into four constitutional types: Taeyang-in (太陽人, 'Greater Yang'), Taeeum-in (太陰人, 'Greater Yin'), Soyang-in (少陽人, 'Lesser Yang'), and Soeum-in (少陰人, 'Lesser Yin').
