Each constitution is defined by the relative strength and weakness of the four viscera [臟腑] — the lungs [肺], spleen [脾], liver [肝], and kidneys [腎] — and determines which medicinal herbs, foods, and therapeutic strategies are appropriate or contraindicated for that individual.

Key constitutional profiles and associated herbal affinities (as grounded in Dongui Bogam tradition):

• TAEYANG-IN (太陽人) — Lung energy [肺氣] is strong; liver energy [肝氣] is weak. Extremely rare constitution. Suited herbs include: Ogapi [오가피, Acanthopanax senticosus], Moru [모과, Chaenomeles sinensis — Chinese quince], Haedong-pi [해동피]. Foods: buckwheat, seafood (light). Avoid: fatty or rich foods.

• TAEEUM-IN (太陰人) — Liver energy [肝氣] is strong; lung energy [肺氣] is weak. Most common Korean constitution (~50%). Suited herbs: Mahuang [마황, Ephedra sinica], Galgeun [갈근, Pueraria lobata — kudzu root], Uimi [의이인, Coix lacryma-jobi — Job's tears], Nokgak [녹각, deer antler], Hasuoh [하수오, Reynoutria multiflora]. Foods: beef, wheat, nuts. Avoid: cold-natured, raw foods.

• SOYANG-IN (少陽人) — Spleen energy [脾氣] is strong; kidney energy [腎氣] is weak. Second most common (~30%). Suited herbs: Saengji-hwang [생지황, Rehmannia glutinosa — raw], Bokryeong [복령, Poria cocos], Gugija [구기자, Lycium chinense], Mokdan-pi [목단피, Paeonia suffruticosa]. Foods: cucumber, barley, pork. Avoid: hot, spicy, stimulating foods.

• SOEUM-IN (少陰人) — Kidney energy [腎氣] is strong; spleen energy [脾氣] is weak. Third most common (~20%). Suited herbs: In삼 [인삼, Panax ginseng — especially Korean red ginseng], Buja [부자, Aconitum carmichaelii — processed aconite], Gyepi [계피, Cinnamomum cassia], Hwanggi [황기, Astragalus membranaceus]. Foods: chicken, rice, warm foods. Avoid: cold foods, raw vegetables.

Divergence from Chinese TCM: In classical Chinese TCM (中醫學), diagnosis and treatment are primarily pattern-based [辨證論治], focusing on immediate symptom clusters, pathogenic factors, and qi/blood/fluid dynamics without fixed constitutional typing. Sasang medicine represents a fundamental epistemological departure: it holds that a person's constitutional type [體質] is innate and immutable, and that the same herb may be therapeutic for one constitution and harmful for another — a concept with no direct equivalent in the TCM canon. For example, Panax ginseng [인삼], universally valued in Chinese medicine as a supreme qi tonic, is considered potentially harmful to Soyang-in individuals in Sasang theory. Similarly, Ephedra [마황], used cautiously in TCM due to its dispersing power, is considered suitable for Taeeum-in. This individual-centered, constitution-first paradigm was partly inspired by but substantially transforms the physiognomic and temperamental observations recorded in the Dongui Bogam, particularly the chapters on internal medicine [內景篇] and miscellaneous disorders.

Key Formulas associated with constitutional types (from Dongui Bogam and later Sasang tradition):
— Ssanghwa-tang (雙和湯): general tonic, favored for Taeeum-in and Soeum-in
— Yukmijihwang-tang (六味地黃湯): kidney-nourishing, suitable for Soyang-in
— Gwakhyangjeongi-san (藿香正氣散): digestive, used for Soeum-in with cold-damp patterns
— Taeeumjowi-tang (太陰調胃湯): a Sasang-specific formula for Taeeum-in digestive regulation, not found in classical Chinese texts
— Hyangsapyeongwi-san (香砂平胃散): spleen-harmonizing, suited to Soeum-in

Plants unique to or emphasized in Korean tradition within this system: Ogapi (오가피, Siberian ginseng / Acanthopanax), Galgeun (갈근, kudzu), and Korean red ginseng (홍삼) are particularly prominent in Sasang medicine and in the Dongui Bogam more broadly, reflecting Korea's distinct botanical pharmacopoeia [鄕藥, hyangyak — 'local medicines'].

entities_mentioned.name: Sasang (사상, 四象)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Four-constitution medicine theoretical system; not a single herb
entities_mentioned.notes: Conceptual framework unique to Korean medicine; formalized by Yi Je-ma (1837–1900) but rooted in Dongui Bogam constitutional observations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ogapi (오가피, 五加皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acanthopanax senticosus / Eleutherococcus senticosus (Siberian ginseng bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Especially associated with Taeyang-in constitution; an herb with strong Korean medicinal identity, listed in Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: Mogwa (모과, 木瓜)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chaenomeles sinensis (Chinese quince)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for Taeyang-in; relaxes sinews, harmonizes stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: Mahuang (마황, 麻黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica
entities_mentioned.notes: In TCM, used cautiously as a dispersing herb; in Sasang, considered appropriate for Taeeum-in constitution.

entities_mentioned.name: Galgeun (갈근, 葛根)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria lobata (kudzu root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Featured prominently in Dongui Bogam; strongly associated with Taeeum-in in Sasang medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Uimi / Uiiin (의이인, 薏苡仁)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coix lacryma-jobi (Job's tears)
entities_mentioned.notes: Diuretic, anti-inflammatory; used for Taeeum-in with damp accumulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Nokgak (녹각, 鹿角)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Deer antler (Cervus nippon or C. elaphus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal product; tonifies yang and essence; associated with Taeeum-in in Sasang tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Hasuoh (하수오, 何首烏)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Reynoutria multiflora / Polygonum multiflorum
entities_mentioned.notes: Liver and kidney tonic; used for Taeeum-in.

entities_mentioned.name: Saengjiwhang (생지황, 生地黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (raw form)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cooling, yin-nourishing; suited to Soyang-in with heat patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: Bokryeong (복령, 茯苓)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (tuckahoe fungus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Calms shen [神], strengthens spleen; suitable for Soyang-in.

entities_mentioned.name: Gugija (구기자, 枸杞子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense / L. barbarum (wolfberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes liver and kidney yin; associated with Soyang-in.

entities_mentioned.name: Mokdan-pi (목단피, 牧丹皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia suffruticosa (tree peony root bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears heat, cools blood; used for Soyang-in with heat signs.

entities_mentioned.name: Insamn / Ginseng (인삼, 人蔘)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng (Korean ginseng)
entities_mentioned.notes: Supreme tonic in both TCM and Korean medicine; in Sasang theory, especially suited to Soeum-in; potentially contraindicated for Soyang-in — a notable divergence from Chinese practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Buja (부자, 附子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii (processed aconite lateral root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Powerfully warming; used for Soeum-in with severe cold deficiency; toxic if improperly processed.

entities_mentioned.name: Gyepi (계피, 桂皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (cassia cinnamon bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms the interior, disperses cold; primary herb for Soeum-in.

entities_mentioned.name: Hwanggi (황기, 黃芪)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies wei qi [衛氣]; suits Soeum-in with qi deficiency and spontaneous sweating.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyangyak (향약, 鄕藥)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Korean indigenous/local medicinal plants as a category
entities_mentioned.notes: A key organizing concept of the Dongui Bogam — the systematic cataloguing of locally available Korean herbs as alternatives to imported Chinese drugs.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT EDITORIAL CAVEAT: The source segment as submitted is a modern prompt/framing document, not an authentic passage from the 1613 Dongui Bogam text itself. Sasang (四象) constitutional medicine was formalized by Yi Je-ma in his 'Dongui Susei Bowon' (東醫壽世保元, 1894), nearly three centuries after the Dongui Bogam. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam does contain extensive proto-constitutional thinking — particularly in its discussions of individual physiology [臟腑], temperament, and the localization of Korean herbal medicine [鄕藥] — which provided an intellectual foundation that Yi Je-ma later systematized. The entry above synthesizes both the Dongui Bogam's relevant content and the Sasang tradition it informed, clearly distinguishing between the two where necessary. The formula 'Taeeumjowi-tang' is a Sasang-specific formula not found in the 1613 Dongui Bogam; all other formulas cited appear in or are consistent with the Dongui Bogam corpus. Researchers should treat constitutional herb assignments as reflecting Sasang medical tradition (post-1894) rather than direct Dongui Bogam prescription.

results.title_inferred: Seonghyang (성향) — Korean Wind-Cold Herb from Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 성향 (省香) / 藿香 (Huòxiāng) — 동의보감 (東醫寶鑑) 탕액편 (湯液篇). 성질은 약간 따뜻하고 [微溫] 맛은 맵고 달며 [辛甘] 독이 없다. 풍한 (風寒) 을 흩어내고, 비위 (脾胃) 를 조화롭게 하며, 구토와 설사를 멈추게 하고, 곽란 (藿亂) 을 치료한다. 또한 기 (氣) 를 잘 통하게 하고, 가슴이 답답하고 그득한 것을 없애며, 입 냄새를 제거한다. 산과 들에서 자라며 우리나라 각처에서 생산된다. 잎이 넓고 줄기는 네모지며, 여름에 자주색 꽃이 핀다. 전초 (全草) 를 약으로 쓰며 음력 5~6월에 채취하여 그늘에서 말린다. 주요 처방으로는 곽향정기산 (藿香正氣散) 이 있으며, 이는 풍한 감기, 구토, 설사, 여름철 더위에 손상된 경우에 쓴다.
results.translation: Seonghyang (성향, 省香), also written as Gwakhyang (藿香, Huòxiāng in Chinese). Its nature is slightly warm [微溫], its flavor is acrid and sweet [辛甘], and it is non-toxic. It disperses wind-cold [風寒], harmonizes the spleen and stomach [脾胃], arrests vomiting and diarrhea, and treats sudden turmoil disorder [藿亂, cholera-like acute gastroenteritis]. It also promotes the smooth flow of qi [氣], relieves chest oppression and fullness, and eliminates bad breath. It grows in mountains and fields and is produced throughout the various regions of our country [Korea]. The leaves are broad, the stems are square in cross-section, and purple flowers bloom in summer. The entire aerial plant [全草] is used medicinally; it is harvested in the fifth and sixth months of the lunar calendar and dried in the shade. The principal formula is Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san (藿香正氣散, 'Agastache Qi-Rectifying Powder'), which is used for wind-cold common cold, vomiting, diarrhea, and damage caused by summer heat.

entities_mentioned.name: Seonghyang / Gwakhyang (성향 / 곽향, 藿香)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agastache rugosa (Korean patchouli / Korean mint, family Lamiaceae); note: Chinese TCM 藿香 more commonly refers to Pogostemon cablin (true patchouli), but in Korean Dongui Bogam tradition the domestic herb used is Agastache rugosa, a plant native to East Asia and widely cultivated in Korea
entities_mentioned.notes: This is a notable point of Korean divergence from Chinese TCM practice. In Chinese medicine, 藿香 (Huòxiāng) typically denotes Pogostemon cablin (Blanco) Benth., a tropical Southeast Asian plant. However, Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam specifies a locally grown Korean herb with square stems, broad leaves, and purple summer flowers — a clear morphological description of Agastache rugosa (Fisch. & C.A.Mey.) Kuntze, known in Korean as 배초향 (Baechohyang). This substitution reflects the Dongui Bogam's core editorial philosophy of prioritizing domestically available Korean medicinal materials (향약, Hyangyak) over imported Chinese equivalents. The therapeutic functions described — dispersing wind-cold, harmonizing the spleen and stomach, treating vomiting, diarrhea, and summer heat disorders — are functionally consistent across both species in their respective traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san (藿香正氣散, Agastache Qi-Rectifying Powder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A classical compound formula; still in clinical use in Korean and Chinese medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-known formula originating from the Taiping Huimin Heji Jufang (太平惠民和劑局方, Song dynasty, 1107 CE), adopted and adapted in the Dongui Bogam. Contains Agastache/Pogostemon as the chief herb, combined with Perilla leaf, White Atractylodes, Magnolia bark, Pinellia, Tangerine peel, and others. Indicated for external wind-cold with internal dampness stagnation — a pattern commonly presenting as acute gastroenteritis with concurrent cold symptoms, or summer-season illness.

entities_mentioned.name: Gwaknan (藿亂, Hogeran / sudden turmoil disorder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical East Asian medical term for acute gastroenteritis with simultaneous vomiting and diarrhea; overlaps with but is not identical to cholera in modern biomedicine
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 藿亂 (Korean: 곽란, gwaknan) in classical Korean-Chinese medicine describes violent and sudden simultaneous vomiting and diarrhea causing collapse of qi dynamic. It was one of the primary indications for Agastache-based formulas. Modern scholars note partial overlap with epidemic cholera but the classical term is broader and includes non-infectious acute gastric crises.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source prompt provides a partially reconstructed/synthetic entry rather than a verbatim classical manuscript passage; the original_text field above represents a scholarly reconstruction in classical Korean-Chinese medical register consistent with the Dongui Bogam Tangaekpyeon (湯液篇, Materia Medica section) style, since no verbatim OCR source text was supplied. This has been flagged for archival transparency. 2. The Korean-vs-Chinese species identification question (Agastache rugosa vs. Pogostemon cablin) is one of the most discussed botanical divergences in Hyangyak (鄕藥, native Korean materia medica) scholarship and is explicitly worth preserving in the Plantacopia record. 3. The romanization 'Seonghyang' (省香) in the prompt title appears to be an alternate or colloquial orthographic rendering; the standard Dongui Bogam entry term is 藿香 (Gwakhyang). 省香 may reflect a variant transcription or scribal abbreviation. Both forms are preserved here.

results.title_inferred: Siho (Bupleurum): Liver Qi Regulation and Alternating Fever-Chill Conditions
results.original_text: 시호 (柴胡) — Bupleurum falcatum L. 간기(肝氣)를 소통시키고, 한열왕래(寒熱往來)를 치료한다.
results.translation:
Siho [柴胡, Bupleurum]. Korean name: 시호 (siho). Chinese botanical name: 柴胡 (cháihú). Plant: Bupleurum falcatum L. (sickle-leaf hare's ear).

**Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory):**
Siho enters the Liver [肝] and Gallbladder [膽] channels and the Pericardium [心包] and Triple Warmer [三焦] channels. Its primary actions are: (1) to dredge and course Liver qi [疏肝理氣], relieving constraint and stagnation of the Liver organ system; (2) to resolve the Shaoyang [和解少陽] — that is, to mediate between the exterior and interior planes of the body, treating the characteristic pattern of alternating fever and chills [寒熱往來, hanyeol wangrae]; (3) to raise and upbear the clear yang qi [昇陽], lifting sunken Middle Burner qi; and (4) to relieve heat from constraint [退熱解表] in febrile disorders. It is classed as bitter [苦] and slightly cold [微寒] in nature, with a light, ascending, and yang character.

**Conditions Treated:**
- Alternating fever and chills [寒熱往來]: the hallmark Shaoyang-stage disorder, as codified in Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghanlun tradition and adopted in Joseon Korean practice.
- Liver qi stagnation [肝氣鬱結]: flank pain [脇痛], emotional constraint, depression, irritability.
- Prolapse disorders due to sinking of qi [中氣下陷]: prolapse of the rectum or uterus, chronic diarrhea with exhaustion.
- Intermittent fevers [瘧疾, haksil] including malarial-type conditions.
- Menstrual irregularity associated with Liver constraint.
- Headache and dizziness of Shaoyang type.

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**
1. **Soshiho-tang [小柴胡湯, Xiao Chaihu Tang]** — 'Minor Bupleurum Decoction.' The foundational Shaoyang formula: Siho, Banha [半夏, Pinellia], Insamn [人蔘, Ginseng], Hwanggeun [黃芩, Scutellaria], Gamcho [甘草, Licorice], Saenggang [生薑, Fresh Ginger], Daechu [大棗, Jujube]. Indicated for alternating fever-chills, chest and flank fullness, loss of appetite, nausea, and bitter taste in the mouth.
2. **Daeshiho-tang [大柴胡湯, Da Chaihu Tang]** — 'Major Bupleurum Decoction.' For Shaoyang disorder with interior excess: adds Daehwang [大黃, Rhubarb] and Jisil [枳實, Immature Bitter Orange] to the base formula.
3. **Bojoong ikgi-tang [補中益氣湯, Buzhong Yiqi Tang]** — 'Tonify the Middle and Augment Qi Decoction.' Uses Siho in a smaller dose as an ascending herb alongside Seungma [升麻, Cimicifuga] to raise sunken Spleen-Stomach qi. This formula appears prominently in the Dongui Bogam's internal medicine chapters and reflects Li Dongyuan's [李東垣] influence.
4. **Siho-seungsanyak [柴胡勝邪藥]** — Korean-adapted Shaoyang dispelling formula recorded by Heo Jun for febrile epidemic disorders [疫病] common in Joseon.
5. **Haksil-bang [瘧疾方]** — Formulas for intermittent fever (malarial patterns) frequently employ Siho as the chief herb, combined with Sanghwang [常山, Dichroa febrifuga] in classical tradition, though Heo Jun notes Korean clinical adjustments based on the patient's constitution [體質].

**Preparation Methods:**
- Standard decoction [煎湯]: dried root slices boiled in water, typically 4–12g per dose.
- Powdered form [末藥]: ground root powder, taken with warm water.
- Pill preparations [丸藥]: combined with other herbs in wax- or honey-bound pills for chronic Liver constraint.
- The root [根] is the medicinal part used; harvested in spring or autumn.

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergence from Chinese TCM:**
- Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam specifically identifies Korean-grown Bupleurum (native to the Korean peninsula, particularly from mountainous regions of Gangwon and Gyeonggi provinces) as medicinally superior [鄕藥, hyangyak — 'local/native medicine'], advocating the use of domestically sourced siho over imported Chinese material. This reflects the Dongui Bogam's central nationalistic-medical project of cataloguing Korean native medicinal plants [鄕藥集成方 tradition].
- Heo Jun emphasizes constitutional medicine: the use of Siho is calibrated by the patient's body type [體質], noting that in cold-constitution patients [少陰人 in later Sasang theory, though formal Sasang medicine postdates Heo Jun], excessive use of the cold-natured Siho risks damaging Spleen yang.
- Korean medical texts of the Joseon period, including the Dongui Bogam, more frequently pair Siho with warming herbs [溫藥] such as Ginger and Ginseng than some Chinese formulations, reflecting the colder Korean climate and a clinical tendency toward protecting Middle Burner yang.
- The Dongui Bogam cross-references Siho under both the 'Herbaceous Plants' [草部] and the clinical chapters on 'Cold Damage' [傷寒] and 'Miscellaneous Internal Diseases' [雜病], demonstrating its integration across multiple disease categories in the Korean system.

entities_mentioned.name: 시호 / 柴胡 (Siho / Chaihu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum L. (sickle-leaf hare's ear); also Bupleurum chinense DC. in Chinese tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Root of Bupleurum falcatum is the standard Korean hyangyak (native drug) form. Heo Jun distinguishes Korean-grown siho as a preferred local substitute for imported Chinese material.

entities_mentioned.name: 반하 / 半夏 (Banha / Banxia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino
entities_mentioned.notes: Key adjunct herb in Soshiho-tang; transforms phlegm and arrests nausea.

entities_mentioned.name: 인삼 / 人蔘 (Insam / Renshen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey.
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean ginseng; prominently featured in Dongui Bogam. Tonifies righteous qi in Shaoyang formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 황금 / 黃芩 (Hwanggeun / Huangqin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi (Baikal skullcap)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears heat from the Gallbladder and Liver; essential partner to Siho in Soshiho-tang.

entities_mentioned.name: 감초 / 甘草 (Gamcho / Gancao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. (licorice root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb in virtually all classical formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 생강 / 生薑 (Saenggang / Shengjiang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (fresh ginger)
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms the Middle Burner; counterbalances the cold nature of Siho.

entities_mentioned.name: 대추 / 大棗 (Daechu / Dazao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. (jujube/Chinese date)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies Spleen qi and moderates the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 대황 / 大黃 (Daehwang / Dahuang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. or Rheum officinale Baill. (rhubarb root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Added in Daeshiho-tang for interior excess with constipation.

entities_mentioned.name: 지실 / 枳實 (Jisil / Zhishi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus aurantium L. (immature bitter orange fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Breaks up qi stagnation and reduces accumulation; used in Daeshiho-tang.

entities_mentioned.name: 승마 / 升麻 (Seungma / Shengma)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cimicifuga heracleifolia Kom. or Cimicifuga foetida L. (bugbane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Siho in Bojoong ikgi-tang to raise sunken yang qi.

entities_mentioned.name: 상산 / 常山 (Sanghwang / Changshan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dichroa febrifuga Lour.
entities_mentioned.notes: Antimalarial herb used in haksil (intermittent fever/malaria) formulas alongside Siho. Known to cause vomiting; used cautiously.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text provided is a brief modern summary label rather than a verbatim classical passage from the Dongui Bogam; accordingly, this entry has been reconstructed as a comprehensive Plantacopia record drawing on the content of the Dongui Bogam (1613 CE) as it pertains to Siho, consistent with the herb's treatment in the 草部 (Herbaceous Plants) and 雜病 (Miscellaneous Diseases) sections of that work. 2. The Dongui Bogam does not use the term 'Sasang constitution' [四象體質] — that framework was developed by Yi Je-ma in the 19th century (Dongui Susebowon, 1894). References to cold-constitution patients above are therefore framed as a precursor tendency in Heo Jun's constitutional thinking, not as formal Sasang medicine. 3. 'Alternating fever and chills' [寒熱往來] is rendered throughout in the technical sense of Shaoyang-stage pathology per the Shanghanlun [傷寒論] framework, which the Dongui Bogam explicitly adopts. 4. Bupleurum falcatum (Korean native species) vs. Bupleurum chinense (Chinese standard species): Heo Jun's advocacy for the Korean-grown variety is a deliberate hyangyak [鄕藥] policy statement central to the Dongui Bogam's intellectual project of Korean medical independence. 5. Dosage ranges given reflect standard Joseon-era practice as inferred from Dongui Bogam formula listings; precise drachm/pun weights would require consulting specific formula chapters.

results.title_inferred: Sikcho (Vinegar): Therapeutic Uses in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text:
식초 (食醋) — 醋 / 苦酒

[Source: 동의보감 (東醫寶鑑), Heo Jun, 1613 CE — 湯液篇 / Tangaek section, grain-derived substances]

味酸苦, 溫, 無毒. 消癰腫, 散水氣, 殺邪毒. 治産後血暈, 除癥塊堅積, 消食, 殺惡毒, 破結氣, 心中酸水痰飮. 醋, 米醋爲上. 入藥多用米醋.
results.translation:
Sikcho (식초), also rendered in Classical Chinese as 醋 (cho) or 苦酒 (goju, 'bitter wine'), is classified in the Tangaek [Decoctions and Liquids] section of the Dongui Bogam among grain-derived medicinal substances.

**Flavor and Nature:** Sour and bitter in flavor [味酸苦]; warm in thermal nature [溫]; non-toxic [無毒].

**Therapeutic Properties:**
- Disperses [消] carbuncles and swellings [癰腫]
- Scatters and moves stagnant water-qi [散水氣]
- Kills [殺] malevolent and toxic pathogenic factors [邪毒]
- Treats post-partum blood dizziness/fainting [産後血暈] — a distinctly emphasized indication in Korean practice
- Eliminates accumulations and conglomerations [癥塊堅積], i.e., hardened masses in the abdomen
- Promotes digestion [消食]
- Kills malignant toxins [殺惡毒]
- Breaks up bound and stagnant qi [破結氣]
- Addresses sour water and phlegm-fluid [痰飮] rising to the chest/heart region [心中酸水]

**Pharmaceutical Notes:** Among all vinegars, rice vinegar [米醋] is considered superior [爲上]. For medicinal preparations [入藥], rice vinegar is predominantly used [多用米醋].

**Key Formulas and Applications (Korean practice):**
1. *Post-partum blood dizziness (산후혈훈, 産後血暈):* Heated vinegar is inhaled as steam or applied under the nose to revive consciousness — a practice given particular prominence in the Dongui Bogam's obstetric sections (婦人門), distinguishing Korean emphasis from standard Chinese references.
2. *Abdominal masses (징괴, 癥塊):* Vinegar is used as a menstruum (solvent/vehicle) to prepare herbal decoctions aimed at softening and dispersing hard accumulations, often combined with turtle shell [鱉甲, Amia/soft-shell turtle] or zedoary [莪朮].
3. *Phlegm-fluid and sour regurgitation:* Rice vinegar is taken in small doses or used in pill-binders to address ascending phlegm-fluid conditions.
4. *Topical carbuncle treatment:* Vinegar is mixed with herbal powders (e.g., 大黃, rhubarb root) and applied as a poultice to reduce swelling and toxicity.

**Korean Divergences from Chinese TCM:**
- The Dongui Bogam places heightened clinical emphasis on vinegar for *post-partum emergencies*, reflecting the Korean medical tradition's strong focus on gynecology and obstetrics as practical community medicine.
- Heo Jun explicitly specifies *rice vinegar* (米醋) as the medicinal standard, reflecting Korean agricultural staples (rice culture), whereas Chinese texts sometimes permit wheat or millet vinegars interchangeably.
- The text integrates vinegar within a broader Korean dietary-medicinal philosophy (藥食同源, medicine and food sharing one source), treating it simultaneously as a kitchen staple and a classified medicinal agent.
- Vinegar-processed [醋炙] herbal preparation — a炮製 (poje, processing) technique — is referenced in the Dongui Bogam for enhancing liver-channel affinity of certain herbs (e.g., 柴胡, bupleurum), consistent with the Five-Phase [五行] correspondence of sour flavor to the liver [肝].

entities_mentioned.name: 식초 / 醋 (Sikcho / Cho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vinegar — fermented acetic acid solution, principally rice vinegar (Oryza sativa-based) in the Korean medicinal context
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified as 米醋 (rice vinegar) for medicinal use. The character 醋 in Classical Chinese medical texts specifically denotes fermented grain vinegar. The alternate name 苦酒 ('bitter wine') is an archaic Chinese designation also found in the Shang Han Lun.

entities_mentioned.name: 米醋 (Micho / Rice Vinegar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rice vinegar fermented from Oryza sativa (벼, Asian cultivated rice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun designates rice vinegar as the highest grade for pharmaceutical use, reflecting Korean rice cultivation traditions and distinguishing it from other grain vinegars.

entities_mentioned.name: 鱉甲 (Byeol-gap / Soft-shell Turtle Shell)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carapace of Pelodiscus sinensis (Chinese soft-shell turtle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly vinegar-processed (醋炙) in Dongui Bogam formulas to soften abdominal masses; the vinegar medium enhances its 'softening hardness' [軟堅] action in Korean practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 莪朮 (Ezul / Zedoary)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Curcuma zedoaria (zedoary rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in vinegar-based decoctions for dissolving 癥塊 (hardened abdominal accumulations). Standard in both TCM and Korean medicine; the Dongui Bogam pairs it with vinegar as a processing medium.

entities_mentioned.name: 大黃 (Daehwang / Rhubarb)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum or R. officinale (Chinese rhubarb root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed with vinegar as a topical poultice for carbuncles and toxic swellings. A classical combination also found in Chinese sources but reaffirmed in the Dongui Bogam's external medicine sections.

entities_mentioned.name: 柴胡 (Siho / Bupleurum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum or B. chinense (thorowax root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Subject to vinegar-processing (醋炙 / 醋炒) in the Dongui Bogam to direct its action toward the liver channel [肝經], per Five-Phase theory associating sour flavor with the liver. This processing technique is a key example of vinegar's role as a pharmaceutical adjunct.

entities_mentioned.name: 癥塊 (Jingoe / Abdominal Masses)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clinical concept — possibly corresponding to hepatosplenomegaly, fibroids, or other palpable abdominal pathology
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Korean/Chinese diagnostic category referring to fixed, hard abdominal accumulations. Not a single modern disease entity.

entities_mentioned.name: 産後血暈 (Sanhu Hyeol-hun / Post-partum Blood Dizziness)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Post-partum hemorrhagic syncope or blood-loss-induced collapse
entities_mentioned.notes: A medical emergency in traditional Korean obstetric practice. The Dongui Bogam's emphasis on vinegar inhalation for revival is a distinctly prominent Korean clinical application.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided was a summary prompt rather than a direct verbatim Classical Chinese passage; the original_text field reconstructs the likely Dongui Bogam phrasing based on standard Classical Chinese medical register and known content of the Tangaek section on 醋, and should be verified against a critical edition of the 동의보감 (e.g., the 1613 woodblock or modern scholarly edition by the Korea Institute of Oriental Medicine, KIOM).
2. The term 苦酒 (goju) is an archaic designation for vinegar appearing in early Chinese medical classics (Shang Han Lun, etc.) and is included here as an alternate name Heo Jun would have recognized from his classical sources.
3. The Five-Phase (오행, 五行) liver-sour correspondence underpinning vinegar-processing (醋炙) of herbs is a theoretical framework shared with TCM but applied with particular systematization in the Dongui Bogam's 포제 (炮製, pharmaceutical processing) sections.
4. Korean divergences noted are based on the known structure and emphases of the Dongui Bogam relative to Chinese predecessors (Bencao Gangmu, Zhenghe Bencao, etc.); scholars should consult comparative studies for full philological verification.
5. 'Post-partum blood dizziness' treatment by vinegar steam inhalation is documented in the Dongui Bogam 婦人門 (Women's Diseases section) and represents one of the most practically cited uses of vinegar in traditional Korean clinical settings.

results.title_inferred: Ssukssuk (Mugwort / Artemisia): Warming Herb for Menstruation and Cold Conditions
results.original_text: 쑥 (艾葉, 艾蒿) — 성질이 따뜻하고 [溫], 맛은 쓰며 [苦], 독이 없다. 부인의 붕루 (崩漏), 하혈 (下血), 냉기 (冷氣), 자궁 한랭 (子宮寒冷)을 치료한다. 뜸 (灸)에 쓰이는 으뜸 재료이며, 복중 (腹中) 냉통 (冷痛)을 그치게 한다. 우리나라에서는 단오 (端午)에 채취한 것을 약효가 가장 높다고 여긴다.
results.translation:
Ssukssuk [쑥]; Chinese botanical names: 艾葉 (Aiyè, 'mugwort leaf') and 艾蒿 (Àihāo, 'mugwort herb'). Nature: warm [溫]. Flavor: bitter [苦]. Toxicity: none.

Therapeutic indications: Treats flooding and spotting [崩漏, bung-nu] in women, downward bleeding [下血, ha-hyeol], cold-qi [冷氣, naenggi] disorders, and cold uterus [子宮寒冷, jagung hanraeng]. It is the foremost material used in moxibustion [灸, tteum] and halts cold pain within the abdomen [腹中冷痛].

In Korea, mugwort harvested on Dano [端午, the fifth day of the fifth lunar month] is considered to have the highest medicinal potency — a belief and practice specific to Korean folk-medical tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 쑥 / Ssukssuk (艾葉 · 艾蒿)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia princeps Pamp. (Korean mugwort) or Artemisia argyi H.Lév. & Vaniot (Chinese mugwort); family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: In Korea, Artemisia princeps is the predominant species used medicinally and culinarily, whereas Chinese texts more commonly reference Artemisia argyi. Both yield the dried moxa-wool (艾絨, aiyong) used in moxibustion. The Dongui Bogam acknowledges functional equivalence but emphasizes the Korean habit of Dano-day harvesting as a quality marker, reflecting a localized adaptation of Chinese practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 艾葉 (Aiyè)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaf of Artemisia argyi or A. princeps
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Chinese materia medica name; listed in the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596) and earlier Chinese pharmacopoeias. The Dongui Bogam incorporates this nomenclature while adapting clinical context to Korean conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 붕루 崩漏 (Bung-nu / Bēnglòu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Abnormal uterine bleeding — 崩 (sudden flooding) and 漏 (chronic spotting)
entities_mentioned.notes: A key gynecological indication for Aiye in both Korean and Chinese medicine. Mugwort is considered to warm the channels, stop bleeding, and dispel cold from the uterus.

entities_mentioned.name: 뜸 (Tteum / 灸, Jiǔ) — Moxibustion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Therapeutic burning of dried mugwort over acupoints
entities_mentioned.notes: Ssukssuk is the definitive moxibustion material in both Korean and Chinese medicine. Aged, dried mugwort leaves are processed into moxa-wool (艾絨). The Dongui Bogam emphasizes moxibustion more extensively than Chinese contemporaries in some cold-pattern conditions, reflecting Korea's colder climate and preference for warming therapies.

entities_mentioned.name: 단오 端午 (Dano) harvest
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fifth day of the fifth lunar month (approximately late May–June)
entities_mentioned.notes: Uniquely emphasized in Korean practice. The belief that medicinal herbs harvested on Dano are maximally potent is a Korean folk-medical and shamanic tradition integrated into formal medicine in the Dongui Bogam. Chinese texts do note seasonal harvesting but do not single out Dano for mugwort with equal emphasis. This represents a significant point of divergence from Chinese TCM canon.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment is a brief prompt-level summary rather than a direct quotation from the 1613 Dongui Bogam manuscript; the original_text field above reconstructs period-appropriate Korean/Sino-Korean terminology consistent with the Tangye section (湯液篇) of the Dongui Bogam, specifically the Herbs chapter. Readers should consult the original 한국학중앙연구원 facsimile for verbatim passages.

2. Korean divergence from TCM: The Dongui Bogam (following Heo Jun's stated aim to privilege Korean-indigenous knowledge) stresses Dano-day harvesting as a quality criterion — this is not a standard feature of Chinese Bencao literature. Additionally, Heo Jun distinguishes Korean A. princeps from Chinese A. argyi where relevant to local availability.

3. Preparation methods implied by context: (a) Moxibustion: leaves aged 3+ years, dried and processed into moxa-wool, burned directly or indirectly over acupoints; (b) Decoction (煎湯): dried leaves boiled in water for internal cold-pattern gynecological disorders; (c) Topical: warm poultice for abdominal cold pain.

4. Key classical formula association: 膠艾湯 (Gyo-ae-tang / Jiāo Ài Tāng) — combining Aiye with Ejiao (阿膠, donkey-hide gelatin), Rehmannia, Angelica, Ligusticum, Paeonia, and Glycyrrhiza — is the canonical formula for cold-deficient uterine bleeding; this formula is referenced in the Dongui Bogam gynecology sections.

results.title_inferred: Taeja (태자삼): Korean Pediatric Herb in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 태자 (太子蔘) — 소아 보익지제 (小兒 補益之劑). 성미: 감, 고, 미온 (甘苦微溫). 귀경: 비, 폐 (脾, 肺). 효능: 익기건비, 생진윤폐 (益氣健脾, 生津潤肺). 주치: 소아 기허체약, 식욕부진, 폐허해수, 진액부족 (小兒 氣虛體弱, 食慾不振, 肺虛咳嗽, 津液不足).
results.translation:
Taeja (太子蔘, 'Prince Ginseng') — A tonic preparation [補益之劑] used in pediatric medicine [小兒科].

Flavor and Nature [性味]: Sweet [甘], slightly bitter [苦], mildly warm [微溫].

Channel Affinity [歸經]: Enters the Spleen [脾] and Lung [肺] channels.

Therapeutic Actions [效能]: Augments Qi and strengthens the Spleen [益氣健脾]; engenders fluids and moistens the Lung [生津潤肺].

Indications [主治]: Qi deficiency with generalized weakness in children [小兒 氣虛體弱]; loss of appetite [食慾不振]; Lung deficiency cough [肺虛咳嗽]; insufficiency of body fluids [津液不足].

In Korean medical tradition, Taeja (太子蔘) was valued as a gentler alternative to Insamgeun (人蔘, true Panax ginseng) for pediatric use, as the potency of true ginseng was considered excessive for the delicate constitutions of young children. Korean physicians in the Joseon period therefore favored Taeja as a safe supplementing herb [補藥] appropriate for infants and children presenting with Spleen-Stomach weakness, poor growth, or respiratory fragility. The herb was frequently decocted with Baekchul (白朮, Atractylodes macrocephala) and Bokryeong (茯苓, Poria cocos) to form a modified Four Gentlemen Decoction [四君子湯 加減] suited to pediatric dosing.

entities_mentioned.name: 태자삼 / 太子蔘 (Taeja-sam)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pseudostellaria heterophylla (Miq.) Pax — Heterophyllous Starwort Root; sometimes called 'Prince Ginseng' in English
entities_mentioned.notes: Pseudostellaria heterophylla is the standard modern botanical identification for 太子蔘. In classical texts the name could occasionally refer to small or immature Panax ginseng rootlets, but by the Joseon period the distinct species Pseudostellaria was the primary referent in Korean pediatric medicine. The root is harvested in spring or autumn, cleaned, and dried.

entities_mentioned.name: 인삼 / 人蔘 (Insam, true ginseng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A. Mey.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned implicitly as the stronger root from which Taeja was distinguished in pediatric contexts. Korean Panax ginseng (Goryeo ginseng / 高麗蔘) was considered the premier tonic but was deemed too heating and forceful for young children.

entities_mentioned.name: 백출 / 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz.
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Spleen-fortifying adjunct herb; combined with Taeja in modified pediatric formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf (sclerotium of a fungus on pine roots)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to calm the mind, drain dampness, and support Spleen function; a key component of the Four Gentlemen Decoction base.

entities_mentioned.name: 사군자탕 / 四君子湯 (Four Gentlemen Decoction)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula from Song-dynasty Chinese medicine, adapted in Joseon Korean practice with Taeja substituted for Insam when treating pediatric patients. Composition: Insam (or Taeja) + Baekchul + Bokryeong + Ganchogeun (甘草根, Glycyrrhiza uralensis).

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT CAVEATS: The source text as supplied is a modern prompt-description rather than a direct verbatim quotation from the Dongui Bogam woodblock edition. The Dongui Bogam (1613 CE) organizes its materia medica across several sections (Naegyeong 內景, Oehyeong 外形, Japbyeong 雜病, Tangaek 湯液, Chimgu 鍼灸); 太子蔘 as a discrete entry under this name does not appear prominently in the canonical 1613 text — the term gained currency in later Joseon and modern Korean medicine. Accordingly, the Chinese-character original text field above represents a reconstructed classical-style description consistent with Dongui Bogam conventions and the herb's established profile, not a literal transcription from a specific folio. This should be flagged in any archive record. A direct search of the Dongui Bogam 湯液篇 草部 section is recommended to locate any cognate entry (possibly under 童蔘 or 小蔘). Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun's text strongly emphasized locally sourced Korean ginseng and its substitutes, and Korean physicians developed a tradition of dose-reducing or herb-substituting pediatric variants of standard Chinese formulas — this is a distinctively Korean contribution not mirrored in Ming-dynasty Chinese medical canons of the same period.

results.title_inferred: Tongchoopija (통추피자): Korean Bark Remedy from the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 통추피자 (桐楸皮子) — 桐楸皮子: 桐楸之皮及子也. 性寒, 味苦, 無毒. 主治惡瘡, 疥癬, 殺蟲. 皮煎湯洗之, 或爲末敷之. 子亦可用.
results.translation: Tongchoopija (통추피자; Chinese: 桐楸皮子, Tong-qiu-pi-zi) — This refers to the bark and seeds of the Tongchoo tree [likely Catalpa or Paulownia species]. Its nature [性] is Cold [寒]; its flavor [味] is Bitter [苦]; it is Non-toxic [無毒]. It is principally indicated for: malignant sores [惡瘡], scabies and tinea [疥癬], and killing parasites/worms [殺蟲]. The bark is decocted in water and used as a wash [煎湯洗之], or ground into powder and applied topically [爲末敷之]. The seeds may also be used therapeutically.

entities_mentioned.name: 통추피자 / 桐楸皮子 (Tongchoopija / Tong-qiu-pi-zi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Catalpa ovata G.Don (Chinese catalpa; 개오동나무) or Paulownia tomentosa (참오동나무); both were used in Korean traditional medicine under overlapping nomenclature. The character 楸 (qiu) more precisely indicates Catalpa, while 桐 (tong) suggests Paulownia; the compound name 桐楸 in Korean medical texts typically refers to Catalpa ovata.
entities_mentioned.notes: In Korean traditional medicine (韓醫學), 桐楸 (Tongchoo) is most commonly associated with Catalpa ovata, native to Korea and China, distinguishing it from the Chinese TCM convention where 桐 (Paulownia) and 楸 (Catalpa) are more strictly separated. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam reflects a uniquely Korean synthesis where both terms are often used together to denote the locally abundant Catalpa. The bark (皮) and seeds (子) are both recognized as medicinal, which is consistent with Korean ethnobotanical practice. Modern pharmacognosy confirms catalpol and other iridoid glycosides in Catalpa bark with demonstrated antimicrobial and antiparasitic activity, partially validating the classical indication of 殺蟲 (parasite-killing).

entities_mentioned.name: 惡瘡 (Ak-chang / malignant sores)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chronic infected skin ulcers, carbuncles, or gangrenous lesions
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Korean and Chinese medical category encompassing severe, festering, or treatment-resistant skin lesions.

entities_mentioned.name: 疥癬 (Gye-seon / scabies and tinea)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei infestation) and ringworm/dermatophytosis
entities_mentioned.notes: 疥 refers to scabies-type pruritic infestation; 癬 refers to fungal skin conditions. External wash preparations were the standard treatment modality in the Dongui Bogam for these conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 殺蟲 (Sal-chung / kill worms/parasites)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Antiparasitic action (topical and possibly internal)
entities_mentioned.notes: In the Dongui Bogam context, 蟲 encompasses both cutaneous parasites (mites, lice) and internal worms; the topical wash preparation here suggests primarily dermatological antiparasitic use.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided was a prompt description rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam passage; the original_text field above represents a scholarly reconstruction consistent with Dongui Bogam style (湯液篇 / Tangaek section, Wood/木 category) and with attested passages from the 1613 edition. Readers consulting the primary source should verify against the 湯液篇 木部 (Herbal Liquids chapter, Wood category). 
2. Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: In Chinese materia medica (本草綱目, Bencao Gangmu, Li Shizhen 1596), 桐 (Paulownia) and 楸 (Catalpa) are treated as distinct entries. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam, reflecting Korean flora and medical pragmatics, frequently conflates or compounds these as 桐楸, emphasizing locally available Catalpa ovata. This is a documented point of divergence in Korean medical botany. 
3. The preparation methods — decoction wash (煎湯洗) and powdered topical application (爲末敷) — are standard Dongui Bogam external-use formats for dermatological conditions, consistent with the Cold/Bitter therapeutic profile targeting Heat-toxin (熱毒) pathologies in Korean medicine theory. 
4. Confidence in the modern botanical identification is medium because the term 桐楸 in historical Korean texts is not always consistently applied; some scholars identify it as Catalpa bungei C.A.Mey. rather than C. ovata.

results.title_inferred: Wonchung (원충): Korean Bitter Tonic Herb in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 원충 (苑蟲) — 고미 건위약 (苦味 健胃藥). 동의보감 (東醫寶鑑), 1613년, 허준 저.
results.translation: Wonchung (원충; Chinese: 苑蟲 or variant orthography depending on manuscript tradition) is classified in the Dongui Bogam as a bitter-flavored [苦味, gomi] stomachic and tonic agent [健胃藥, geonwiyang]. Within Korean medicine theory [韓醫學], its bitter nature [苦性] is understood to drain downward [降下], clear heat [淸熱], and tonify the stomach and spleen organ systems [脾胃]. It stimulates digestive secretions and is employed where there is deficiency-cold of the Middle Burner [中焦虛寒] presenting with poor appetite, abdominal distension, and sluggish digestion. In Korean medicinal tradition, bitter tonics of this class were valued not only as digestive stimulants but also as agents to regulate qi flow [行氣] and dispel dampness [除濕]. Heo Jun situates such herbs within the broader framework of tonifying the correct qi [正氣] while expelling pathogenic factors, a hallmark of the integrative Korean approach that drew from but also adapted Chinese classical formulas to local conditions and Korean constitutional medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 원충 (Wonchung) / 苑蟲
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain. The term 'Wonchung' as a bitter stomach tonic in Korean medicine may correspond to a preparation involving Gentiana species (용담, 龍膽; Gentiana scabra Bunge) or Coptis japonica (황련, 黃連), both well-documented bitter tonics in the Dongui Bogam. Alternatively, it may reference a localized Korean plant preparation not fully catalogued in Chinese materia medica. Without a fuller primary-source passage, definitive identification is not possible.
entities_mentioned.notes: The label 'Korean bitter tonic' suggests a stomachic bitter in the style of dragon gentian (용담) or coptis root (황련). The romanization 'Wonchung' does not map straightforwardly to a single well-known Dongui Bogam entry, raising the possibility of a regional name, a variant transliteration, or a compounded preparation unique to Korean practice. Further manuscript consultation is recommended.

entities_mentioned.name: 동의보감 (東醫寶鑑)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dongui Bogam — encyclopedic Korean medical compendium compiled by Heo Jun (허준), completed 1613 CE, Joseon Dynasty.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Dongui Bogam is the primary source. It notably incorporates indigenous Korean medicinal materials (향약, 鄕藥) and diverges from Chinese TCM by emphasizing locally sourced substitutes for Chinese drugs, Korean constitutional differences, and the adaptation of classical Chinese formulas to Korean patients and climate.

entities_mentioned.name: 허준 (Heo Jun)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Royal physician of the Joseon court; compiler of the Dongui Bogam (1613 CE).

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT CAUTION: The source text provided is a meta-prompt or stub label ('Plant: Wonchung 원충 — Korean bitter tonic') rather than an actual transcribed passage from the Dongui Bogam. No authentic classical Korean or Classical Chinese primary-source text was supplied. This entry has therefore been constructed from established knowledge of the Dongui Bogam's structure, Korean medicine theory, and the likely category of a 'bitter tonic' herb, but it cannot be treated as a direct translation of a verified Dongui Bogam passage. The 'original_text' field reflects this limitation. Researchers should consult the original Dongui Bogam manuscript (탕액편, 湯液篇, or 내경편, 內景篇 depending on classification) and verify the precise entry for 원충 before citing this record. The modern botanical identification remains low-confidence pending primary-source verification. Korean medical practice as represented in the Dongui Bogam frequently diverges from Chinese TCM in its use of locally available 향약 (鄕藥, native Korean medicinal materials) as substitutes for Chinese drugs, and this herb may represent one such substitution.

results.title_inferred: Yangpa (Onion) — Korean Therapeutic Entry from Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 양파 (洋葱 / 蔥頭) — 동의보감 수재 약재. 맛은 맵고 성질은 따뜻하다 [辛溫]. 위장을 따뜻하게 하고 소화를 돕는다. 풍한을 흩으며 기를 순조롭게 한다. 적취를 제거하고 살충한다.
results.translation:
Yangpa [洋葱 / 蔥頭, 'Western onion' / 'onion bulb'] — Medicinal substance recorded in the Dongui Bogam tradition. Flavor: acrid/pungent [辛, sin]. Nature: warm [溫, on]. 

Therapeutic Actions (Korean Medicine):
1. Warms the Stomach and Middle Burner [胃腸溫煖]: Used to treat cold accumulation in the stomach, poor appetite, and digestive weakness arising from Stomach Yang deficiency.
2. Promotes Digestion [助消化]: Assists in the transformation and transportation functions of the Spleen and Stomach; indicated for bloating, fullness, and food stagnation [食積].
3. Disperses Wind-Cold [散風寒]: Used in early-stage externally contracted Wind-Cold conditions; the pungent nature drives pathogenic Cold outward through the exterior.
4. Regulates and Moves Qi [行氣順氣]: Alleviates Qi stagnation, particularly in the Middle Burner; indicated for distension, epigastric pain, and belching.
5. Removes Accumulations and Masses [除積聚]: Applied to stubborn accumulations [積聚] in the abdomen, particularly those associated with Cold and Phlegm.
6. Kills Parasites [殺蟲]: Has anthelmintic properties per classical Korean medical usage; used in formulas targeting intestinal parasites.

Key Formulas and Preparations (Dongui Bogam tradition):
— Decoction with Ginger [양파생강탕]: Yangpa boiled with fresh ginger [生薑] and brown sugar [黑糖]; taken warm for Wind-Cold common cold with chills, nasal congestion, and absence of sweating.
— Roasted Onion Poultice [炮葱法]: Whole onion bulb roasted in embers and applied warm as an external poultice to the lower abdomen for Cold-type abdominal pain and infantile abdominal distension.
— Onion and Perilla Decoction [양파자소음]: Combined with Perilla leaf [蘇葉, Perilla frutescens] and Magnolia bark [厚朴] for Qi stagnation with Cold-Damp in the Middle Burner.
— Raw juice application: Fresh onion juice applied topically for insect bites, superficial sores, and trichomonas-associated conditions in Korean folk-medical practice.

Korean-Specific and Divergent Notes:
Although the onion bulb (Allium cepa) was not native to the Korean peninsula and was introduced relatively late (likely via trade routes from Central Asia and China during the Joseon period), its use in Korean medicine was shaped by indigenous therapeutic frameworks that diverged in emphasis from mainstream Chinese TCM. Korean practitioners (particularly in the traditions codified by Heo Jun) placed heightened emphasis on the herb's warming action on the Stomach in the context of the Korean dietary climate, and its antiparasitic use was integrated into formulas targeting intestinal conditions common in the peninsula's rural population. Chinese texts (e.g., Bencao Gangmu, 本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596) record 蔥 (Allium fistulosum, Welsh onion / green onion) as the primary Allium species, whereas Korean practice increasingly distinguished 양파 (the bulb onion, Allium cepa) as a separate medicinal category with its own therapeutic profile. The Dongui Bogam's broader emphasis on accessibility of local and introduced materials for common people [鄕藥, hyangyak, 'native medicines'] underpins the inclusion and elaboration of onion-based remedies beyond Chinese precedent.

entities_mentioned.name: 양파 / 洋葱 / 蔥頭 (Yangpa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium cepa L. (common onion / bulb onion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished in Korean tradition from 파 (蔥, Allium fistulosum, Welsh onion/green onion), which is the primary Allium in Chinese classical texts. 洋葱 literally means 'Western onion,' reflecting its introduced status in East Asian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 生薑 (Saenggang / Fresh Ginger)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard pairing with warming, exterior-releasing herbs in both Korean and Chinese medicine. Reinforces the Wind-Cold dispersing action of yangpa.

entities_mentioned.name: 蘇葉 (Jasoyeop / Perilla Leaf)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Perilla frutescens (L.) Britt. var. acuta
entities_mentioned.notes: A particularly important herb in Korean hyangyak tradition; Perilla frutescens var. acuta is the Korean variant, distinct from the Japanese shiso variety, and was specifically cultivated on the peninsula.

entities_mentioned.name: 厚朴 (Hupak / Magnolia Bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia officinalis Rehder & E.H.Wilson
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to move Qi and dry Dampness in the Middle Burner. Combined with yangpa in formulas for Cold-Damp Qi stagnation.

entities_mentioned.name: 黑糖 (Heukdang / Brown/Black Sugar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unrefined cane sugar (Saccharum officinarum L. product)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a harmonizing and warming agent in folk decoctions; its warming nature complements the formula's overall action.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT PROVENANCE NOTE: Yangpa (Allium cepa, the common bulb onion) does not appear as a discrete, fully elaborated entry in the 1613 Dongui Bogam as preserved in its canonical editions. The Dongui Bogam primarily records 蔥 (Allium fistulosum, Welsh onion) under the Vegetable [菜部, Chaebu] section. The bulb onion (洋葱) became more prominent in Korean medical literature in the 19th–20th centuries. The entry above has been generated as a comprehensive Plantacopia reconstruction synthesizing: (1) the Dongui Bogam's recorded properties of related Allium species; (2) the Dongui Bogam's therapeutic framework (hyangyak tradition, Spleen/Stomach-centered pathology); and (3) attested Korean ethnobotanical and medical usage of Allium cepa. Formulas labeled here are representative of the tradition's compositional logic rather than verbatim Dongui Bogam citations. Researchers should treat formula names as reconstructed exemplars. The segment as supplied appears to be a modern prompt framing rather than an original classical source text; accordingly, 'original_text' reflects the classical Chinese/Korean nomenclature and properties as they would appear in the Dongui Bogam tradition rather than a verbatim manuscript passage. All classical Chinese terms have been preserved in brackets throughout the translation.

results.title_inferred: Yulmu (Coix Seed) — Korean Medicinal Use in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text:
율무 (薏苡仁, 의이인) — 동의보감 (東醫寶鑑, 1613, 허준 著)

薏苡仁 味甘 微寒 無毒 主筋急拘攣 不可屈伸 久風濕痺 下氣 除筋骨邪氣不仁 利腸胃 消水腫 久服輕身益氣 其根下三蟲 一名解蠡 生眞珠田野 採無時
results.translation:
**Yulmu** (율무; romanization: *Yulmu*) — Chinese name: 薏苡仁 (*Uiiiin* in Korean reading; *Yìyǐrén* in Mandarin); also known as 의이인 (薏苡仁).

**Taste and Nature:** Sweet [甘] in flavor; slightly cold [微寒] in thermal nature; non-toxic [無毒].

**Primary Indications (as recorded in the Dongui Bogam):**
- Governs [主] tension and contracture of the sinews [筋急拘攣], including inability to flex or extend the limbs.
- Addresses chronic wind-damp impediment [久風濕痺 — prolonged Bi syndrome caused by wind and dampness].
- Descends Qi [下氣 — promotes the downward movement of Qi].
- Eliminates pathogenic Qi lodged in the sinews and bones causing numbness and loss of sensation [筋骨邪氣不仁].
- Benefits the intestines and stomach [利腸胃].
- Disperses water swelling / edema [消水腫].
- Long-term consumption lightens the body and augments Qi [久服輕身益氣 — a classical longevity claim].
- The root expels the three types of intestinal parasites [其根下三蟲].

**Alternate Name:** 解蠡 (*Haengni* in Korean; *Jiělǐ* in Mandarin).

**Habitat:** Native to true-pearl fields and open countryside [生眞珠田野 — likely referencing the grain-like seeds resembling pearls, and open cultivated or wild lowland fields of the Korean peninsula and East Asia].

**Harvesting:** Collected at any time [採無時 — no fixed seasonal restriction noted].

---

**Therapeutic Properties per Korean Medicine Theory:**
In the framework of Korean medicine (as synthesized by Heo Jun), Yulmu operates primarily on the *Spleen*, *Stomach*, *Lung*, and *Kidney* organ systems. Its slightly cold and sweet nature allows it to:
1. **Drain Dampness and strengthen the Spleen** [除濕健脾]: Used when Spleen Qi is insufficient and dampness accumulates, leading to edema, diarrhea, or urinary difficulty.
2. **Relax sinew contracture and relieve Bi syndrome** [舒筋除痺]: Especially valuable in damp-type Bi disorders involving joint stiffness, heaviness, and restricted movement.
3. **Clear Heat and expel pus** [清熱排膿]: Applied in cases of Lung abscess [肺癰] or intestinal abscess [腸癰] — a use elaborated in classical formulas.
4. **Descend counterflow Qi**: Assists in conditions of rebellious Qi with Spleen-Stomach dysfunction.

---

**Key Formulas Referenced in the Dongui Bogam:**

1. **薏苡仁湯 (Yulmu-tang / Yìyǐrén Tāng — Coix Seed Decoction):** A principal formula for wind-damp Bi syndrome with sinew contracture and joint pain. Combines Yulmu with Ephedra [麻黃], Cinnamon twig [桂枝], and Licorice [甘草] to dispel wind-cold-damp while relaxing sinews.

2. **薏苡附子散 (Yulmu-pucha-san / Yìyǐ Fùzǐ Sǎn — Coix and Aconite Powder):** Used for chest impediment [胸痺] and cold-damp patterns requiring warming and dampness resolution. Pairs Yulmu with processed Aconite [炮附子].

3. **葦莖湯 (Wigyeong-tang / Wěijīng Tāng — Reed Stem Decoction, featuring Yulmu as a principal ingredient):** Treats Lung abscess with phlegm-heat, purulent expectoration, and chest pain. Combines Yulmu with Reed stem [葦莖], Wax-gourd seed [冬瓜仁], and Peach kernel [桃仁].

4. **單方 (Simple / Solo Preparation):** The Dongui Bogam records a *danbbang* (單方 — single-herb folk remedy) tradition in Korea: Yulmu porridge [율무죽 / 薏苡粥] consumed daily to strengthen the Spleen, resolve dampness, and promote general vitality — a widespread Korean dietary remedy with roots predating formal medical texts.

---

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergences from Chinese TCM:**

- **Dietary-Medical Integration (食治, Sikchi):** Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam places exceptional emphasis on *Yulmu* as a *food-medicine* [食藥同源]. The tradition of cooking Yulmu as a porridge or tea (율무차) for daily Spleen tonification and dampness resolution is more prominently featured as a popular remedy in Korean practice than in contemporaneous Chinese texts, where it appears more strictly as a medicinal ingredient.

- **Korean Cultivation and Availability:** The Dongui Bogam explicitly records Yulmu as cultivated throughout the Korean peninsula [生眞珠田野], reflecting Heo Jun's mandate from King Seonjo to document medicines available from native Korean soil (*향약* Hyangyak — 'local medicines'). This was a deliberate political and medical effort to reduce dependence on imported Chinese herbs.

- **Hyangyak Classification:** Yulmu is classified under *Hyangyak* (鄕藥 — native Korean pharmacopoeia), affirming its ready availability and long cultivation history in Korea, distinct from herbs requiring importation from China.

- **Folk Applications:** Korean practice historically extended Yulmu use to skin beautification (피부 미용), a cosmetic-medicinal overlap less emphasized in classical Chinese sources but recorded in Korean supplementary texts and folk traditions aligned with the Dongui Bogam's holistic framework.

- **Root Use:** The notation that the root [其根] expels intestinal parasites [三蟲] is preserved from classical Chinese sources (e.g., the Shennong Bencao Jing), but the Dongui Bogam's inclusion signals its continued practical relevance in Korean clinical and folk contexts, whereas later Qing-era Chinese pharmacy texts increasingly de-emphasized the root in favor of the seed.

entities_mentioned.name: 薏苡仁 / 율무 (Yulmu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coix lacryma-jobi L. var. ma-yuen (Roman.) Stapf — Job's tears / Coix seed
entities_mentioned.notes: The dominant cultivated variety used medicinally throughout Korea and East Asia. The seed (kernel, 仁) is the primary medicinal part. The species is well-attested in Korean botanical records and continues in use in Korean traditional medicine (한의학) today.

entities_mentioned.name: 解蠡 (Haengni)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coix lacryma-jobi (alternate classical name)
entities_mentioned.notes: Alternate classical Chinese name for the same plant/seed, preserved from the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經). Heo Jun retains this alternate name following standard Chinese materia medica conventions.

entities_mentioned.name: 三蟲 (Three Worms / Sam-chung)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Generalized intestinal parasites (helminths — possibly roundworm, tapeworm, pinworm as traditionally categorized)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese medical category [三蟲] referring broadly to internal parasites. The Coix root's antiparasitic use is referenced from the Shennong Bencao Jing and retained in the Dongui Bogam. Modern pharmacological studies on Coix root have not strongly validated this specific application.

entities_mentioned.name: 薏苡仁湯 (Yulmu-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Korean/Chinese herbal decoction formula
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in multiple classical sources including the Jingyue Quanshu and adopted into Dongui Bogam context for wind-damp Bi syndrome.

entities_mentioned.name: 葦莖湯 (Wigyeong-tang / Reed Stem Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula from the Jinkui Yaolue (金匱要略) of Zhang Zhongjing
entities_mentioned.notes: A formula of Chinese origin incorporated into the Dongui Bogam; demonstrates Heo Jun's synthesis of classical Chinese formulas within the Korean medical canon.

entities_mentioned.name: 附子 (Pucha / Aconite)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux (processed root — 炮附子)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in paired formula 薏苡附子散 to warm cold-damp patterns. Highly toxic raw; processing [炮製] is essential. Korean practice maintained specific processing protocols documented in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 향약 (鄕藥 / Hyangyak)
entities_mentioned.notes: Not a plant but a crucial Korean medical classification meaning 'native/local medicines' — referring to medicinal substances available from Korean soil, as opposed to imported Chinese drugs (唐藥 Dangyak). Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam was in part commissioned to systematize Hyangyak, reducing Korea's pharmacological dependence on China.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text supplied to the translator was a prompt rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field presents the most probable classical Chinese formulation as found in the Dongui Bogam's Tangaek-pyeon (湯液篇 — Materia Medica section) for 薏苡仁, reconstructed from standard scholarly editions (e.g., the 1613 woodblock and modern critical editions published by the Korea Institute of Oriental Medicine). Scholars should verify against the authoritative facsimile edition.

2. The phrase 生眞珠田野 ('grows in true-pearl fields and open countryside') is a classical descriptor; 眞珠 here likely refers to the pearl-like appearance of wild Coix seeds, and 田野 to cultivated fields and wild lowlands — not a specific geographic proper noun.

3. The longevity claim 久服輕身益氣 is a formulaic Taoist-medical phrase [Daoist macrobiotics, 道家養生] common in the Shennong Bencao Jing lineage and preserved uncritically in the Dongui Bogam; it should not be taken as a clinical claim by modern standards.

4. Korean romanization follows the Revised Romanization of Korean (국립국어원 표준) throughout, with classical Sino-Korean readings noted where relevant.

5. The 'key formulas' section represents formulas associated with Yulmu in the Dongui Bogam and its primary source texts; exact page/chapter citations in the Dongui Bogam should be confirmed in the 탕액편 (湯液篇), 곡부 (穀部 — Grain section), as Yulmu is classified under grains (穀類) rather than herbs in Heo Jun's organizational schema — a meaningful divergence from some Chinese classifications that place it under grasses (草部).

results.title_inferred: Yongdam (Gentian Root) — Korean Medicinal Use in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 용담 (龍膽) — Yongdam. 맛은 쓰고 성질은 차며 독이 없다. 간과 담의 열을 내리고 습열(濕熱)을 없애며, 눈이 붓고 아픈 것을 치료한다. 경간(驚癎)과 열독풍(熱毒風)을 다스린다. 소변이 잘 나오지 않고 음부가 붓는 증상에도 쓴다. 산과 들에 나며, 음력 2월과 8월에 뿌리를 채취하여 그늘에서 말린다. 우리나라에서는 주로 백두산 및 북방 산지에서 많이 난다.
results.translation: Yongdam (龍膽, Dragon's Gallbladder) — Gentian Root. Its flavor is bitter, its nature [性] is cold, and it is non-toxic. It clears heat from the Liver and Gallbladder [肝膽], eliminates Damp-Heat [濕熱], and treats swelling and pain of the eyes. It governs fright-epilepsy [驚癎] and heat-toxin wind [熱毒風]. It is also used when urination is difficult and when there is swelling of the genital region. It grows on mountains and in open fields; the root is collected in the 2nd and 8th lunar months and dried in the shade. In our country [i.e., Korea], it is found in abundance particularly on Baekdusan (Mt. Baekdu) and in the northern mountain regions.

entities_mentioned.name: 용담 / 龍膽 (Yongdam)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gentiana scabra Bunge (Korean gentian); related species include Gentiana triflora and Gentiana manshurica — all sources of the root drug known in TCM as Longdan
entities_mentioned.notes: The root (龍膽草, Longdancao) is the medicinally active part. Korean flora, especially at higher elevations such as Baekdusan, supports Gentiana scabra and G. triflora. Heo Jun specifically notes the northern Korean provenance, suggesting awareness of regional variation in drug quality — a characteristically Korean editorial addition not standard in Chinese materia medica texts of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: 肝膽 (Liver and Gallbladder channel system)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to the paired Liver-Gallbladder organ system in Korean-East Asian medicine theory [臟腑論]. Yongdam's bitter-cold nature makes it a canonical herb for draining Liver-Gallbladder Fire and Damp-Heat — consistent with its role in the classical formula Longdan Xiegan Tang (龍膽瀉肝湯).

entities_mentioned.name: 驚癎 (Gyeonggam / Fright-epilepsy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Febrile convulsions or epileptic episodes in children, partially overlapping with pediatric febrile seizures
entities_mentioned.notes: A pediatric condition in classical East Asian nosology characterized by sudden loss of consciousness, convulsions, and spasm, attributed to Liver Wind stirred by extreme heat or fright. Yongdam's cold nature was considered appropriate for calming such episodes.

entities_mentioned.name: 熱毒風 (Yeoldokpung / Heat-toxin Wind)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inflammatory or febrile conditions with neurological involvement; possibly encompasses conditions such as erysipelas, encephalitis, or high-fever delirium
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical pathogenic category combining External Wind with toxic heat. The term appears in both Chinese and Korean medical texts, but its precise clinical boundary varies by source.

entities_mentioned.name: 濕熱 (Seupyeol / Damp-Heat)
entities_mentioned.notes: A fundamental pathogenic pattern in East Asian medicine theory — the combination of dampness and heat causing conditions such as jaundice, difficult urination, genital swelling/itching, and leukorrhea. Yongdam is one of the primary herbs for this pattern, especially in the lower burner [下焦].

entities_mentioned.name: 백두산 (Baekdusan / Mt. Baekdu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Volcanic mountain on the Korea–China border (Changbai Shan in Chinese); highest peak on the Korean peninsula
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun's explicit citation of Baekdusan as a source region is a hallmark of the Dongui Bogam's project to document Korean-specific medicinal resources [鄕藥, hyangyak], distinguishing it from Chinese pharmacopoeias. This geographic note underscores the Korean identity of the text.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a synthesis prompt rather than a verbatim classical text passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field above reconstructs a plausible period-appropriate Korean/Classical Chinese composite entry consistent with the Dongui Bogam's Tangaek (湯液) chapters on root drugs, drawing on attested Dongui Bogam content for Yongdam. Key divergences from Chinese TCM usage worth flagging: (1) Heo Jun consistently adds Korean geographic sourcing notes — here Baekdusan — which is absent from Chinese sources like the Bencao Gangmu; (2) The Dongui Bogam emphasizes hyangyak (鄕藥, 'local/native medicines'), encouraging use of domestically sourced Gentiana rather than imported Chinese stock; (3) While the formula Longdan Xiegan Tang (龍膽瀉肝湯) is shared across Chinese and Korean traditions, Korean practitioners in the Joseon period sometimes modified dosages downward, reflecting a clinical tradition attentive to Korean constitutional types. The bitter-cold classification and organ-system targeting are fully consistent with shared Sino-Korean medical theory.

results.title_inferred: Cheongpi (Green Tangerine Peel): Liver Qi Regulation and Pain Relief
results.original_text: 청피 (靑皮) — 푸른 귤껍질. 간기(肝氣)를 소통시키고 적취(積聚)를 흩으며 통증을 다스린다. 성질은 온(溫)하고 맛은 고(苦)·신(辛)하며 독이 없다. 간경(肝經)과 담경(膽經)에 귀경(歸經)한다.
results.translation:
Cheongpi (靑皮) — Green Tangerine Peel. [Source: Citrus reticulata Blanco, immature fruit peel; also written 靑橘皮.]

Nature and Flavor: The nature [性] of Cheongpi is warm [溫]; the flavor [味] is bitter [苦] and acrid [辛]; it is non-toxic [無毒].

Channel Tropism [歸經]: Enters the Liver Channel [肝經] and Gallbladder Channel [膽經]. Some classical sources additionally include the Stomach Channel [胃經] and Triple Burner [三焦].

Therapeutic Properties (per Dongui Bogam and Korean medicine theory):
1. Dredges and soothes Liver Qi [疏肝理氣]: Cheongpi is acrid and dispersing in character, penetrating deeply into the Liver and Gallbladder. It is the primary herb for breaking up constrained Liver Qi [肝氣鬱結], relieving hypochondriac distension and pain, and treating emotional depression linked to Liver Qi stagnation.
2. Breaks Up Accumulations and Disperses Masses [破氣散積]: Owing to its stronger breaking [破] action compared to Jinpi [陳皮, mature tangerine peel], Cheongpi is specifically employed for focal accumulations [積聚], masses [癥瘕], and food stagnation [食積] that have congealed into firm lumps in the abdomen or flanks.
3. Relieves Pain [止痛]: Indicated for flank pain, hypochondriac pain, epigastric pain, hernia pain [疝氣痛], and breast distension with pain — all presentations involving Qi constraint and stagnation.
4. Descends Counterflow Qi [降逆氣]: Assists in directing rebellious Qi downward, useful in nausea, belching, and fullness of the middle jiao.

Key Formulas from the Dongui Bogam:
• Cheongpi Tang (靑皮湯): Cheongpi combined with Sihо [柴胡, Bupleurum], Baekjak [白芍, white peony root], and Gamscho [甘草, licorice] — used for Liver Qi stagnation with flank pain and emotional constraint.
• Mokgwahwan (木瓜丸) / variant decoctions: Cheongpi paired with Mokgwa [木瓜, Chaenomeles] and Ojuyeong [吳茱萸, Evodia] for cold-type hernia pain [寒疝].
• Sagansan (瀉肝散): Cheongpi with Yongdam [龍膽, Gentiana] and Taeksaji [澤瀉, Alisma] to clear excess Liver fire combined with Qi stagnation.
• Yugi-eum (流氣飮): A broader formula incorporating Cheongpi to regulate the flow of Qi through multiple channels, used in Dongui Bogam's internal medicine section [內景篇] for abdominal masses.

Preparation Methods:
• Decoction [煎湯]: Typical dosage 3–9 g, decocted in water.
• Stir-frying [炒]: Dry-fried or bran-fried [麩炒] to moderate its harsh dispersing nature and protect the Stomach.
• Vinegar-processing [醋製]: Soaking or stir-frying with vinegar [醋炒靑皮] greatly enhances its ability to enter the Liver and break up blood stasis combined with Qi stagnation; this vinegar-processing method is particularly emphasized in the Dongui Bogam and Korean practice.
• Powdering [末]: Ground into fine powder and taken with warm water or ginger decoction for acute pain episodes.

Contraindications: Contraindicated in Qi deficiency [氣虛] patients, in cases of profuse sweating due to exterior deficiency, and in pregnant women. Prolonged use may damage Qi.

Korean-Specific Notes and Divergences from Chinese TCM:
- Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam places particular emphasis on Cheongpi's role in emotional disorders tied to Liver constraint [肝鬱], reflecting the Korean medical tradition's strong attention to the relationship between emotional life [心身] and Liver function — an emphasis somewhat more pronounced than in contemporaneous Ming Chinese texts.
- Korean practice frequently pairs Cheongpi with Hyanghwaja [香附子, Cyperus rotundus] for emotional-type Qi stagnation in women, a pairing that appears consistently in Korean formularies and is highlighted in the Dongui Bogam as especially suited to conditions of 'seven emotions injury' [七情傷].
- The Dongui Bogam distinguishes carefully between Cheongpi (immature, green peel — for Liver, breaks Qi forcefully) and Jinpi [陳皮] (mature, aged peel — for Spleen-Lung, regulates Qi more gently), codifying a distinction that Korean medicine treats as clinically fundamental.
- Some Korean regional preparations used Jeju Island-grown citrus [濟州橘皮], noted in Korean sources as having a distinct aromatic quality; Heo Jun acknowledges domestic Korean sources of citrus peel as viable substitutes for imported Chinese stock.

entities_mentioned.name: Cheongpi 청피 (靑皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco — immature (unripe, green) fruit peel; dried pericarp of unripe mandarin/tangerine
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Jinpi (陳皮), which is the mature, aged peel of the same fruit. The immature peel contains higher concentrations of synephrine and hesperidin, consistent with its stronger Qi-breaking action in Korean medicine theory.

entities_mentioned.name: Jinpi 진피 (陳皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco — mature, dried tangerine peel (aged)
entities_mentioned.notes: Contrasted with Cheongpi throughout the Dongui Bogam; milder action, directed more at Spleen and Lung rather than Liver.

entities_mentioned.name: Siho 시호 (柴胡)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum chinense DC. or Bupleurum scorzonerifolium Willd.
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently combined with Cheongpi in formulas targeting Liver Qi stagnation and alternating fever-chills.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyanghwaja 향부자 (香附子)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyperus rotundus L. — nutgrass rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Key pairing herb with Cheongpi in Korean medicine for emotional/Liver Qi disorders, especially in women.

entities_mentioned.name: Yongdamsagansan 용담사간산 (龍膽瀉肝散/湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula; not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Formula appearing in Dongui Bogam; uses Cheongpi alongside Gentiana (Yongdam) to address excess Liver fire with Qi constraint.

entities_mentioned.name: Mokgwa 모과 (木瓜)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chaenomeles sinensis (Thouin) Koehne — Chinese quince fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with Cheongpi in hernia and cold-Qi pain formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Ojuyeong 오수유 (吳茱萸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tetradium ruticarpum (A.Juss.) T.G.Hartley (syn. Evodia rutaecarpa)
entities_mentioned.notes: Warming herb paired with Cheongpi for cold-type hernia pain [寒疝痛].

entities_mentioned.name: Jeju citrus peel 제주귤피 (濟州橘皮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco cultivars grown on Jeju Island (Tamna), Korea
entities_mentioned.notes: Uniquely Korean material; Heo Jun references domestically cultivated Korean citrus as a valid source for both Cheongpi and Jinpi, distinguishing Korean medical practice from reliance on Chinese imports. Jeju Island citrus cultivation dates to the Goryeo period.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment is a brief label/stub ('Plant: Cheongpi 청피 — Green tangerine peel — liver qi, pain'); a full Dongui Bogam entry has been reconstructed from the canonical Dongui Bogam text (內景篇, 湯液篇 — 木部/果部) and standard Korean medical scholarship. Where specific Dongui Bogam wording is reconstructed rather than directly quoted, this reflects faithful synthesis from the received text tradition.
2. The vinegar-processing (醋炒) method is a particularly significant preparation detail emphasized in Korean practice and in the Dongui Bogam relative to some Chinese sources, which is worth highlighting for pharmacohistorical purposes.
3. The distinction between Cheongpi (immature) and Jinpi (mature) peel is codified with unusual precision in the Dongui Bogam compared to earlier Chinese materia medica, reflecting Heo Jun's systematizing approach.
4. 'Accumulations and gatherings' [積聚] is a classical pathological category in East Asian medicine referring to palpable or fixed abdominal masses (積, ji) and more diffuse, mobile accumulations (聚, chui); both are addressed by Cheongpi's Qi-breaking action.
5. The Jeju Island citrus reference reflects the Dongui Bogam's broader nationalistic-medical project of identifying Korean domestic materia medica as therapeutically equivalent to Chinese imports — a hallmark of Heo Jun's editorial philosophy.

results.title_inferred: Danggwi (당귀) — Korean Angelica as Blood Tonic in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 당귀 (當歸) — 혈을 보하고 조화롭게 하며, 어혈을 풀고 통증을 멎게 한다. 성질은 따뜻하고[溫] 맛은 달고 매우며[甘辛] 독이 없다. 혈허(血虛), 복통, 월경불순, 산후 어혈, 타박상, 변비에 쓴다. 뿌리를 가을에 채취하여 그늘에서 말린다. 머리 부분[歸頭]은 지혈에, 몸통[歸身]은 보혈에, 꼬리[歸尾]는 어혈 제거에 쓴다.
results.translation: Danggwi (當歸; 'Return of the Proper') — This herb supplements and harmonizes the blood [補血 and調血], disperses blood stasis [破瘀血], and arrests pain. Its nature is warm [溫], its flavor sweet and acrid [甘辛], and it is non-toxic. It is used for blood deficiency [血虛], abdominal pain, irregular menstruation, postpartum blood stasis, traumatic injury, and constipation. The root is harvested in autumn and dried in the shade. The head of the root [歸頭, Gwi-du] is used specifically to stop bleeding; the body of the root [歸身, Gwi-sin] to nourish and supplement the blood; and the tail of the root [歸尾, Gwi-mi] to break up and dispel blood stasis. In the Dongui Bogam, Heo Jun distinguishes the Korean species (Angelica gigas Nakai) as the superior domestic variety [土當歸], noting it is larger, more resinous, and considered more potent than the Chinese import (Angelica sinensis), and recommends its preferential use wherever Korean-sourced materials are available. Key formulas in which Danggwi appears include: Samuljeon (四物煎, Four-Substance Decoction) for blood deficiency and menstrual irregularity; Ssanghwa-tang (雙和湯, Double Harmony Decoction) uniquely emphasized in Korean practice for recovery from illness and fatigue; Danggwi Su-san (當歸鬚散) for postpartum pain; and궁귀탕 (芎歸湯, Cnidium-Angelica Decoction) for pregnancy disorders. Preparation: decoct the dried root slices in water; alternatively, steep in rice wine [酒] to enhance its blood-moving properties.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggwi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean Danggwi; distinct from Chinese Angelica sinensis Diels)
