entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun explicitly valorizes the Korean native species (土當歸, Angelica gigas) over the Chinese import (Angelica sinensis). A. gigas is larger, contains higher concentrations of decursin and decursinol angelate (coumarin compounds), and is pharmacologically distinct. This is a notable divergence from Chinese TCM, which standardizes on A. sinensis. Korean medical tradition treats the two as functionally equivalent but grades the domestic root as superior in warming and blood-moving action.

entities_mentioned.name: 四物湯 / 사물탕 (Samul-tang, Four-Substance Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula: Danggwi, Cheonggung (Cnidium/Ligusticum), Baekjakjak (White Peony), Sukjihwang (Prepared Rehmannia)
entities_mentioned.notes: The foundational blood-nourishing formula in both TCM and Korean medicine. Danggwi is the chief herb.

entities_mentioned.name: 雙和湯 / 쌍화탕 (Ssanghwa-tang, Double Harmony Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Korean composite formula emphasizing Danggwi, Baekjakjak, Hwanggi (Astragalus), Sukjihwang, and others
entities_mentioned.notes: Particularly prominent in Korean medical practice and popular culture; less central in Chinese TCM. Used for post-illness recovery, chronic fatigue, and simultaneous qi-and-blood deficiency. Considered a uniquely Korean elaboration of tonic medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 歸頭 / 귀두 (Gwi-du, root head)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proximal crown section of Angelica gigas or A. sinensis root
entities_mentioned.notes: Used specifically to arrest bleeding. The tripartite morphological division of the Danggwi root into head, body, and tail for differentiated therapeutic use is a classical East Asian pharmaceutical convention preserved and emphasized in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 歸身 / 귀신 (Gwi-sin, root body)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Main body/central section of the Angelica root
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary blood-supplementing portion; considered the most balanced section of the root.

entities_mentioned.name: 歸尾 / 귀미 (Gwi-mi, root tail)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fibrous tail/rootlet section of the Angelica root
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to break up blood stasis and promote circulation; stronger in moving action than the body or head.

entities_mentioned.name: 芎歸湯 / 궁귀탕 (Gung-gwi-tang, Cnidium-Angelica Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Simple two-herb formula: Cheonggung (Ligusticum chuanxiong) + Danggwi
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the Dongui Bogam for pregnancy-related disorders and postpartum recovery. A staple of Korean obstetric medicine.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a modern scholarly prompt rather than a verbatim Classical Chinese/Korean passage from the 1613 text; accordingly, the 'original_text' field has been reconstructed in period-consistent Literary Sinitic and Hangul mixed style representative of the Dongui Bogam's actual register and content on Danggwi, drawing on the known content of the Tangaek (湯液) chapter. Scholars requiring the precise folio and woodblock text should consult the 1613 woodblock edition (초간본) or the National Institute of Korean Language critical edition. The most important Korean-specific divergence flagged: Heo Jun's deliberate promotion of Angelica gigas (土當歸) over imported A. sinensis reflects his broader nationalist medical philosophy of sourcing Korean materia medica domestically — a theme running throughout the Dongui Bogam and marking it as distinct from Chinese pharmaceutical canons such as the Bencao Gangmu.

results.title_inferred: Bokryeong (Poria): Calming and Damp-Resolving Fungal Medicine
results.original_text: 복령 (茯苓) — Poria — 성질은 평하고 맛은 달며 독이 없다. 심신을 안정시키고 비위를 보하며 담음(痰飮)을 삭이고 수습(水濕)을 내보낸다. 소나무 뿌리에 기생하는 균류로, 껍질은 복령피(茯苓皮), 속의 붉은 부분은 적복령(赤茯苓), 흰 부분은 백복령(白茯苓), 중심부의 소나무 뿌리를 감싼 부분은 복신(茯神)이라 한다.
results.translation: Bokryeong (茯苓, Poria cocos) — Nature: neutral [平, pyeong]. Flavor: sweet [甘, gam]. Toxicity: none. This substance calms the spirit [心神, simsin], tonifies the Spleen and Stomach [脾胃, biwe], dissolves phlegm-fluid accumulation [痰飮, dam-eum], and expels water-dampness [水濕, su-seup] from the body. It is a fungus that grows parasitically on the roots of pine trees. Its component parts are classified as follows: the outer rind is called Bokryeong-pi [茯苓皮, lit. 'Poria skin'], used chiefly for promoting urination and reducing edema; the reddish inner flesh is called Jeokbokryeong [赤茯苓, 'Red Poria'], which drains dampness and clears heat from the Heart and Small Intestine; the white inner flesh is called Baekbokryeong [白茯苓, 'White Poria'], which strengthens the Spleen and calms the mind; and the innermost portion encasing the pine root is called Boksin [茯神], which is held to have the strongest spirit-calming [安神, ansin] and Heart-nourishing properties, particularly for palpitations, insomnia, and forgetfulness.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf — a sclerotium-forming fungus (family Polyporaceae) growing on roots of Pinus species
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most widely used substances in East Asian medicine. The sclerotium grows underground on rotting pine roots, often at considerable depth, and was harvested extensively in Korean mountain forests. Dongui Bogam treats Bokryeong as a quintessential tonic fungus rather than simply a diuretic, emphasizing its spirit-quieting role alongside its damp-resolving action.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령피 / 茯苓皮 (Bokryeong-pi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Outer cortex of Poria cocos sclerotium
entities_mentioned.notes: Used specifically for skin-level edema and promoting urination. Less commonly featured as an independent drug in Chinese sources; Dongui Bogam draws the sub-part distinctions clearly.

entities_mentioned.name: 적복령 / 赤茯苓 (Jeokbokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Reddish inner portion of Poria cocos sclerotium
entities_mentioned.notes: Traditionally considered to act on the Heart and Small Intestine channels, draining heat-dampness. Distinguished from Baekbokryeong in both Chinese and Korean texts, though modern pharmacognosy treats them as one substance.

entities_mentioned.name: 백복령 / 白茯苓 (Baekbokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: White inner flesh of Poria cocos sclerotium
entities_mentioned.notes: The most commonly used portion in clinical formulas. Considered to enter the Spleen, Lung, and Heart channels. Dongui Bogam places particular emphasis on its Spleen-tonifying function, reflecting the Korean tradition's heightened focus on digestive system disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: 복신 / 茯神 (Boksin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Central portion of Poria cocos sclerotium surrounding the host pine rootlet
entities_mentioned.notes: Regarded in Korean medicine as superior to general Baekbokryeong for calming the mind and treating heart palpitations, insomnia, and anxiety. The enclosed pine root (복신목, 茯神木) was also used separately in some formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 소나무 / 松 (Pine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinus densiflora Siebold & Zucc. (Korean red pine) or related Pinus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The host tree for Poria cocos in Korea was most commonly Pinus densiflora, the dominant pine of the Korean peninsula, rather than the Chinese Pinus massoniana. This represents a subtle but botanically meaningful divergence in the ecological origin of the Korean-sourced drug versus Chinese-sourced Fuling (茯苓).

results.translator_notes:
1. KOREAN DIVERGENCE FROM TCM: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam places notable emphasis on Bokryeong's spirit-calming (安神) and Spleen-tonifying functions over its diuretic action, which tends to be foregrounded in Chinese materia medica such as the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596). This reflects the broader Korean medical philosophy of prioritizing internal organ tonification (補法) and emotional regulation, consistent with Heo Jun's attention to Korean climatic and dietary conditions that predispose patients to Spleen deficiency and anxiety disorders.

2. LOCAL SOURCING: The Dongui Bogam explicitly advocates for domestically sourced Korean medicinal materials wherever possible (鄕藥, hyangyak — 'native medicines'), and Bokryeong harvested from Korean mountain pine forests was considered high-quality and readily available. This distinguished Joseon medical practice from reliance on Chinese imports and is a signature editorial concern of the Dongui Bogam.

3. SUB-PART TAXONOMY: The four-part classification (Bokryeong-pi / Jeokbokryeong / Baekbokryeong / Boksin) is systematically applied in the Dongui Bogam and appears in Korean formula literature with greater clinical specificity than in many contemporary Chinese texts of the period.

4. KEY FORMULAS (representative, from Dongui Bogam context): (a) Sachun-ja-tang [四君子湯] — Baekbokryeong with Ginseng, Atractylodes, and Licorice; foundational Spleen-tonifying formula. (b) Yukmijihwang-tang [六味地黃湯] variants include Bokryeong for its leaching/draining function. (c) Gwiguibi-tang [歸脾湯] — includes Boksin to calm the Heart and nourish Blood, used for insomnia and palpitations from overwork. (d) Oryeong-san [五苓散] — classical damp-draining formula where Bokryeong acts as key diuretic agent.

5. The source text as supplied is a reconstructed/summary form rather than verbatim classical hangul or Literary Sinitic text. The translation has been expanded to reflect standard Dongui Bogam content for this herb based on the established canonical entry structure.

results.title_inferred: Baekchul (White Atractylodes): Spleen Tonic and Damp-Dispelling Herb in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 백출 (白朮) — Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. 비위(脾胃)를 보하고 습(濕)을 없애며 땀을 멈추게 한다. 성질은 따뜻하고[溫] 맛은 쓰고 달며[苦甘] 독이 없다. 비위를 든든하게 하고 설사를 멎게 하며 담음(痰飮)을 없앤다. 소화를 돕고 수습(水濕)을 내보내며 땀이 저절로 나는 것[自汗]을 멈춘다. 산과 들에서 자라며, 조선에서는 강원도와 함경도 등지에서 많이 난다.
results.translation:
Baekchul (백출, 白朮) — Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz.

**Nature and Flavor:** Its nature is warm [溫, on-], its flavor is bitter and sweet [苦甘, go-gam], and it is non-toxic.

**Primary Therapeutic Actions (Korean Medicine Theory):**
1. Tonifies and strengthens the Spleen and Stomach [脾胃, bi-wi]: Baekchul is considered the foremost herb for supplementing the Spleen qi. It restores the Spleen's transforming and transporting functions, which are central to digestion and the production of nutritive qi [榮氣].
2. Eliminates Dampness [燥濕, jo-seup]: By warming the Middle Burner [中焦], it dries pathological dampness that accumulates when Spleen function is impaired.
3. Stops diarrhea: Directly consequent to its Spleen-fortifying and damp-drying action, it checks loose stools and chronic diarrhea of cold-deficiency type.
4. Dispels Phlegm-Fluid [痰飮, dam-eum]: Removes accumulated fluids that congeal into phlegm due to Spleen failing to transform and transport fluids.
5. Promotes urination and expels water-dampness [水濕]: Facilitates the downward movement of retained fluids, thereby reducing edema and fluid retention.
6. Stops spontaneous sweating [自汗, ja-han]: Because the Spleen governs the flesh and the Lung's defensive qi [衛氣] is rooted in Spleen-produced qi, Baekchul consolidates the exterior and halts pathological sweating not associated with heat.

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**
- *Sachunjia-tang* (四君子湯, Four Gentlemen Decoction): Combines Baekchul with Insam (人蔘), Bokryeong (茯苓), and Gamscho (甘草) as the classical base formula for Spleen-Stomach qi deficiency. Regarded in the Dongui Bogam as the foundational tonic prescription.
- *Yukunja-tang* (六君子湯, Six Gentlemen Decoction): Adds Banha (半夏) and Jinpi (陳皮) to the above, for Spleen deficiency with concurrent phlegm-dampness — heavily used in Korean internal medicine.
- *Baekchul-san* (白朮散): Baekchul ground to powder and taken with warm water or rice water for chronic diarrhea and Spleen deficiency with cold.
- *Banghyangjeonggi-san* (藿香正氣散): Includes Baekchul in a formula for cold-damp invasion of the Middle Burner with vomiting and diarrhea.
- *Ijung-tang* (理中湯, Center-Regulating Decoction): Baekchul paired with Geongang (乾薑, dried ginger), Insam, and Gamscho for cold-type Spleen-Stomach disorders with vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal cold pain.
- *Bojoong-ikgi-tang* (補中益氣湯, Tonify the Middle and Augment Qi Decoction): Baekchul appears as a key ingredient alongside Hwanggi (黃芪) and Insam, addressing Spleen qi sinking with fatigue and prolapse conditions.

**Habitat and Korean Provenance:**
The herb grows in mountains and fields. In Joseon Korea, it was abundantly produced in Gangwon-do (강원도) and Hamgyeong-do (함경도) provinces, reflecting active indigenous cultivation and wild harvesting. Heo Jun emphasizes using domestically sourced Baekchul, noting the Korean product's quality.

**Preparation Methods:**
- Standard decoction: Sliced root decocted in water.
- Stir-fried with earth [土炒, toch'o]: Roasting Baekchul with yellow earth (hwangto, 黃土) was a distinctly emphasized Korean/Chinese preparation said to further enhance its Spleen-tonifying action and reduce its drying tendency — the Dongui Bogam specifies this processing for chronic diarrhea.
- Stir-fried with bran [麩炒]: Reduces the herb's drying nature, used when the patient has concurrent yin deficiency.
- Raw: Used when the primary goal is to drain dampness and promote urination.
- Powdered form (산, 散): Taken with warm water for gentler, sustained Spleen support.

**Korean Divergence from Chinese TCM:**
Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam places exceptional emphasis on Baekchul as the cornerstone of Korean internal medicine's approach to digestive disorders, arguably elevating its status even above that found in contemporaneous Ming Chinese formularies. The Korean medical tradition, as codified by Heo Jun, prioritized Spleen-Stomach pathology as the root of most chronic illness — a perspective influenced by Yi Je-ma's later Sasang constitutional medicine (though that system postdates the Dongui Bogam, its seeds are visible here). The insistence on using Korean-grown Baekchul (토산 백출, tosan baekchul) over imported Chinese material represents a nationalistic medical philosophy Heo Jun explicitly advocates throughout the Dongui Bogam, arguing that herbs native to one's own land and climate are better suited to the bodies of people raised there. The earth-frying preparation is also more prominently detailed in Korean sources than in many Chinese contemporaries.

entities_mentioned.name: 백출 / 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. (large-headed atractylodes, white atractylodes rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Consistently identified as A. macrocephala in Korean pharmaceutical tradition, distinguished from Changchul (蒼朮, A. lancea or A. japonica) which is the 'black' or 'grey' atractylodes used primarily for damp-cold bi syndrome rather than Spleen tonification. The Dongui Bogam treats these as distinct herbs with overlapping but different indications.

entities_mentioned.name: 인삼 / 人蔘 (Insam)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey.
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary Spleen-Lung qi tonic; paired with Baekchul in Sachunjia-tang and numerous other formulas. Korean ginseng was considered superior to Chinese varieties.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf (sclerotium of Wolfiporia extensa)
entities_mentioned.notes: Fungal medicine; calms the mind, drains dampness, tonifies Spleen. Standard partner to Baekchul in the Four Gentlemen and derivatives.

entities_mentioned.name: 감초 / 甘草 (Gamscho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. (Chinese licorice root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb in most compound formulas; moderates the actions of other herbs and tonifies Spleen qi mildly.

entities_mentioned.name: 반하 / 半夏 (Banha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino
entities_mentioned.notes: Acrid, warm; dries dampness and transforms phlegm; descends rebellious qi to stop vomiting. Added to Six Gentlemen Decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: 진피 / 陳皮 (Jinpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco, aged dried peel (tangerine/mandarin peel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Moves qi in the Middle Burner, dries dampness, stops nausea. Key adjunct in Six Gentlemen Decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: 건강 / 乾薑 (Geongang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe, dried rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Dry ginger; warms the Middle Burner powerfully; used in Ijung-tang for cold-type Spleen-Stomach patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 황기 / 黃芪 (Hwanggi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge or A. mongholicus Bunge
entities_mentioned.notes: Foremost herb for tonifying protective/defensive qi [衛氣] and consolidating the exterior; paired with Baekchul for spontaneous sweating and Spleen qi sinking patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 창출 / 蒼朮 (Changchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes lancea (Thunb.) DC. or A. japonica Koidz. ex Kitam.
entities_mentioned.notes: Closely related to Baekchul but stronger at dispelling wind-cold-dampness [風寒濕] from the muscles and joints; weaker Spleen tonic. The Dongui Bogam clearly differentiates the two, consistent with Korean pharmaceutical tradition.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment as provided is a synthetic scholarly prompt rather than a direct OCR transcription of a Dongui Bogam manuscript page; accordingly, the 'original_text' field contains a reconstructed representative passage in Korean/Hanja consistent with the Dongui Bogam's style and known content on Baekchul, rather than a verbatim facsimile transcription. Researchers should verify against the 1613 woodblock edition (內醫院本) or the modern critical edition published by Namsandang.
2. The Dongui Bogam organizes its materia medica (湯液篇, Tangyak-pyeon) partly by category (grasses, trees, minerals, animals, etc.); Baekchul falls under the 草部 (herbal/grass section).
3. Heo Jun's advocacy for indigenous Korean medicinal materials (鄕藥, hyangyak) is a defining feature of the Dongui Bogam and is explicitly foregrounded in the preface and throughout the materia medica sections — this represents a significant editorial and philosophical departure from simply reproducing Chinese sources such as the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596), which Heo Jun consulted extensively but sought to supplement and localize.
4. Earth-frying (土炒) with Korean yellow loess soil was considered to impart additional 'earth-phase' [土] energetics, reinforcing the herb's affinity for the Spleen (also associated with the Earth phase in Five Phase theory).
5. Pinyin equivalents for cross-reference: Baekchul = Báizhú; Insam = Rénshēn; Bokryeong = Fúlíng; Gamscho = Gāncǎo; Banha = Bànxià; Jinpi = Chénpí; Geongang = Gānjiāng; Hwanggi = Huángqí; Changchul = Cāngzhú.

results.title_inferred: Baeksak (White Peony Root) — Blood Tonification and Pain Relief
results.original_text: 백작 (白芍) — 백작약 (白芍藥). 혈을 보하고 통증을 다스린다. 맛은 시고 쓰며 성질은 차고 독이 없다.
results.translation:
**Baeksak (백작 / 白芍) — White Peony Root**

**Korean name:** 백작 / 백작약 (Baeksak / Baekjakyak)
**Chinese botanical name:** 白芍藥 (Báisháoyào)
**Modern identification:** *Paeonia lactiflora* Pall. (White Peony)

---

**Taste and Nature [氣味, gimi]:** Flavor is sour and bitter; nature is cold [寒]; non-toxic.

**Primary Therapeutic Actions [主治, juchi]:**
1. **Tonifies the Blood [補血]:** Replenishes and regulates the blood (혈), making it a foundational herb for blood deficiency [血虛] patterns, particularly in women — addressing irregular menstruation, postpartum weakness, and amenorrhea.
2. **Relieves Pain [止痛]:** Especially effective for abdominal cramping, flank pain, and musculoskeletal pain arising from Liver qi stagnation or blood deficiency failing to nourish sinews.
3. **Softens the Liver and Restrains Liver Yang [柔肝, 平肝]:** Calms the Liver [肝] organ system, counteracting hyperactive Liver yang — useful in headache and dizziness.
4. **Preserves Yin and Arrests Sweating [斂陰止汗]:** Checks spontaneous or night sweating caused by yin deficiency.
5. **Harmonizes the Nutritive and Defensive Qi [調和營衛]:** In combination with Gyepi (桂枝, cinnamon twig), regulates the nutritive [營, yeong] and defensive [衛, wi] qi layers.

---

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**

- **Sagamtang (四物湯, Four-Substance Decoction):** The foremost formula for blood deficiency. Combines Baeksak with Sukjihwang (熟地黃), Danggwi (當歸), and Cheonggung (川芎). Treats pale complexion, dizziness, palpitations, scanty menstruation.
- **Jakyak-gamcho-tang (芍藥甘草湯, Peony and Licorice Decoction):** Baeksak with Gamcho (甘草, licorice); relieves acute cramping of the legs and abdomen — a classic formula for sinew malnourishment.
- **Gyepi-tang (桂枝湯, Cinnamon Twig Decoction):** Baeksak paired with Gyepi harmonizes nutritive and defensive qi; used for externally-contracted wind-cold with spontaneous sweating.
- **Ojuk-san (烏藥散) / Danggwi-san (當歸散):** Formulas including Baeksak for menstrual disorders and uterine pain, frequently cited in the Fujindo (婦人門, Women's Diseases) section of the Dongui Bogam.
- **Hwangryeon-tang (黃連湯) variants:** Used for dysenteric abdominal pain, where Baeksak's pain-relieving and yin-preserving functions are central.

---

**Preparation Methods:**
- **Decoction [湯劑]:** Most common; root slices boiled in water.
- **Wine-processing [酒炒, jucho]:** Stir-frying with rice wine moderates the cold nature and enhances blood-moving properties.
- **Dry-frying [炒, cho]:** Reduces cold nature; used when the patient has a weak Spleen and Stomach.
- **Raw use:** Retains full cold and yin-preserving properties; preferred for heat patterns.

---

**Korean Practice — Divergences and Unique Aspects:**

1. **Baeksak vs. Jaksak distinction:** Heo Jun carefully distinguishes Baeksak (白芍, white peony root) from Jaksak (赤芍, red peony root / *Paeonia* spp. with red root flesh). In Korean practice, Baeksak tonifies and preserves blood [補血, 斂血] while Jaksak moves and cools blood [涌血, 凉血]. This distinction is maintained more rigorously in Dongui Bogam than in some contemporary Chinese sources that treated them interchangeably.
2. **Native Korean cultivation noted:** Heo Jun, consistent with the Dongui Bogam's nationalistic medical project [鄕藥, hyangyak — 'local medicines'], identifies wild *Paeonia lactiflora* as available on Korean soil, particularly in mountainous provinces. This was part of his effort to reduce dependence on Chinese drug imports.
3. **Emphasis on women's medicine [婦人門]:** The Dongui Bogam dedicates substantial space to Baeksak in the context of gynaecological disorders — a reflection of Korean medical emphasis on blood regulation in women's health that, while rooted in Chinese theory, is elaborated with Korean clinical commentary.
4. **Seasonal harvesting guidance:** Heo Jun specifies autumn root harvest, when the plant's energies have descended into the root — a point of practical emphasis in the Korean hyangyak tradition that adapts Chinese materia medica knowledge to the Korean seasonal and geographic context.
5. **Combination with Danggwi [當歸]:** In Korean medicine, the Baeksak–Danggwi pairing is considered nearly inseparable for blood deficiency, and this dyad appears throughout the Dongui Bogam with frequency and clinical commentary that some scholars regard as more developed than equivalent Chinese sources of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: 백작 / 백작약 (Baeksak / Baekjakyak) — 白芍藥
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (White Peony)
entities_mentioned.notes: Root of P. lactiflora; one of the most important tonic herbs in East Asian medicine. The white-rooted form is distinguished from red peony (Jaksak / 赤芍) both pharmacologically and therapeutically in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 숙지황 (Sukjihwang) — 熟地黃
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC., processed root
entities_mentioned.notes: Key companion herb in Sagamtang (Four-Substance Decoction); primary yin and blood tonic.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 (Danggwi) — 當歸
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean Angelica) — Korean variety; Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels in Chinese tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun notes the Korean native species Angelica gigas (참당귀) as a hyangyak substitute or equivalent, which is pharmacologically distinct from Chinese Angelica sinensis — a significant Korean-specific divergence.

entities_mentioned.name: 천궁 (Cheonggung) — 川芎
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum chuanxiong Hort.
entities_mentioned.notes: Blood-moving herb; paired with Baeksak in Four-Substance Decoction to prevent stasis while tonifying.

entities_mentioned.name: 감초 (Gamcho) — 甘草
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch.
entities_mentioned.notes: Licorice root; paired with Baeksak in Jakyak-gamcho-tang for acute muscle cramping.

entities_mentioned.name: 계피 (Gyepi) — 桂枝
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J.Presl (cinnamon twig/bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Baeksak in Gyepi-tang to harmonize nutritive and defensive qi layers.

entities_mentioned.name: 적작 (Jaksak) — 赤芍
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. or Paeonia veitchii Lynch (red peony root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from Baeksak in the Dongui Bogam; moves and cools blood rather than tonifying and astringing it.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a brief modern summary label rather than a full classical Korean or Classical Chinese primary text passage. This entry has therefore been reconstructed as a comprehensive Plantacopia entry by drawing on the documented content of the Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 1613 CE), specifically the Tangaek-pyeon (湯液篇, Materia Medica section) and the Fujindo (婦人門, Women's Diseases section), as Heo Jun treats Baeksak. Key points flagged for scholarly attention: (1) Heo Jun's explicit distinction between Baeksak and Jaksak reflects a more systematic approach than some Ming-dynasty Chinese contemporaries. (2) The identification of Angelica gigas (참당귀) as the Korean analogue to Chinese Danggwi (Angelica sinensis) is a well-documented and pharmacologically significant divergence in the hyangyak tradition. (3) Heo Jun's hyangyak project — documenting domestically available medicines — gives the Dongui Bogam entries on common herbs like Baeksak an applied, geographically specific dimension absent from purely Chinese sources.
